Ayah

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَنفَالِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلْأَنفَالُ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا۟ ذَاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ ۖ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١)
They ask you concerning the anfaal. Say, ‘The anfaal belong to Allah and the Apostle.’ So be wary of Allah and settle your differences, and obey Allah and His Apostle, should you be faithful. (1)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas that on the day of the Battle of Badr, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) established rewards to encourage the Muslim warriors. For instance, he said: "Whoever captures such-and-such an enemy individual and brings him to me shall receive such a reward." This encouragement (in addition to the spirit of faith and Jihad burning within them) caused the young soldiers of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to hasten toward the objective in an honorable competition. However, the elderly and senior individuals remained stationed under the banners. When the Battle of Badr concluded, the youths rushed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to receive their honorable rewards, but the elders said to them: "We also have a share, for we were your support and source of encouragement; had things become difficult for you and had you retreated, you certainly would have returned to us." At this point, a verbal dispute arose between two men from the Ansar regarding the spoils of war. Consequently, the above verse was revealed, explicitly declaring the spoils to belong to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to deal with as He wished. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) then divided them equally among all the warriors and commanded that peace and reconciliation be established among the religious brothers.

Commentary

As we read in the circumstances of revelation, the above verse was revealed after the Battle of Badr and discusses war spoils, stating a broad Islamic ruling as a general law. Addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "They ask you about the Anfal" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ).

"Say: The Anfal belong to Allah and the Messenger" (قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَ الرَّسُولِ).

Therefore, "Fear Allah and set things right among yourselves, and reconcile the brothers who have quarreled with one another" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ).

"And obey Allah and His Messenger, if you are believers" (وَ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

This means that faith is not merely by words; rather, the manifestation of faith is unconditional obedience to the commands of Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in all matters of life. Not only regarding war spoils, but in everything, you must be attentive to their commands and submit to their orders.

What is Anfal?

"Anfal" is originally derived from the root "Nafl" (rhyming with Naf') meaning "excess" or "addition." The reason voluntary prayers are called "Nafilah" is that they are additions to the obligatory ones. Similarly, if a "grandchild" is called Nafilah, it is because they are an addition to the children. A "Nawfal" is a person who gives many gifts.

If war spoils are also called Anfal, it is either because they are a series of additional properties that remain without an owner and fall into the hands of the warriors while no specific owner exists for them, or because the warriors fight for victory over the enemy, not for booty; thus, the booty is an additional matter that falls into their hands.

Three important points must be noted here

1- The concept of the verse is a general and universal ruling

1- Although the above verse was revealed regarding war spoils, its concept is a general and universal ruling, encompassing all additional properties—that is, whatever has no private owner. For this reason, in the narrations reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), we see a broad concept expressed for Anfal. In authentic narrations from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

"It is what is taken from the Dar al-Harb (abode of war) without fighting, such as that from which its people have fled—which is called Fay’—and the inheritance of one who has no heir, and the estates of kings if they were not usurped, and marshes, the depths of valleys, and dead lands; for these belong to Allah and His Messenger, and after him to whoever stands in his place, to spend it wherever he wishes for his own interests and the interests of those dependent on him." [1]

Although all war spoils are not mentioned in the above hadith, in another hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

"The spoils of Badr belonged specifically to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and he divided them among the warriors as a favor from him." [2]

From what has been said, we conclude that the primary concept of Anfal includes not only war spoils but all properties that have no private owner, and all such properties belong to Allah, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and his successor. In other words, they belong to the Islamic government and are spent in the direction of the general interests of the Muslims.

However, regarding war spoils and movable property that fall into the hands of soldiers through combat, the law of Islam—as we will explain in this same Surah—is established such that four-fifths of it is given to them as encouragement and compensation for part of the warriors' hardships, and only one-fifth is taken as Khums to be spent on the purposes mentioned under verse 41. Thus, spoils are also included in the general concept of Anfal and are originally the property of the Islamic government, and granting four-fifths of it to the warriors is an act of gift and favor (take note).

[^1] and [^2]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 254.

2- Does this verse contradict verse 41?

2- It might be imagined that the above verse (assuming it includes war spoils) contradicts the forty-first verse of this same Surah, which says: "Only one-fifth of the spoils (their Khums) belongs to Allah, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and other uses," because the implication is that the remaining four-fifths belong to the warriors.

However, considering what was said above, it becomes clear that war spoils originally all belong to Allah and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and it is a type of gift and favor that they place four-fifths at the disposal of the warriors. In other words, the Islamic government spends four-fifths of its right from the movable spoils on the Mujahideen, and thus no contradiction remains.

From this, it also becomes clear that the verse of Khums—as some commentators have imagined—does not abrogate the verse of Anfal; rather, both remain in full force.

3- The conduct of Muslims regarding spoils and "Islah Dhat al-Bayn"

3- As we read in the circumstances of revelation, a dispute occurred among some Muslims regarding the war spoils. To cut off this dispute, its root—the issue of booty—was first addressed by placing it entirely at the disposal of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Then, he gave orders to others to reconcile among the Muslims and the individuals who had quarreled.

Fundamentally, "Islah Dhat al-Bayn" (reconciling between people), creating understanding, removing bitterness and enmities, and transforming them into sincerity and friendship is one of the most important Islamic programs.

"Dhat" means the essence, structure, and foundation of something, and "Bayn" means the state of connection and bond between two persons or two things. Therefore, "Islah Dhat al-Bayn" means repairing the foundation of relationships, strengthening and consolidating bonds, and eliminating the factors and causes of division and hypocrisy.

In Islamic teachings, such importance is given to this subject that it is introduced as one of the highest forms of worship. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), in his final testament while on his deathbed, said to his children:

"I heard your grandfather, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), saying: 'Reconciling the relationship between people is superior to all [voluntary] prayer and fasting.'" [1]

In the book al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said:

"A charity that Allah loves is reconciling between people when they become corrupted [in their relations] and bringing them close together when they have become distant." [2]

Also in the same book, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said to Mufaddal (one of the Imam's companions):

"If you see a dispute between two of our Shia (regarding financial matters), settle it using my wealth." [3]

For this reason, we read in another narration that Mufaddal one day saw two Shias quarreling over an inheritance. He invited them to his home, paid them the four hundred dirhams that were the subject of their dispute, and ended the matter. Then he said to them: "Know that this was not from my own wealth, but Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) commanded me that in such cases, I should use the Imam's capital to create peace and reconciliation among the companions." [4]

The reason for all this emphasis on this social issue becomes clear with a little reflection, because the greatness, ability, power, and honor of a nation are not possible except under the shadow of understanding and cooperation. If disputes and small differences are not rectified, the root of enmity and hostility gradually penetrates the hearts and turns a united nation into a scattered group—a group that is vulnerable, weak, and helpless in the face of every incident and every enemy. Even within such a population, many fundamental Islamic issues like prayer and fasting, or the very existence of the Quran, will be endangered.

For this reason, some stages of "Islah Dhat al-Bayn" are religiously obligatory, and it is even permissible to use the resources of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) to achieve it, while other stages that do not heavily impact the fate of Muslims are highly recommended (Mustahabb Mu'akkad).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha. [^2] and [^3]: Usul al-Kafi, Chapter on Islah Dhat al-Bayn, Hadith 1 and 2.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَإِذَا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ زَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٢)
The faithful are only those whose hearts tremble [with awe] when Allah is mentioned, and when His signs are recited to them, they increase their faith, and who put their trust in their Lord, (2)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣)
maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. (3)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٤)
It is they who are truly the faithful. They shall have ranks near their Lord, forgiveness and a noble provision. (4)

Commentary

Five Distinctive Qualities of the Believers

In the previous verse, in connection with the discussion that had arisen among some Muslims regarding the spoils of war, mention was made of piety, God-wariness, and faith. To complete this subject, the verses under discussion describe the qualities of "true and sincere believers" in short yet profound phrases.

In these verses, Allah refers to five categories of the prominent qualities of believers, three of which are spiritual, internal, and psychological, while two are practical and external. The first three consist of "a sense of responsibility," "the evolution of faith," and "trust in God (Tawakkul)"; the other two consist of "connection with Allah" and "connection and bond with Allah’s creation."

First, He says: True believers are only those who, whenever the name of Allah is mentioned, their hearts tremble due to a sense of responsibility before Him: (إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

"Wajal" (وجل) refers to that state of fear and apprehension that overcomes a person, originating from one of two things: sometimes it is due to perceiving one's responsibilities and the possibility of failing to perform the necessary duties before Allah; and sometimes it is due to perceiving the grandeur of His station and contemplating His infinite and awe-inspiring existence.

To explain: sometimes a person goes to visit a great figure who is truly worthy of the title of greatness in every respect. The visitor may become so influenced by that person's majestic status that they feel a kind of awe within their heart, to the point that they may stammer while speaking or even forget what they intended to say—even if that great person shows the utmost love and affection toward them and everyone else, and even if the visitor has committed no wrong. This type of fear is a reflection and reaction to the perception of greatness.

The Holy Quran says: لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلى‌ جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ: "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have surely seen it humbled and rent asunder from the fear of Allah" (Al-Hashr: 21). We also read: إِنَّما يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ: "Only those of His servants who possess knowledge [of His greatness] fear Allah" (Fatir: 28).

Thus, there is a constant link between awareness and fear. Therefore, it is a mistake to assume that the source of fear and awe is solely the failure to perform duties and responsibilities.

Then, He describes their second quality: they are constantly moving along the path of "evolution" and do not remain still for a moment: "And when His verses are recited to them, they increase them in faith" (وَ إِذا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُهُ زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً). Growth and evolution are characteristics of all living beings; a being devoid of growth and evolution is either dead or on the verge of death. True believers possess a living faith, whose sapling grows day by day through the irrigation of Allah’s verses, producing more flowers, blossoms, and fresh fruits. They do not stagnate like the "living dead," nor are they in a state of deadly stagnation and monotony. With every new day, their thoughts, faith, and attributes are renewed.

Their third prominent quality is that "they rely and put their trust only in their Lord" (وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ).

The horizon of their thought is so high that they refuse to rely on weak and helpless creatures, no matter how great they may appear. They take their water from the source and seek whatever they desire from the boundless ocean of the universe—from the Pure Essence of the Lord. Their spirits are great, their level of thinking is elevated, and their sole reliance is on Allah.

Let there be no misunderstanding: the concept of Tawakkul (trust in God), as some distorters have imagined, is not to ignore the world of causes, fold one's arms, and sit in a corner. Rather, its meaning is self-building, high-mindedness, lack of dependency on this or that, and deep-sightedness. Utilizing the world of causes in nature and life is the very essence of trust in Allah, for any effect these causes have is by the will of Allah and according to His decree.

After mentioning these three spiritual and psychological qualities of true believers, He states that in the light of their sense of responsibility, perception of the Lord's greatness, increasing faith, and the high-mindedness of trust, they possess two strong bonds in terms of action: a powerful bond with Allah and a powerful bond with Allah’s servants. "They are those who establish prayer (which is the manifestation of the relationship with Allah) and spend out of what We have provided them for the sake of Allah’s servants" (الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ).

The expression "establishing prayer" (instead of merely "saying prayer") indicates that they not only pray themselves but also act to ensure that this firm connection with the Lord remains established everywhere. The expression مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ ("from what We have provided them") is a broad term encompassing all material and spiritual assets. They do not withhold not only their wealth but also their knowledge, their intelligence and thought, their social status and influence, and all the blessings at their disposal from the servants of Allah.

In the final verse under discussion, He describes the high status and the abundant rewards of such true believers.

First, He says: "Those are the believers in truth" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا).

Then, He mentions three important rewards for them:

  1. "They have high ranks with their Lord" (لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ). Ranks whose measure and quantity have not been specified, and this very ambiguity points to their extraordinary nature. [1]
  2. Additionally, they will be "subject to His forgiveness and mercy" (وَ مَغْفِرَةٌ).
  3. "And a noble provision—meaning great, continuous, and eternal blessings that are untainted by defect or flaw and are beyond measure—awaits them" (وَ رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ).

Truly, we Muslims who claim to follow Islam—and sometimes consider ourselves so entitled that we ignorantly blame Islam and the Quran for our own backwardness—if we were to implement only the content of these few verses, which clarify the qualities of true believers, in our lives; if we were to cast away weakness, abjectness, and dependency on others under the shelter of faith and trust; if we were to step into a new stage of faith and awareness with every passing day; if we always felt a sense of responsibility toward what happens in our society in the light of faith; and if our relationship with God and His creation were so strong that we spent all our existential assets for the advancement of society—would our condition be as it is today?!

It is also necessary to mention that faith has stages and degrees. It is possible for it to be so weak in some stages that it does not manifest significant practical effects and may even coexist with many impurities. However, it is impossible for a firm, true, and solid faith to be devoid of practical, positive, and constructive aspects.

The fact that some have not considered "action" as part of faith is because they were only looking at the very lowest stage of faith.

[^1]: For further explanation regarding "rank" (darajah) and "ranks" (darajat), please refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 155.

Ayahs

كَمَآ أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَيْتِكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ لَكَـٰرِهُونَ (٥)
As your Lord brought you out from your home with a just cause, a part of the faithful were indeed reluctant. (5)
يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ فِى ٱلْحَقِّ بَعْدَ مَا تَبَيَّنَ كَأَنَّمَا يُسَاقُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَوْتِ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (٦)
They disputed with you concerning the truth after it had become clear, as if they were being driven towards death as they looked on. (6)

Commentary

In the first verse of this Surah, we read that some of the newly converted Muslims were somewhat dissatisfied with the manner in which the spoils of the Battle of Badr were distributed. In the verses under discussion, Allah tells them: It is not a new thing for something to be unpleasant to you while your benefit lies within it, just as the original Battle of Badr—the spoils of which are currently being debated—was unpleasant for some, yet you saw what brilliant results it ultimately brought for the Muslims.

Therefore, you must not evaluate Divine decrees with your limited vision; rather, you must submit to them and benefit from the final outcomes.

In the first verse, He says: "This dissatisfaction of some individuals regarding the method of distributing the spoils of Badr is just as when your Lord brought you out of your home and station in Medina by the truth, while a group of the believers were reluctant" (كَما أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَيْتِكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ إِنَّ فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لَكارِهُونَ).

The word "بالحق" (by the truth) indicates that this command to set out was carried out according to a Divine revelation and a heavenly decree, the result of which was the attainment of truth for the Islamic society.

"This superficial and impatient group, on the way to Badr, constantly disputed and argued with you regarding this command of truth, and even though they had realized the reality that this was the command of Allah, they still did not cease their objections" (يُجادِلُونَكَ فِي الْحَقِّ بَعْدَ ما تَبَيَّنَ). And such fear and terror had gripped their entire being that it was "as if they were being driven toward death while they were looking on" (كَأَنَّما يُساقُونَ إِلَى الْمَوْتِ وَ هُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ).

However, subsequent events showed how much they were plagued by error and groundless fear, and what brilliant victories this war brought for the Muslims. Having witnessed such a scene, why do they open their mouths in protest regarding the spoils after the Battle of Badr?

Furthermore, from the expression "فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ" (a group of the believers), it becomes clear that, firstly, this dispute and argument were not due to a spirit of hypocrisy or lack of faith, but rather due to weakness of faith and a lack of sufficient insight into Islamic matters.

Secondly, only a limited number thought this way, while the majority, who were true mujahid Muslims, were submissive to the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his orders.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ إِحْدَى ٱلطَّآئِفَتَيْنِ أَنَّهَا لَكُمْ وَتَوَدُّونَ أَنَّ غَيْرَ ذَاتِ ٱلشَّوْكَةِ تَكُونُ لَكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُحِقَّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعَ دَابِرَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧)
When Allah promised you [victory over] one of the two companies, [saying], ‘It is for you,’ you were eager that it should be the one that was unarmed. But Allah desires to confirm the truth with His words, and to root out the faithless, (7)
لِيُحِقَّ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٨)
so that He may confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse. (8)

The Battle of Badr: The First Armed Conflict Between Islam and Infidelity

Since the previous verses made a brief reference to the Battle of Badr, the Holy Qur'an now draws the discussion toward the Battle of Badr itself. In the verses under discussion and those to follow, it describes sensitive episodes of the battle, each containing a world of lessons, so that Muslims may forever remember these truths—which they experienced in the recent past—and draw inspiration from them throughout their lives.

To clarify the commentary of these and subsequent verses, we must first review a summary of the events of this Islamic struggle, which was the first armed encounter between Muslims and their persistent, bloodthirsty enemies, so that the nuances and allusions within these verses become fully clear.

According to what historians, traditionists, and commentators have recorded, the Battle of Badr began when Abu Sufyan, the leader of Mecca, was returning from Syria toward Medina at the head of a relatively significant trade caravan consisting of forty people with 50,000 Dinars worth of merchandise. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله instructed his companions to prepare for departure and hasten toward this large caravan, which carried a major portion of the enemy's capital. By confiscating this wealth, they aimed to deal a heavy blow to the enemy's economic power and, consequently, their military strength.

The Prophet and his companions had the right to launch such an attack because, firstly, with the migration of Muslims from Mecca to Medina, much of their property had fallen into the hands of the Meccans, causing them heavy losses, and they had the right to compensate for such damages. Furthermore, during the thirteen years of the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله and the Muslims' stay in Mecca, the people of Mecca had clearly shown they would spare no effort to harm the Muslims, even going as far as preparing to kill the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله himself. Such an enemy would not remain idle after the Prophet's migration to Medina and would surely mobilize their forces for a more decisive strike. Therefore, reason and logic dictated that the Muslims take a preemptive measure by confiscating the vast capital of their trade caravan to deal them a severe blow, while also strengthening their own economic and military foundation for future self-defense. This is a measure that has been and remains part of all military strategies in the world, past and present. Those who, without considering these aspects, try to portray the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله movement toward the caravan as a form of banditry are either uninformed individuals ignorant of the historical roots of Islamic issues, or biased individuals seeking to distort realities.

In any case, Abu Sufyan became aware of the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله decision through his friends in Medina. On the other hand, since this caravan had faced similar potential threats when traveling to Syria to fetch the goods, he quickly sent a messenger to Mecca to inform its people. The messenger entered Mecca having followed Abu Sufyan's advice: he had slit the nose of his camel and cut its ears so that blood flowed provocatively, tore his shirt from both sides, and sat backward on the camel to attract everyone's attention. He cried out: "O victorious people! Save your caravan! Save your caravan! Hasten and hurry, though I do not believe you will arrive in time, for Muhammad and those who have left your religion have rushed out of Medina to intercept the caravan!" At this time, a strange and terrifying dream seen by Atikah, the daughter of Abdul-Muttalib and the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله aunt, was spreading from mouth to mouth, increasing the people's agitation.

The dream was that three days prior, she saw someone shouting, "O people, hasten to your places of slaughter!" Then this herald went atop Mount Abu Qays and rolled a large boulder down. The boulder shattered, and a piece of it hit every house of the Quraysh, and a flood of blood flowed from the valley of Mecca.

When she woke up terrified and informed her brother Abbas, the people were plunged into fear. However, when the story of this dream reached Abu Jahl, he said: "This is a second prophet appearing among the children of Abdul-Muttalib! By the idols of Lat and Uzza, we shall wait three days; if no sign of its interpretation appears, we will sign a document among ourselves stating that the Banu Hashim are the most lying of the Arab tribes." But on the third day, the cry of Abu Sufyan's messenger shook all of Mecca.

Since many people of Mecca had a share in this caravan, they mobilized quickly. About 950 fighting men, including many of the elders and notables of Mecca, set out with 700 camels and 100 horses, with Abu Jahl in command of the army.

On the other hand, to protect himself from the Muslims, Abu Sufyan changed his route and marched rapidly toward Mecca.

The Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله, with 313 men—who constituted almost the entire fighting force of Islam at that time—had reached the vicinity of the land of Badr, between Mecca and Medina, when news of the Quraysh army's movement reached him.

At this point, he consulted with his companions: should they pursue Abu Sufyan's caravan and confiscate its goods, or prepare to confront the army? A group preferred confronting the enemy army, but another group was reluctant and preferred to pursue the caravan. Their reasoning was: "When we left Medina, we did not intend to confront the Meccan army and we lack the combat readiness to engage them, while they are coming toward us with definite foresight and sufficient preparation for war."

This hesitation and doubt in this group increased when it became clear that the enemy's numbers were nearly triple those of the Muslims, and their equipment was several times greater. Despite all this, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله favored the view of the first group and ordered preparation for an attack on the enemy army. When the two armies faced each other, the enemy could not believe that the Muslims had come to the field with such few numbers and equipment; rather, they thought a significant part of the Islamic army was hidden somewhere to launch a surprise attack at the right moment. Thus, they sent someone to investigate, but they soon realized the population was exactly what they saw.

On the other hand, as mentioned, a group of Muslims was overcome with fear and insisted that fighting this massive group, against whom there was no balance of power, was not advisable. But the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله heartened them with this divine promise, saying: "Allah has promised me that you will be victorious over one of the two groups: either the Quraysh caravan or their army, and Allah's promise is unfailing. By Allah, it is as if I see with my own eyes the places where Abu Jahl and a number of the Quraysh leaders will be killed." He then ordered the Muslims to camp by the well of Badr ("Badr" was originally the name of a man from the Juhaynah tribe who dug a well in that land; subsequently, that well and that land were named the land of Badr and the well of Badr).

In the midst of this, Abu Sufyan managed to lead the caravan out of the danger zone and hurried toward Mecca via a detour along the coast (the Red Sea). He sent a message to the army via a messenger: "Allah has saved your caravan. I think fighting Muhammad is unnecessary in these circumstances, for he has enemies who will settle accounts with him." However, the army commander, Abu Jahl, did not submit to this suggestion and swore by the great idols Lat and Uzza: "We will not only fight them but will pursue them into Medina or take them captive and bring them to Mecca so that the sound of this victory reaches all the Arab tribes."

Finally, the Quraysh army also entered the land of Badr and sent their slaves to the well to fetch water. The Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله companions captured them and brought them to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله for questioning. He asked them: "Who are you?" They said: "We are the slaves of the Quraysh." He asked: "How many is the army?" They said: "We have no information on this." He asked: "How many camels do they slaughter for food each day?" They said: "Nine to ten camels." He said: "Their population is between nine hundred and a thousand" (each camel feeds one hundred fighting men).

The environment was truly terrifying and awe-inspiring. The Quraysh army, which had entered the field with abundant military gear, sufficient strength and food, and even singing women and musicians to incite or entertain the troops, found themselves facing an opponent they could not believe would step onto the battlefield under such conditions.

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, seeing that his companions might not sleep peacefully at night due to fear and might face the enemy the next day with exhausted bodies and spirits, told them according to a divine promise: "Do not grieve; if your numbers are few, a great host of heavenly angels will hasten to your aid." He comforted them completely and made them certain of the final victory promised by Allah, such that they slept peacefully that night. Another problem the warriors feared was the condition of the Badr field, which was covered with soft sand in which feet would sink. That night, a welcome rain fell; they were able to perform ablution with its water, cleanse and refresh themselves, and the ground beneath their feet became firm and solid. Remarkably, this downpour was so intense on the enemy's side that it distressed them.

New information received by secret scouts who had approached the enemy camp at night from the Islamic army was quickly reflected among the Muslims: they reported that the Quraysh army, despite all its resources, was deeply afraid, as if Allah had cast an army of terror into their hearts. The next morning, the small army of Islam lined up against the enemy with a powerful spirit.

Previously, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله had offered them peace so that no excuse would remain, and he sent a representative among them saying: "I do not wish for you to be the first group we attack." Some Quraysh leaders were inclined to shake the hand extended for peace, but again Abu Jahl prevented it.

Finally, the fire of war was ignited. Hamzah, the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله uncle, and Ali عليه السلام, who was the youngest in the army, along with a group of other brave warriors of Islam, dealt severe blows to their opponents in the single combats that were the custom of that day and struck them down. The enemy's morale weakened further. Abu Jahl issued an order for a general attack, having previously ordered that those companions of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله who were from Medina be killed and the Meccan Migrants (Muhajirin) be captured and brought to Mecca for propaganda purposes. These were sensitive moments. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله had instructed the Muslims not to look too much at the mass of the crowd but to look only at their opponents, to grit their teeth, speak little, seek help from Allah, and never disobey the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله command under any circumstances, remaining hopeful of final victory. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله raised his hands toward the sky and said: "O Lord! If this group is destroyed, You will not be worshipped."

A wind blew strongly toward the Quraysh army, and the Muslims attacked them with the wind at their backs. Their steadfastness, perseverance, and bravery put the Quraysh in a tight spot. As a result, seventy of the enemy army, including Abu Jahl, were killed and rolled in dust and blood, and seventy were taken prisoner by the Muslims. The Muslims, however, had only a few casualties. Thus, the first armed struggle of the Muslims against their powerful enemy ended with an unexpected victory. [1]

Commentary

Now that we have a summary of the Battle of Badr, we return to the commentary of the Surah's verses. In the first verse under discussion, reference is made to Allah's general promise of victory during the Battle of Badr, saying: "Remember when Allah promised you that one of the two groups (the Quraysh trade caravan or their army) would be yours" (وَ إِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللَّهُ إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ أَنَّها لَكُمْ). But you, to avoid the troubles of war and the casualties and discomforts resulting from it, "wished that the caravan would be yours, not the Quraysh army" (وَ تَوَدُّونَ أَنَّ غَيْرَ ذاتِ الشَّوْكَةِ تَكُونُ لَكُمْ).

It is mentioned in narrations that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said to them: "إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ (one of the two groups) is for you: either the 'Ir or the Nafir," where 'Ir means the caravan and Nafir means the army. But as you observe in the verse, instead of "army," the expression "ذاتِ الشَّوْكَةِ" is used, and instead of "caravan," "غَيْرَ ذاتِ الشَّوْكَةِ" is used.

This expression contains a subtle point, for "shawkah," which means power and intensity, is originally derived from "shawk," meaning "thorn." Subsequently, it was applied to the spearheads of soldiers and thereafter to any kind of weapon. Since weapons are a sign of power and intensity, any kind of power and intensity is also called shawkah.

Therefore, "dhat shawkah" means armed soldiers, and "ghayr dhat shawkah" means an unarmed caravan, for even if there were armed men in it, they were certainly not significant.

That is, a group of you, out of a desire for ease or interest in material benefits, preferred to face the enemy's merchandise rather than armed soldiers, whereas the end of the war showed that their absolute best interest lay in crushing the enemy's military power so that the path for great future victories would be paved.

Therefore, it follows by saying: "Allah intends by this to establish the truth by His words, strengthen the religion of Islam, and cut off the roots of the disbelievers" (وَ يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ وَ يَقْطَعَ دابِرَ الْكافِرِينَ) [1]. Thus, this was a great lesson for all you Muslims to be far-sighted and future-oriented in various events, not short-sighted and thinking only of today—even if far-sightedness and considering the consequences involve many difficulties, and short-sightedness is accompanied by ease and fleeting material benefits. For the first victory is a deep-rooted and comprehensive victory, while the second is a superficial and temporary one.

This was not only a lesson for the Muslims of that day; the Muslims of today must also draw inspiration from this heavenly teaching. They must never abandon fundamental programs for the sake of difficulties, discomforts, and exhausting efforts, nor should they pursue non-fundamental but simple and low-effort programs.

In the final verse, it reveals the matter even more clearly: "The main goal of this program (the Muslims' encounter with the enemy army at the field of Badr) was that the Truth—meaning Monotheism, Islam, justice, and the liberation of humanity from the clutches of superstitions, bondage, and injustices—be established and settled, and that Falsehood—meaning polytheism, infidelity, faithlessness, oppression, and corruption—be nullified and eliminated, even if the criminal polytheists and polytheistic criminals are averse to it" (لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَ يُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ).

Is this verse an emphasis on the same point mentioned in the previous verse, as it appears at first glance? Or does it contain a new point? Some commentators, such as Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir and the author of Al-Manar in his commentary, have said that "Truth" in the previous verse referred to the Muslims' victory in the Battle of Badr, but "Truth" in the second verse refers to the victory of Islam and the Qur'an, which was the result of the military victory at Badr. Thus, military victory in those specific circumstances was a prelude to the victory of the goal and the school of thought.

It is also possible that the previous verse referred to Allah's Will (the Legislative Will manifested in the form of the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله command), and the latter verse refers to the result of this decree and command (reflect on this).

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 121–136; Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, pp. 521–523 (abridged and summarized, with explanations in some parts). [1]: "Dabir" means the tail or rear of something; therefore, "cutting the dabir" is a metaphor for uprooting.

Ayahs

إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّى مُمِدُّكُم بِأَلْفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ (٩)
When you appealed to your Lord for help, He answered you: ‘I will aid you with a thousand angels in a file.’ (9)
وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ بِهِۦ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠)
Allah did not appoint it but as a good news, and to reassure your hearts. Victory comes only from Allah. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (10)
إِذْ يُغَشِّيكُمُ ٱلنُّعَاسَ أَمَنَةً مِّنْهُ وَيُنَزِّلُ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً لِّيُطَهِّرَكُم بِهِۦ وَيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمْ رِجْزَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيَرْبِطَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتَ بِهِ ٱلْأَقْدَامَ (١١)
When He covered you with a trance as a [sense of] security from Him, and He sent down water from the sky to purify you with it, and to repel from you the defilement of Satan, and to fortify your hearts, and to make [your] feet steady with it. (11)
إِذْ يُوحِى رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ أَنِّى مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ سَأُلْقِى فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلرُّعْبَ فَٱضْرِبُوا۟ فَوْقَ ٱلْأَعْنَاقِ وَٱضْرِبُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ كُلَّ بَنَانٍ (١٢)
Then your Lord signaled to the angels: ‘I am indeed with you; so steady the faithful. I will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless. So strike their necks, and strike their every limb joint!’ (12)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١٣)
That, because they defied Allah and His Apostle. And whoever defies Allah and His Apostle, Allah is indeed severe in retribution. (13)
ذَٰلِكُمْ فَذُوقُوهُ وَأَنَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٤)
‘Taste this, and [know] that for the faithless is the punishment of the Fire.’ (14)

Commentary

Instructive Lessons from the Field of Badr

These verses point to sensitive parts of the Battle of Badr and the various blessings that Allah bestowed upon the Muslims in this perilous scene, in order to stir their sense of obedience and gratitude and to open the way for future victories and advancements before them.

First, referring to the assistance of the angels, He says: "Remember when, out of intense fear and anxiety caused by the large number of the enemy and their superior military equipment, you took refuge in Allah, stretched out the hand of need toward Him, and requested His help" (إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ).

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was also united with the Muslims in this plea and seeking of help from Allah; he raised his hands toward the heavens and submitted:

اللهم انجز لى ما وعدتنى اللهم ان تهلك هذه العصابة لا تعبد فى الارض

"O Allah, fulfill for me what You have promised me. O Lord, if this group of believers is destroyed, Your worship will be removed from the earth." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) continued this plea and supplication so much that his cloak fell from his shoulders to the ground. [^1] At this moment, "Allah accepted your request and said: I will assist and help you with one thousand of the angels following one another in succession" (فَاسْتَجابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّي مُمِدُّكُمْ بِأَلْفٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ).

"Murdifin" (مردفين) is from the root "irdaf" (ارداف), meaning to be placed one after another. Therefore, the concept of this word is that the angels descended one after another to aid the Muslims.

Another possibility suggested for the meaning of the verse is that this group of one thousand had multiple other groups behind them, which aligns with verse 124 of Surah Al-Imran, where the Prophet said to the believers: "Is it not enough for you that your Lord should reinforce you with three thousand angels?"

However, the apparent meaning is that the number of angels at Badr was one thousand, and "murdifin" is an adjective for those thousand. The verse in Surah Al-Imran was a promise to the Muslims that if necessary, Allah would send an even greater number to help them.

Then, so that no one imagines that victory is solely in the hands of angels and the like, He says: "Allah made it only as a glad tiding and to reassure your hearts thereby" (وَ ما جَعَلَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرى‌ وَ لِتَطْمَئِنَّ بِهِ قُلُوبُكُمْ). "Otherwise, victory is not except from Allah," and above all these outward and inward means is His will and volition (وَ مَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ). "For Allah is so Mighty and Powerful that no one can stand against His will, and He is so Wise and Knowing that He will not grant His help except to those who are worthy" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

Did the Angels Fight?

There has been much discussion among commentators on this matter. Some believe that the angels formally entered the battlefield and attacked the enemy army with their own specific weapons, striking many of them down; they also cite some narrations in this regard.

However, there are indications that show the view of the second group—who say the angels descended only to encourage and strengthen the morale of the believers—is closer to reality.

Because, firstly: In the verse above, we read that He said: all of this was for the reassurance of your hearts so that you might fight better by feeling this support, not that they themselves engaged in combat.

Secondly: If the angels were to have bravely struck down the enemy soldiers, what virtue would remain for the mujahideen of Badr, about whom so much has been said in the narrations?

Thirdly: The number of those killed at Badr was 70, a significant portion of whom fell by the sword of Ali (عليه السلام), and the rest by the hands of other Islamic warriors, whose names are mostly recorded in history as the slayers. Therefore, what remains for the angels, and whom did they strike down?

Then He reminds the believers of His second blessing, saying: "Remember when a light sleep overcame you, providing a source of calm and security for your souls and bodies from Allah" (إِذْ يُغَشِّيكُمُ النُّعاسَ أَمَنَةً مِنْهُ). "Yughashshikum" (يغشى) is from the root "ghashayan" (غشيان), meaning to cover or surround; it is as if sleep was cast over them like a veil, covering them. "Nu'as" (نعاس) refers to the beginning of sleep or a short, light, and relaxing nap. Perhaps it indicates that while resting, such a deep sleep did not overcome you that the enemy could exploit the situation and launch a night attack.

Thus, the Muslims, on that night of great distress, benefited from this great blessing which helped them immensely on the battlefield the following day.

The third favor He bestowed upon you in that field was that "He sent down water from the sky for you" (وَ يُنَزِّلُ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً), "to purify you thereby and to remove from you the defilement of Satan" (لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ بِهِ وَ يُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمْ رِجْزَ الشَّيْطانِ). This defilement might have been satanic whispers, or physical impurity due to ritual impurity (janabah) affecting some that night, or both. In any case, this life-giving water, which collected in the hollows around Badr while the enemy held the wells and the Muslims were in dire need of washing and quenching their thirst, washed away all these impurities.

Furthermore, "Allah wanted to strengthen your hearts with this blessing" (وَ لِيَرْبِطَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِكُمْ) and also "He wanted to make your steps firm with the rainfall in that sandy area where your feet would sink and slip" (وَ يُثَبِّتَ بِهِ الْأَقْدامَ). It is also possible that "firming the steps" refers to strengthening morale and increasing the level of steadfastness and endurance in light of this blessing, or it could refer to both aspects.

Another of the Lord's blessings upon the mujahideen of the Battle of Badr was the fear and terror He cast into the hearts of the enemies, severely shaking their morale. Regarding this, He says: "Remember when your Lord revealed to the angels: 'I am with you, so strengthen and make firm those who believe'" (إِذْ يُوحِي رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلائِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) and "I will soon cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieve" (سَأُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ). And this was truly strange—that the powerful army of the Quraysh, against the small army of the Muslims, had (according to historical accounts) lost its morale to such an extent that a group was terrified of engaging with the Muslims. Sometimes they thought to themselves that these were not ordinary people; some said they have carried death upon their camels and brought it as a gift for you from Yathrib (Medina).

Undoubtedly, this casting of terror into the hearts of the enemy, which was an effective factor in victory, was not without reason. The steadfastness of the Muslims, their congregational prayers, their passionate slogans, the display of loyalty by the true believers, and words such as those of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh—who, representing the Ansar, said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! We have believed in you and testified to your prophethood, that whatever you say is from Allah. Give whatever command you wish and take whatever you want from our wealth. By Allah, if you command us to plunge into this sea (referring to the Red Sea nearby), we will plunge into it. We hope Allah grants us the success to perform a service that brings joy to your eyes"—yes, such words, which were spread among friend and foe alike, combined with what they had previously seen of the endurance of Muslim men and women in Mecca, all joined hands to create terror for the enemies.

The strong wind blowing toward the enemy front, the downpour that fell upon them, the terrifying memory of Atikah's dream in Mecca, and similar factors were other causes for their fear and anxiety.

Then He reminds them of the command given to the Muslims through the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) on the field of Badr, which was: when fighting the polytheists, avoid non-lethal strikes and do not waste your energy on them; rather, deliver lethal blows to the enemy's body—"strike above the necks, upon their brains and heads" (فَاضْرِبُوا فَوْقَ الْأَعْناقِ) "and disable their hands and feet" (وَ اضْرِبُوا مِنْهُمْ كُلَّ بَنانٍ). "Banan" (بنان) is the plural of "bananah," meaning the fingertips or the fingers themselves. In the verse under discussion, it could be a metaphor for the hands and feet, or it could mean its literal sense (fingers), because the severing or disabling of fingers, if on the hand, takes away the power to carry a weapon, and if on the foot, the power to move.

There is also the possibility that if the attacking enemy is on foot, make his head the target, and if he is mounted, his hands and feet.

As we previously mentioned, some consider this sentence to be addressed to the angels, but indications show that the addressees in this sentence are the Muslims. Even if the addressees were the angels, the goal of striking the brains and limbs might be to create terror in them such that their hands and feet falter and their heads bow down. (Of course, this interpretation is contrary to the apparent meaning of the phrase and must be proven with the help of the indications we previously mentioned regarding the angels not fighting).

After all these discussions, so that no one imagines these severe commands and decisive, crushing instructions to be contrary to the principles of chivalry, mercy, and fairness, He says: "They deserve such a thing because they rose in enmity, hostility, disobedience, and rebellion against Allah and His Messenger" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَاقُّوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ). "Shaqqu" (شاقوا) is from the root "shiqaq" (شقاق), which originally means a split or separation. Since the opponent, enemy, and rebel separates his rank, his action is called shiqaq. "And whoever rises in opposition and enmity against Allah and His Messenger will be caught in painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter, for Allah (just as His mercy is vast and infinite) is severe and painful in His punishment" (وَ مَنْ يُشاقِقِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

Then, to emphasize this matter, He says: "Taste this punishment of the world—the punishment of hard blows on the battlefield, being killed, taken captive, defeat, and failure—and wait for the punishment of the other abode," "for the punishment of the Fire awaits the disbelievers" (ذلِكُمْ فَذُوقُوهُ وَ أَنَّ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابَ النَّارِ).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ زَحْفًا فَلَا تُوَلُّوهُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ (١٥)
O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless advancing [for battle], do not turn your backs [to flee] from them. (15)
وَمَن يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُۥٓ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفًا لِّقِتَالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزًا إِلَىٰ فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ بَآءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٦)
Whoever turns his back [to flee] from them that day—unless [he is] diverting to fight or retiring towards another troop—shall certainly earn Allah’s wrath, and his refuge shall be hell, an evil destination. (16)
فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَتَلَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَمَىٰ ۚ وَلِيُبْلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلَآءً حَسَنًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٧)
You did not kill them; rather, it was Allah who killed them; and you did not throw when you threw, rather, it was Allah who threw, that He might test the faithful with a good test from Himself. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (17)
ذَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُوهِنُ كَيْدِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٨)
Such is the case, and [know] that Allah undermines the stratagems of the faithless. (18)

Commentary

Fleeing from Jihad is Forbidden!

As noted in the commentary of the previous verses, recounting the story of the Battle of Badr and Allah’s various blessings upon the "early Muslims" in this event is intended to provide lessons from the past for the future. Therefore, in the verses under discussion, the address is directed toward the believers, recommending and emphasizing a general military instruction: "O you who have believed, when you meet those who disbelieve in a battlefield, do not turn your backs to them [in flight]" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلَا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبَارَ). The word laqitum is derived from the root liqa’, meaning to meet or encounter, but in many instances, it refers to meeting on the battlefield.

Zahfan originally means moving toward something in a way that the feet are dragged along the ground, similar to the movement of a child before they learn to walk, or a camel that drags its feet on the ground due to exhaustion. Subsequently, it was applied to the movement of a massive army because, from a distance, it appears as if they are gliding across the earth as they advance.

In the above verse, the use of the word zahfan indicates that even if the enemy is numerous in terms of personnel and equipment and you are in the minority, you must not flee the field of struggle—just as the enemy's numbers at Badr were several times your own, yet you showed steadfastness and ultimately emerged victorious.

In principle, fleeing from war is considered one of the greatest sins in Islam. However, based on certain verses of the Quran, this is conditioned upon the enemy's population being at most twice that of the Muslims, the discussion of which will come, Allah willing, under verses 65 and 66 of this same Surah.

For this reason, in the following verse, the painful punishment for those who flee the field of Jihad is described, along with its exceptions: "And whoever turns his back to them on such a day—unless it be as a strategy of war or to retreat to [join] another company—has certainly incurred the wrath of Allah" (وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتَالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلَىٰ فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ بَاءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ). As we observe, two forms of retreat are exempted in this verse; they appear as flight in outward form but are, in essence, forms of struggle and Jihad.

The first is described as mutaharrifan li-qital. Mutaharrif is derived from taharruf, meaning to move from the center toward the sides or edges. The meaning of this phrase is that warriors retreat from the enemy as a military tactic to draw them out, only to suddenly deliver a surprise blow to them, or to exhaust the enemy through hit-and-run tactics—where war is sometimes an attack and sometimes a retreat for the purpose of a renewed assault. As the Arabs say: "War is karr wa farr" (attack and retreat). The second form is when a warrior finds himself alone on the field and retreats to join a group of fellow warriors, only to launch an attack after joining them.

In any case, the command prohibiting flight from the field must not be interpreted in a rigid manner that eliminates military maneuvers and tactics—the very maneuvers that are the source of many victories.

At the end of the verse, it states that not only do those who flee incur the wrath of God, but "their refuge is Hell—and wretched is the destination" (وَ مَأْواهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ). The word ba’a is from the root buwa’, meaning to return and take up residence, but its root meaning is to level or flatten a place. Since a person levels their spot when taking up residence, the word has come to mean this; also, because a person returns to their residence, it has come to mean "return." In the above verse, it indicates that they become subject to the continuous and lasting wrath of the Lord, as if they have taken up residence within His anger.

Ma’wa originally means a refuge or shelter. The fact that we read in the verse that the refuge of those who flee Jihad is Hell indicates that by fleeing, they seek to choose a refuge for themselves to remain safe from destruction, but on the contrary, their refuge becomes Hell—not only in the next world but also in this world, where they will take refuge in a burning hell of humiliation, misery, defeat, and deprivation.

Thus, in the book Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, in response to one of his friends who asked about the philosophy behind many rulings, writes regarding the prohibition of fleeing from Jihad: "Allah prohibited fleeing from Jihad because it causes weakness and frailty in the religion, and contempt for the program of the Prophets, Imams, and just leaders. It also causes them to be unable to triumph over enemies and punish the enemy for opposing the call to the Oneness of the Lord, the implementation of justice, the abandonment of tyranny, and the eradication of corruption. Furthermore, it causes the enemies to become emboldened against the Muslims, and even leads to Muslims being captured and killed at their hands, and ultimately, the religion of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, being abolished." [^1] Among the many distinctions that Ali عليه السلام possessed—and which he sometimes mentioned as an example for others—was this very issue of never fleeing the field of Jihad, where he says:

"I have never fled from an advancing army (zahf) at all, and no one has ever dueled me except that I saturated the earth with his blood!" [^2]

It is strange that a group of Sunni commentators insist that the ruling of the above verse was specific to the Battle of Badr and that this threat and intensification regarding fleeing from Jihad pertains only to the warriors of Badr. This is while there is not only no evidence in the verse for such specificity, but the concept of the verse is a general one concerning all warriors and all mujahideen. Other evidences in verses and narrations also confirm this (of course, this Islamic ruling has conditions that will be explained in future verses of this same Surah).

Then, so that the Muslims would not become arrogant due to the victory at Badr and rely solely on their own physical strength, but rather always keep their hearts and souls warm and enlightened with the remembrance of Allah and His aid, He says: "It was not you who killed them [at Badr], but it was Allah who killed them" (فَلَمْ تَقْتُلُوهُمْ وَلَكِنَّ اللَّهَ قَتَلَهُمْ). "And you [O Prophet] did not throw [dust and pebbles] into their faces when you threw, but it was Allah who threw" (وَمَا رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلَكِنَّ اللَّهَ رَمَىٰ).

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 138. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 139.

It is mentioned in Islamic narrations and by commentators that on the day of Badr, the Prophet said to Ali: "Take a handful of dust and pebbles from the ground and give it to me." Ali عليه السلام did so, and the Prophet threw them toward the polytheists and said: "May their faces be disgraced!" (shahat al-wujuh). It is written that this act had a miraculous effect, and that dust and pebbles entered the eyes of the enemies, causing terror to strike them all.

There is no doubt that outwardly, the Prophet and the warriors of Badr performed all these actions. However, saying "it was not you who did this" indicates that, firstly: the physical and spiritual power and the strength of faith, which were the source of these programs, were granted to you by Allah, and you stepped forth in His way with God-given strength. Secondly: in the field of Badr, miraculous events occurred—which we previously mentioned—that served to strengthen the morale of the Muslim warriors and cause the collapse of the enemy's morale. This extraordinary effect was also from the Lord.

In reality, the above verse is a subtle reference to the school of "Neither compulsion (jabr) nor absolute delegation (tafwid), but a matter between the two matters." This is because while it attributes the killing of the enemies to the Muslims and the throwing of dust to the Prophet, it simultaneously negates this attribution from them (observe closely).

Undoubtedly, there is no contradiction in such an expression; rather, the goal is that this act was both your act and Allah’s act. It was your act because it was carried out by your will, and it was Allah’s act because the power and aid were from Him. Therefore, those who have imagined that the above verse is evidence for the school of predestination (jabr) find their answer hidden within the verse itself.

Similarly, for those proponents of "Unity of Existence" (wahdat al-wujud) who have used this verse as a pretext for their school, the answer is also subtly reflected in this verse. For if the intention were to state that God and the creation are one, He should not have affirmed the attribution of the action to them in one sense and negated it from them in another. This negation and affirmation are themselves evidence of the plurality of the Creator and the created. If we free our minds from incorrect and biased preconceptions, we will see that the verse has no connection to any of the deviant schools, but rather points only to the middle school of "a matter between the two matters"—and that is for a pedagogical purpose: namely, to eliminate the effects of arrogance that usually afflict individuals after victories.

At the end of the verse, He points to another important point: that the field of Badr was a field of testing for the Muslims, "and that He might test the believers with a good test from Himself" (وَلِيُبْلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْهُ بَلَاءً حَسَناً).

Bala’ originally means testing; however, sometimes it is through blessings, which is called "a good test" (bala’ hasan), and sometimes through calamities and punishments, which is called "an evil test" (bala’ sayyi’), as we read regarding the Children of Israel: "And We tested them with good things and evil things" (Al-A'raf: 168).

Allah wanted to let the believers taste the flavor of victory in this first armed encounter with powerful enemies and to make them hopeful and encouraged regarding the future. This divine gift was a test for all of them, but they must never draw a negative conclusion from this victory and become afflicted with arrogance, underestimate the enemy, forget self-building and preparation, or neglect reliance on the grace of the Lord.

Therefore, He concludes the verse with the statement: "Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

This means that Allah heard the cries for help from the Prophet and the believers and was aware of their sincerity of intention and devotion. For this reason, He included them all in His grace and made them victorious over the enemy. In the future as well, Allah will treat the Muslims according to their intentions, the degree of their sincerity, and the extent of their steadfastness and endurance. Sincere believers and mujahideen will ultimately be victorious, while hypocritical pretenders and those who speak without action will fail.

In the following verse, to emphasize and generalize this subject, He says: "The fate of the believers and the disbelievers and the outcome of their affair was as you heard" (dhalikum). [^1] Then, stating the reason, He says: "And [know] that Allah is the weakener of the plot of the disbelievers" (وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ مُوهِنُ كَيْدِ الْكَافِرِينَ), so that they cannot harm the believers or their programs.

Ayah

إِن تَسْتَفْتِحُوا۟ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْفَتْحُ ۖ وَإِن تَنتَهُوا۟ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تَعُودُوا۟ نَعُدْ وَلَن تُغْنِىَ عَنكُمْ فِئَتُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَوْ كَثُرَتْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٩)
If you sought a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you; and if you cease [your belligerence against the Prophet and his followers], it is better for you, but if you resume, We [too] shall return and your troops will never avail you though they should be ever so many, and [know] that Allah is with the faithful. (19)

Commentary

There is a discussion among commentators regarding who is being addressed in the above verse. A group believes that the addressees in this verse are the polytheists, because before they departed from Mecca for the battlefield of Badr, they came to the Kaaba. Out of the arrogance they possessed and because they considered themselves to be in the right, they took hold of the curtains of the Kaaba and said: "O Allah! Grant victory to the superior of the two armies, the more guided of the two groups, and the more honorable of the two parties." [^1] It is also narrated that Abu Jahl said in his prayer: "O Allah! Our religion is an ancient and old religion, while the religion of Muhammad is new and raw; whichever of these two religions is more beloved to You, grant victory to its followers." [^2] Therefore, after the end of the Battle of Badr, the above verse was revealed and said to them: "If you were seeking victory and the religion of truth, then the religion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله has triumphed and its truthfulness has become manifest to you" (إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ).

"And if you desist from the religion of polytheism and opposing the command of Allah, it is better for you" (وَ إِنْ تَنْتَهُوا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ).

"And if you return to war against the Muslims, We too shall return once more," meaning We will make the Muslims victorious and you defeated (وَ إِنْ تَعُودُوا نَعُدْ).

And never be deceived by the abundance of your numbers, for "your forces, however numerous they may be, shall avail you nothing" (وَ لَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْكُمْ فِئَتُكُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لَوْ كَثُرَتْ).

"And Allah is with the believers" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

However, what makes this interpretation seem unlikely is that in the preceding and succeeding verses, the address is entirely directed toward the believers, and the context of the verses shows that there is a spiritual connection between them. Therefore, it seems improbable that in the middle of these, only one verse would be addressed to the disbelievers.

Consequently, another group of commentators considers the believers to be the addressees, and the best way to interpret the verse according to this view is as follows:

After the Battle of Badr—as we have seen—a dispute arose among some of the new and weak-in-faith Muslims regarding the distribution of the spoils of war. Verses were revealed that rebuked them and placed the spoils entirely at the disposal of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and he distributed them equally among the Muslims. Then, for the education and upbringing of the believers, He reminded them of the events of the Battle of Badr—how Allah granted them victory against a powerful group.

This verse follows the same theme: if you Muslims requested conquest and victory from Allah, He answered your prayer and you were victorious.

And if you refrain from protesting and disputing before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is better for you.

And if you return to that same method of protest, We too shall return and leave you alone in the clutches of the enemy.

And your numbers, however large they may be, will achieve nothing without the help of Allah.

And Allah is with the true believers who are obedient to His command and His Prophet.

Since the upcoming verses specifically reproach the Muslims for certain acts of opposition, and we read the same meaning in the previous verses, and also because the connection between these verses is a clear spiritual bond, the second interpretation appears stronger.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi under the verse, and also Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 15, p. 142. [^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and other commentaries under the verse.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَا تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَسْمَعُونَ (٢٠)
O you who have faith! Obey Allah and His Apostle, and do not turn away from him while you hear [him]. (20)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٢١)
Do not be like those who say, ‘We hear,’ though they do not hear. (21)
۞ إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلْبُكْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٢)
Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are the deaf and dumb who do not exercise their reason. (22)
وَلَوْ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا لَّأَسْمَعَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ أَسْمَعَهُمْ لَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٢٣)
Had Allah known any good in them, He would have surely made them hear, and were He to make them hear, surely they would turn away, being disregardful. (23)

Commentary

Deaf Listeners!

These verses follow the previous discussions regarding the call for Muslims to fully obey the Prophet of Islam in war and peace and in all programs. The tone of the verses indicates that some believers had fallen short of their duty in this regard; therefore, in the first verse, it says: "O you who have believed, obey Allah and His Messenger" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ) and again, for emphasis, it adds: "and do not turn away from him while you hear [his words, commands, and prohibitions]" (وَلَا تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ وَأَنْتُمْ تَسْمَعُونَ).

There is no doubt that obeying the command of Allah is mandatory for everyone, whether believers or disbelievers, but since the Prophet's audience and the participants in his educational programs were the believers, the address here is directed at them.

In the next verse, it emphasizes this same issue once more, saying: "And do not be like those who say, 'We have heard,' while in fact they do not hear" (وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ قَالُوا سَمِعْنَا وَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ). This is a profound expression that the Quran uses regarding those who know but do not act, and who hear but do not follow through—those who outwardly appear in the ranks of the believers but are not obedient to the command. It says: they have hearing ears, they hear the words and speech, and they understand their meanings, but because they do not act accordingly, it is as if they are entirely deaf. This is because all these matters are a prelude to action, and when there is no action, the preludes are useless.

Regarding who these individuals are—those whom the Quran describes with such a quality and warns Muslims to be careful not to become like—some have suggested they are the hypocrites who had inserted themselves into the ranks of the Muslims. Others have said it refers to a group of Jews, and some believe it refers to the Arab polytheists. However, there is no obstacle to including all those who speak without acting from these three groups within the meaning of the verse.

Since speech without action and hearing without following through is one of the greatest plagues of human societies and the source of various misfortunes, the next verse emphasizes this issue once more and continues the discussion with another beautiful expression, saying: "Indeed, the worst of living creatures in the sight of Allah are the deaf and dumb who do not use reason" (إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ) [1]. Since the Quran is a book of action and not a ceremonial book, it emphasizes results everywhere. Fundamentally, it considers any useless being as non-existent, any living thing without movement or effect as dead, and any human organ that is not effective in the path of guidance and salvation as being absent. In this verse, those who outwardly have healthy ears but are not in the path of hearing the verses of Allah, the words of truth, and the salvation-giving programs are considered to lack ears. Those who have healthy tongues but have placed the seal of silence on their lips—neither defending the truth nor fighting against oppression and corruption, neither guiding the ignorant nor commanding the right nor forbidding the wrong, nor inviting to the path of truth, but rather using this great blessing of Allah in the path of vain talk, flattery of the powerful and wealthy, or distorting the truth and strengthening falsehood—are considered like the dumb and mute. And those who benefit from the blessing of intelligence and reason but do not think correctly are counted as the insane!

[^1]: "Summ" (صم) is the plural of "Asamm" (اصم) meaning deaf, and "Bukm" (بكم) is the plural of "Abkam" (ابكم) meaning dumb.

In the next verse, it says that Allah does not withhold anything in inviting them toward the truth: "If Allah had known any good in them, He would have made them hear" (وَلَوْ عَلِمَ اللَّهُ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا لَأَسْمَعَهُمْ).

It is mentioned in some narrations that a group of stubborn idolaters came to the Prophet and said: "If you raise our great ancestor 'Qusay ibn Kilab' from the grave and he bears witness to your prophethood, we will all submit!" The above verse was revealed and stated that if they had said this out of truthfulness, Allah would have performed this for them miraculously.

But they are lying and making excuses, and their goal is to shirk the burden of truth.

"And even if Allah were to grant their request and make them hear the words of truth further, or raise their ancestor Qusay ibn Kilab and they heard his testimony, they would still turn away and be averse" (وَلَوْ أَسْمَعَهُمْ لَتَوَلَّوْا وَهُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ).

These sentences are about those who have heard the words of truth many times, and the soul-nurturing verses of the Quran have reached their ears, and they have understood its sublime content, yet due to prejudice and stubbornness, they rose to deny it. Such individuals have lost the merit for guidance due to their own actions, and Allah and His Prophet no longer have any business with them.

This verse is a crushing response to the followers of the school of determinism (Jabr), and it shows that the source of all happiness begins from the human being himself, and Allah treats people according to the readiness and merit they demonstrate.

Two points must be noted here

1- Sometimes, some novices construct a logical syllogism from the above verse and derive a confusing result for themselves, saying: The Quran says in the above verse, "If Allah knew any good in them, He would make them hear," and "If He made them hear, they would turn away." The result of these two sentences becomes: "If Allah sees good in them, they will turn away!" And this result is not a correct one.

But their mistake lies in the fact that the phrase "He would make them hear" in the first part of the statement means that if they had a prepared ground, He would make them hear the truth.

However, in the second part of the statement, the meaning is that if He were to do such a thing while the ground is not prepared, they would turn away.

Therefore, the above phrase is used in two different and separate meanings in the verse, and thus one cannot form a logical syllogism from them [1] (Observe carefully).

This is exactly like someone saying: "If I knew so-and-so would accept my invitation, I would invite him; but currently, the situation is such that if I invite him, he will not accept, therefore I will not invite him."

[^1]: In logical terms, there is no "middle term" in the above syllogism, because in the first sentence it is "He would make them hear while He knows good in them," and in the second sentence it is "He would make them hear while He knows no good in them." Therefore, the common middle term does not exist in the two sentences to form a syllogism, and these two sentences are distinct and separate (Observe carefully).

2- Hearing the word of truth has stages:

Sometimes a person only hears words and phrases without thinking about their meaning. There is a group of stubborn individuals who are not even willing to do this much hearing.

As the Quran says: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَا تَسْمَعُوا لِهذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَالْغَوْا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ: "And those who disbelieve say, 'Do not listen to this Quran and make noise therein that you may overcome'"—so that no one hears the word of truth (Fussilat: 26).

And sometimes a person is willing to hear the words and speech but never intends to act, like the hypocrites referred to in verse 16 of Surah Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, where it says: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّى إِذَا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ قَالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ مَا ذَا قَالَ آنِفاً: "And among them are those who listen to you, but when they depart from you, they say to those who were given knowledge [out of denial or mockery], 'What was it that he (Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله) said just now?'"

And sometimes their condition is such that the sense of distinguishing good from evil has been so stripped from them that even if they listen, they do not comprehend the truth, and this is the most dangerous stage.

The Quran says regarding all these three groups: they are in reality deaf and unhearing, because the true listener is one who both listens and understands, thinks, and resolves to act out of sincerity.

And how many there are in our age and time who, upon hearing the verses of the Quran (without comparison, like hearing musical melodies), show emotions and manifest noise and phrases indicating excitement and passion, but their entire effort is limited to this and nothing more, and in practice, they are wretchedly polluted individuals who bear no resemblance to the content of the Quran.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ ۖ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَأَنَّهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٤)
O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life. Know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart and that toward Him you will be mustered. (24)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ فِتْنَةً لَّا تُصِيبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنكُمْ خَآصَّةً ۖ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢٥)
And beware of a punishment, which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively, and know that Allah is severe in retribution. (25)
وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ أَنتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُّسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ تَخَافُونَ أَن يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ فَـَٔاوَىٰكُمْ وَأَيَّدَكُم بِنَصْرِهِۦ وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٢٦)
Remember when you were few, abased in the land, and feared lest the people should despoil you, and He gave you refuge, and strengthened you with His help, and provided you with all the good things so that you may give thanks. (26)

Commentary

An Invitation to Life

Following the previous verses that invited Muslims to knowledge, action, obedience, and submission, these verses pursue the same objective through a different path.

First, it says: "O you who have believed, respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ). The above verse explicitly states that the invitation of Islam is an invitation toward life—spiritual life, material life, cultural life, economic life, political life in its true sense, moral and social life, and ultimately, life in all dimensions.

This expression is the shortest and most comprehensive description of Islam and the religion of truth. If someone asks, "What is the goal of Islam? And what can it give us?" we answer in one short sentence: Its goal is life in all fields, and this is what it grants us.
