Were people dead before the dawn of Islam and the call of the Quran, such that the Quran invites them to life?! The answer to this question is: Yes, they lacked life in its Quranic sense, for we know that life has various stages to which the Quran has referred.

Sometimes it refers to "vegetative life," as it says: "Know that Allah gives life to the earth after its death" (اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا) (Al-Hadid, Verse 17). Sometimes it is mentioned in the sense of "animal life," such as: "Indeed, He who has given it [the earth] life is the Giver of Life to the dead" (إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتَى) (Fussilat, 39). At times, it refers to "intellectual, rational, and human life," such as: "Is he who was dead and We gave him life..." (أَوَمَنْ كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ...)—meaning, is he who was astray and We guided him like those who remain astray? (Al-An'am, 122). Sometimes it refers to the "eternal life" of the next world, such as: "Oh, I wish I had sent forth [some good] for my life!" (يَا لَيْتَنِي قَدَّمْتُ لِحَيَاتِي) (Surah Al-Fajr, Verse 24). And at times, it refers to "infinite knowledge and power," as we say regarding Allah: "He is the Ever-Living who does not die" (هُوَ الْحَيُّ الَّذِي لَا يَمُوتُ).

Considering what we have said about the types of life, it becomes clear that although the people of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) possessed material and animal life, they were deprived of human, spiritual, and rational life. The Quran came and invited them to life.

From this, it becomes well-evident how mistaken are those who perceive religion as a series of dry, soulless programs outside the scope of life and on the margins of intellectual and social agendas. A true religion is one that creates movement in all fields of life, provides spirit, grants thought and reflection, creates a sense of responsibility, and establishes solidarity, unity, progress, and perfection in all areas—being life-giving in every sense of the word! Furthermore, this reality also clarifies that those who have interpreted the above verse solely as "Jihad," "Faith," "the Quran," or "Paradise," and introduced these as the only factors of life in the verse, have in fact restricted its meaning. The concept of the verse encompasses all of these and more; anything, any thought, any program, and any command that creates a form of human life is included in the above verse.

Then it says: "And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart, and that to Him you will be gathered" (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَأَنَّهُ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

There is no doubt that by "heart," as we have previously stated, is meant the soul and the intellect. As for how Allah stands between a person and his soul and intellect, many possibilities have been suggested:

Sometimes it is said: It refers to the intensity of Allah's closeness to His servants, as if He is within his soul and between him and himself, just as the Quran says: "And We are closer to him than [his] jugular vein" (وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ). Sometimes it is said: It refers to the fact that the turning of hearts and thoughts is in the hand of Allah, as we read in the supplication: "O Turner of hearts and sights" (يا مقلب القلوب و الأبصار).

And sometimes it is said: The meaning is that if it were not for Allah's grace, man would never realize the truth of the truth and the falsehood of falsehood.

It has also been said: The meaning is that people must strive to perform acts of obedience and good deeds while they have the opportunity, for Allah creates an intervention between man and his heart through death.

However, with a general view, all these interpretations can be gathered into a single one: that Allah is present and observant everywhere and encompasses all beings. While He is not identical to the beings of this world, He is not separate or alien from them either. Death and life, knowledge and power, peace and security, success and happiness are all in His hand and by His power. For this reason, man can neither hide anything from Him, nor perform any task without His success (tawfiq), nor is it appropriate to turn to anyone other than Him or request from anyone else, for He is the Owner of all things and encompasses the entire existence of man! The connection of this sentence to the previous one is that if the Prophet invites to life, he is the messenger of One in whose hand are life, death, guidance, and intellect.

Therefore, to emphasize this matter, it says that not only today are you within the scope of His power, but in the other abode as well, you shall return to Him; here and there, you are all before Him.

Then it points to the dire consequences of not accepting the life-giving call of Allah and the Prophet, saying: "And fear a trial (fitnah) which will not strike those who have wronged among you exclusively" (وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لَا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً).

"Fitnah" is used in the Holy Quran in several instances; sometimes it means trial and examination, and sometimes it means calamity, affliction, and punishment. This word originally meant placing gold in a furnace to reveal its quality; subsequently, it was used for trials that reveal the nature of human inner qualities, and also for calamities and punishments that result in the purification of the human soul or the mitigation of his sins.

In the verse under discussion, "fitnah" means social calamities and afflictions that affect everyone, where, as the saying goes, "the dry and the wet burn together."

In reality, this is the nature of social events: when a society falls short in fulfilling its mission, and as a result, lawlessness, chaos, insecurity, and the like are produced, the good and the bad burn in its fire. This is a warning that Allah gives in this verse to all Islamic societies, and its meaning is:

Members of society are not only duty-bound to perform their own tasks but are also responsible for compelling others to fulfill their duties. This is because discord, fragmentation, and lack of coordination in social matters will lead to the failure of programs, and the "smoke will go into everyone's eyes." I cannot say that because I have performed my duty, I will remain immune to the dire effects of others' negligence, for the effects of social issues are not individual or personal.

This is exactly like a situation where one hundred thousand strong soldiers are needed to prevent an enemy invasion; if a group, say fifty thousand, performs its duty, it will certainly not be enough, and the dire results of defeat will affect both those who fulfilled their duty and those who did not. As we said, this is the characteristic of collective and group issues.

This reality can be clarified in another way: the righteous of society have a duty not to remain silent before the wicked. If they choose silence, they will be partners and participants in their fate before Allah.

As narrated in a famous Hadith from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, does not punish the general public for the actions of a specific group until they see the forbidden (munkar) among them while they are capable of denouncing it. If they do so [remain silent], Allah will punish both the specific group and the general public." [^1]

From what we have said, it becomes clear that this rule is valid both in the context of divine punishments in this world and the hereafter, and in the context of the results and effects of collective and group actions. [^1] At the end of the verse, in a threatening tone, it says: "And know that Allah is severe in penalty" (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقَابِ).

Lest the grace and mercy of Allah make them heedless, causing them to forget the severity of divine punishments, and lest turmoil and trials overtake them, just as they overtook the Islamic society and, due to forgetting these divine laws, drove them backward.

A brief look at Islamic societies in our time—the successive defeats they suffer against enemies, the trials of "colonialism," "Zionism," "atheism," materialism, moral corruption, the disintegration of families, the fall of youth into the lap of indecency, and scientific decline—vividly illustrates the truth and content of this verse: how these trials have engulfed the small and the great, the good and the bad, the scholar and the ignorant. These will continue until the time Muslims develop a social spirit, practically accept public oversight in society, and the two duties of "enjoining what is right" and "forbidding what is wrong" are implemented as definitive and non-negotiable obligations.

Once again, the Quran takes the hands of the Muslims and returns them to their historical past, making them realize what state they were in and what stage they are in now, so they may properly understand the lesson taught in the previous verses.

It says: "And remember when you were few and oppressed in the land, fearing that people might snatch you away" (وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ أَنْتُمْ قَلِيلٌ مُسْتَضْعَفُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تَخافُونَ أَنْ يَتَخَطَّفَكُمُ النَّاسُ).

This expression is a subtle one that reveals the extreme weakness and small numbers of the Muslims at that time, as if they were like a small object suspended in the air that the enemy could easily snatch away. This refers to the situation of Muslims in Mecca before the Hijrah (migration) against the powerful polytheists, or to the Muslims after the Hijrah against the great powers of that day, such as Persia and Rome.

"But He sheltered you" (فَآوَاكُمْ). "And supported you with His victory" (وَأَيَّدَكُمْ بِنَصْرِهِ). "And provided you with good things" (وَرَزَقَكُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ). "That perhaps you might be grateful" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 9, p. 638. [^1]: There is a discussion among commentators whether the word "la tusibanna" is a negative (nafy) or a prohibitive (nahy) form. A group considers it prohibitive, meaning: "Beware of trials, for they must not strike only the wrongdoers." Others consider it negative. However, since according to scholars of Arabic literature, the "nun of emphasis" (nun al-tawkid) only appears in prohibitions or the response to an oath, they have considered the sentence as a response to an oath, as if an oath is implied in the verse.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَخُونُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوٓا۟ أَمَـٰنَـٰتِكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٧)
O you who have faith! Do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts knowingly. (27)
وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٨)
Know that your possessions and children are only a test, and that Allah—with Him is a great reward. (28)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the revelation of the above verses, several narrations have been cited. Among them, Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام narrated that the Prophet ordered the siege of the "Banu Qurayza" (a tribe of Jews in Medina). This siege lasted for twenty-one nights, forcing them to propose a peace treaty similar to the one their brothers from the "Banu Nadir" (another group of Jews in Medina) had made—namely, to emigrate from the land of Medina and head toward Sham (Greater Syria). The Prophet refused this proposal (perhaps because their sincerity in this offer was doubtful) and stated that they must only accept the arbitration of "Sa'd ibn Mu'adh." They requested that the Prophet send "Abu Lubaba" (who was one of the Prophet's companions in Medina) to them, as Abu Lubaba had a history of friendship with them, and his family, children, and wealth were among them.

The Prophet accepted this request and sent Abu Lubaba to them. They consulted with Abu Lubaba, asking whether it was advisable to accept the arbitration of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh.

Abu Lubaba pointed to his throat, signaling that if they accepted, they would be executed, and advised them not to submit to this proposal. The messenger of divine revelation, Gabriel, informed the Prophet of this matter.

Abu Lubaba says: "I had not yet taken a step when I realized that I had betrayed Allah and the Prophet." The above verses were revealed concerning him.

At this moment, Abu Lubaba became severely distressed, to the point that he tied himself with a rope to one of the pillars of the Prophet's Mosque and said: "By Allah, I shall neither eat food nor drink water until death overtakes me, unless Allah accepts my repentance."

Seven days and nights passed; he neither ate nor drank, until he fell unconscious to the ground. Allah accepted his repentance. This news reached him through the believers, but he swore: "I will not untie myself from the pillar until the Prophet comes and releases me."

The Prophet came and untied him. Abu Lubaba said: "To complete my repentance, I shall abandon my house where I committed the sin and give up all my wealth." The Prophet replied: "It is sufficient for you to give one-third of your wealth as charity in the way of Allah." [1] This same theme regarding the occasion of the verse's revelation appears in Sunni books as well. However, since the preceding verses were related to the event of Badr, some have found it unlikely that this verse concerns the story of the Jews and Banu Qurayza, as that event occurred much later. Therefore, they have suggested that the aforementioned narrations mean that the story of Abu Lubaba is one of the instances to which the verse applies, rather than it being revealed at that specific time; such an interpretation regarding the occasion of revelation has precedents.

For example, in some books, it is narrated from certain companions that a particular verse was revealed regarding the assassination of Uthman, while we know that Uthman's assassination occurred many years after the Prophet's passing.

There is also the possibility that the verse was revealed during the Banu Qurayza incident but, due to its relevance to the verses of Badr, was joined to them by the Prophet's command.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 143.

Commentary

Treachery and Its Source

In the first verse, Allah addresses the believers, saying: "O you who have believed, do not betray Allah and the Messenger" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَخُونُوا اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ).

Betrayal of Allah and the Prophet consists of placing the military secrets of Muslims at the disposal of others, strengthening enemies in their struggle, or generally casting aside the obligations, prohibitions, and divine programs. Thus, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that whoever abandons any part of the Islamic programs has committed a form of betrayal against Allah and the Prophet.

Then He says: "nor betray your trusts" (وَتَخُونُوا أَمَانَاتِكُمْ). [1] "Betrayal" (Khiyanah) originally means withholding a right that a person has committed to fulfill, and it is the opposite of "trust" (Amanah).

Although "trust" usually refers to financial trusts, in the logic of the Quran, it has a broad concept that encompasses all aspects of social, political, and moral life. Thus, it is stated in a Hadith: "المجالس بالأمانة" (Gatherings are a trust), meaning conversations held in private sessions are a trust.

In another Hadith, we read: "إذا حدث الرجل بحديث ثم التفت فهو أمانة" (When a man relates something and then looks around [to see if anyone heard], it is a trust).

[1]: "Takhunu" is originally "la takhunu," where the "la" is omitted due to the context of the preceding clause.

From this perspective, the land and water of Islam in the hands of Muslims are a divine trust; their children are a trust; and above all, the Holy Quran and its teachings are considered the great trust of the Lord.

Some have said that Allah's trust is His religion, the Prophet's trust is his Sunnah, and the believers' trust is their wealth and secrets; however, "trust" in the above verse encompasses all of these.

In any case, betrayal of trust is among the most loathsome acts and the ugliest of sins. One who betrays a trust is, in reality, a hypocrite, as narrated in a Hadith from the Prophet: "آية المنافق ثلاث: إذا حدث كذب، وإذا وعد أخلف، وإذا ائتمن خان، وإن صام وصلى وزعم أنه مسلم" (The signs of a hypocrite are three: when he speaks, he lies; when he promises, he breaks it; and when he is entrusted, he betrays—even if he fasts, prays, and claims to be a Muslim).

Fundamentally, refraining from betrayal of trust is among human duties and rights; that is, even if the owner of the trust is not a Muslim, one cannot betray their trust.

At the end of the verse, it says: It is possible that you might commit something that is a betrayal out of mistake or lack of knowledge, but never undertake such an act knowingly (وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ). Of course, actions like those of Abu Lubaba are not instances of ignorance or mistake; rather, love and affection for wealth and children and the preservation of personal interests sometimes close a person's eyes and ears in a sensitive moment, leading them to commit betrayal against Allah and the Prophet. This is, in truth, a conscious betrayal. But the important thing is for a person to wake up quickly, like Abu Lubaba, and compensate for the past.

In the next verse, He warns the Muslims to be careful that affection for material matters and fleeting personal interests does not cast a veil over their eyes and ears, leading them to commit betrayals that jeopardize the fate of their society. He says: "And know that your properties and your children are but a trial" (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا أَمْوَالُكُمْ وَأَوْلَادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ).

"Fitnah," as we have previously mentioned, means a "means of testing" in such contexts. In reality, the most important means of testing faith and disbelief, character and lack thereof, and the measure of a person's human value are these two subjects.

How wealth is acquired, how it is spent, how it is maintained, and the degree of attachment and affection for it are all fields of human trial. There are many who are very strict and faithful regarding ordinary acts of worship and outward displays of religion, and even sometimes regarding the performance of recommended acts (Mustahabbat); however, when a financial matter comes into play, everything is set aside, and all divine laws, human issues, and truth and justice are forgotten.

Regarding children, who are the fruits of a person's heart and the blossoms of his life, it is often the same. We see many who are outwardly committed to religious matters and human and moral issues, but when it comes to their children, it is as if a veil falls over their thoughts and they forget all these matters. Love for the child causes them to consider the forbidden as permissible and the permissible as forbidden, and to submit to any deed and trample upon any right to secure an imaginary future for them. We must entrust ourselves to Allah in these two great fields of examination and be alert, for many have slipped and fallen in these two fields, bringing eternal condemnation upon themselves.

If one day a slip occurs from us, we must, like Abu Lubaba, rise to compensate for the slip and even sacrifice the wealth that caused such a slip in this path.

At the end of the verse, He gives glad tidings to those who emerge victorious from these two fields of testing: "and that Allah has with Him a great reward" (وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ).

No matter how great the love for a child may seem, and no matter how much wealth is considered significant and attractive, the reward and recompense of the Lord are still superior, more sublime, and greater than them.

Here, there are questions such as: Why does Allah, with His encompassing knowledge, test people? And why is Allah's testing universal, even including the Prophets? And what are the materials of divine testing and the way to victory in them?

The answers to all these questions have been explained in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 384 to 390.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّكُمْ فُرْقَانًا وَيُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢٩)
O you who have faith! If you are wary of Allah, He shall appoint a criterion for you, and absolve you of your misdeeds, and forgive you, for Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (29)

Commentary

Faith and Insight

In the preceding verses, a series of life-giving instructions that guarantee material and spiritual felicity were explained; however, putting them into practice is not possible except under the shadow of Taqwa (piety/God-wariness). Therefore, in this verse, reference is made to the importance of Taqwa and its effects on human destiny. In this verse, four results and fruits are described for Taqwa and piety.

First, It says: "O you who have believed, if you fear Allah and avoid opposing His command, He will grant you a special radiance and insight so that you can distinguish truth from falsehood well" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقَانًا).

"Furqan" is an intensive form derived from the root "farq" (separation), and here it means something that clearly separates truth from falsehood.

This short yet profound sentence expresses one of the most important and destiny-shaping issues for mankind: that on the path toward victories, there are always precipices and detours. If a person does not see and recognize them well and does not avoid them, he will fall so severely that no trace of him remains. On this path, the most important issue is the recognition of truth and falsehood, the recognition of good and evil, the recognition of friend and foe, the recognition of the beneficial and the harmful, and the recognition of the factors of happiness or misery. If a human truly recognizes these realities well, reaching the destination becomes easy for him.

The problem is that in many such cases, man falls into error; he perceives falsehood as truth, chooses an enemy instead of a friend, and mistakes a detour for the highway.

Here, a powerful vision and understanding are required, along with extraordinary radiance and insight.

The above verse states: This vision and understanding are the fruit of the tree of Taqwa. But how Taqwa and the avoidance of sin and rebellious whims and desires grant a person such vision and understanding might be ambiguous to some; however, a little reflection clarifies the link between the two.

To explain: Firstly, the power of human intellect is sufficiently prepared to perceive realities, but veils of greed, avarice, lust, vanity, envy, and excessive love for wealth, spouse, children, and status appear like black smoke before the eye of the intellect, or cover the surrounding space like thick dust. It is evident that in such a dark environment, man cannot behold the face of truth and falsehood. But if this dust is washed away with the water of Taqwa and this black, dark smoke is removed, seeing the face of truth becomes easy.

As the poet says: The beauty of the Friend has no veil or curtain, But settle the dust of the road so that you may behold.

Or as another poet says: Truth is a furnished palace, Whims and desires are the rising dust. Do you not see that wherever dust rises, The eye sees not, though the man be sighted!

Secondly, we know that every perfection existing anywhere is a reflection of the Perfection of the Truth (Allah). The closer a person gets to Allah, the more powerful a reflection of that Absolute Perfection will be manifested in his being. Based on this, all knowledge and sciences originate from His knowledge. Whenever a person draws closer to Him in the light of Taqwa and avoidance of sin and whims, and joins the drop of his existence to the boundless ocean of His Being, he will receive a greater share of that knowledge.

In other words, the human heart is like a mirror and the existence of the Lord is like the world-illuminating sun. If this mirror is darkened and obscured by the rust of whims and desires, no light will be reflected in it. But when it is polished in the light of Taqwa and piety and the rust is removed, the dazzling light of that brilliant sun will reflect in it and illuminate everywhere.

Therefore, throughout history, we observe insights in the lives of pious men and women that are never perceivable through ordinary science and learning. They recognized many events whose roots were unknown amidst social turmoils, and they saw the loathsome faces of the enemies of truth from behind thousands of deceptive veils!

This extraordinary effect of Taqwa on the true recognition, vision, and understanding of humans has also appeared in many other narrations and verses. In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 282: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ يُعَلِّمُكُمُ اللَّهُ: "Be wary of Allah, and Allah shall teach you." And in the famous Hadith, it is stated: "The believer looks with the light of Allah." And in Nahj al-Balagha, in the short sayings, we read: "The falling of intellects occurs mostly under the lightning of greeds," which blinds the eye of the intellect so that it does not see the precipices and stumbling blocks.

Thirdly, from the perspective of rational analysis, the link between Taqwa and the perception of realities is also understandable. For example, in societies that revolve around whims and desires, where their propaganda apparatuses step toward fanning these very desires—newspapers promote corruption, radios become loudspeakers for pollution and deviations, and televisions are at the service of lust—it is obvious that in such a society, distinguishing truth from falsehood and good from evil is very difficult for most people. Therefore, that lack of Taqwa is the source of this lack of discernment or misjudgment.

Or, for instance, in a family where there is no Taqwa and children are raised in a polluted environment, becoming accustomed to corruption and licentiousness from childhood, it becomes difficult for them to distinguish virtues from vices when they grow up. Fundamentally, the employment of forces and energies and the wasting of these capitals in the way of sin causes people to remain at a low level of understanding and information and to possess debased thoughts, even if they advance in industry and material life.

Therefore, we clearly see that every lack of Taqwa is the source of a kind of ignorance or misjudgment. For this reason, in today's mechanized world, we observe societies that are very advanced in terms of science and industry, yet in their daily lives, they are so entangled in disorders and terrifying contradictions that it leaves one in wonder. All of this clarifies the greatness of this Quranic statement.

And considering that Taqwa is not limited to practical Taqwa but includes intellectual and rational Taqwa, this reality becomes even more apparent. Intellectual Taqwa, as opposed to intellectual licentiousness, means that in our studies, we seek authentic documents and original content, and we do not express an opinion on any issue without sufficient research and necessary precision. Those who practice intellectual Taqwa undoubtedly reach correct results much more easily than the licentious, while those who are licentious in their choice of documents and style of reasoning have an extraordinary number of errors.

However, an important point that must be seriously noted—and which, like many other constructive Islamic concepts, has fallen prey to distortion among us Muslims—is that many people imagine a pious person is someone who frequently washes his body and clothes with water and considers everyone and everything impure or suspicious, withdraws into isolation in social matters, does not lift a finger, and chooses silence in the face of every issue. Such false interpretations of Taqwa and piety are, in fact, considered among the factors of the decline of Islamic societies. Such Taqwa creates neither awareness nor insight, nor Furqan (the criterion for separating truth from falsehood)!

Now that the first reward of the pious has become clear, we proceed to the commentary on the rest of the verse and their other three rewards.

The Quran says: In addition to distinguishing truth from falsehood, the result of piety is that "Allah will expiate your sins and remove their effects from your being" (وَ يُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ). Furthermore, "He will include you in His forgiveness" (وَ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ) and many other rewards await you that none but Allah knows, for "Allah is the Possessor of Great Bounty" (وَ اللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ).

These four effects are the fruits of the tree of Taqwa and piety. The existence of a natural relationship between Taqwa and some of these effects does not prevent us from attributing all of them to Allah, because we have repeatedly stated in this Tafsir that every effect any being possesses is by the will of Allah; hence, that effect can be attributed both to Allah and to that being.

Regarding the difference between "Takfir al-Sayyi'at" (expiation of misdeeds) and "Ghufran" (forgiveness), some commentators believe that the first refers to covering them up in this world and the second refers to deliverance from punishment in the Hereafter.

However, another possibility exists here: that "Takfir al-Sayyi'at" refers to the psychological and social effects of sins which are removed in the light of Taqwa and piety, while "Ghufran" refers to the issue of Allah's pardon and forgiveness and deliverance from punishment.

Ayah

وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِيُثْبِتُوكَ أَوْ يَقْتُلُوكَ أَوْ يُخْرِجُوكَ ۚ وَيَمْكُرُونَ وَيَمْكُرُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ (٣٠)
When the faithless plotted against you to take you captive, or to kill or expel you—they plotted and Allah devised, and Allah is the best of devisers. (30)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators and traditionists consider the above verse to refer to the events that led to the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله migration (Hijrah) from Mecca to Medina. These events, narrated with various expressions, all pursue a single truth: that Allah miraculously rescued the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from the trap of a great and certain danger. According to the narration in Al-Durr al-Manthur, the flow of the event is as follows:

A group of the Quraysh and the nobles of Mecca from various tribes gathered to meet at "Dar al-Nadwa" (the assembly place for the consultative meetings of the Meccan elders) to deliberate on the danger threatening them from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

(It is said) that along the way, they encountered a pleasant-looking old man who was, in reality, Satan himself (or a human possessed of a satanic spirit and mind). They asked him, "Who are you?" He replied, "I am an old man from the people of Najd. Since I became aware of your decision, I wished to attend your assembly and not withhold my opinion and goodwill from you." They said, "Very well, enter!" So, he entered Dar al-Nadwa along with them.

One of those present turned to the crowd and said, "You must think of a plan regarding this man (referring to the Prophet of Islam), for by God, it is feared that he will prevail over you (and dismantle your creed and grandeur)."

One person suggested, "Imprison him until he perishes in jail..."

The old man from Najd rejected this view and said, "It is feared that his supporters will rush in and, at a suitable opportunity, free him from prison and take him out of this land. You must think of a more fundamental plan."

Another said, "Expel him from among yourselves so that you may be rid of him; for once he leaves your midst, whatever he does will not harm you, and his dealings will be with others."

The old man from Najd said, "By God, this is also not a sound opinion. Do you not see the sweetness of his speech, the eloquence of his tongue, and his influence over hearts? If you do this, he will go to the other Arabs, and they will gather around him. Then he will return to you with a vast multitude, drive you out of your cities, and kill your nobles!" The crowd said, "By God, he speaks the truth; think of another plan."

Abu Jahl, who had been silent until then, spoke up and said, "I have an opinion that I consider none other to be correct!" They asked, "What is it?" He said, "We shall select a brave, sword-wielding youth from every tribe and give each a sharp sword so that they may attack him collectively at a suitable opportunity. When you kill him in this manner, his blood will be distributed among all the tribes, and I do not believe the clan of Bani Hashim will be able to fight all the clans of Quraysh. In that case, they will surely be satisfied with blood money, and we will be relieved of his trouble."

The old man from Najd said (with joy), "By God, the correct view is exactly what this young man said; I hold no other opinion but that." (Thus, this proposal was unanimously accepted), and they dispersed with this decision.

Gabriel descended and commanded the Prophet not to sleep in his bed that night.

The Prophet set out by night toward the cave (Thawr) [^1] and instructed Ali عليه السلام to sleep in his bed (so that those watching the bed through the cracks in the door would imagine him to be in his bed, granting him time until morning to move away from the danger zone).

When morning came and they rushed into the house and searched, they found Ali عليه السلام in the Prophet's bed, and thus Allah brought their plans to naught. They cried out, "Where then is Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله?" He replied, "I do not know." They set out following the Prophet's footprints until they reached the mountain and came near the cave, but (to their surprise, they saw a spider's web visible across the entrance of the cave. They said to one another, "If he were in this cave, there would be no trace of these spider webs at the entrance," and so they turned back).

The Prophet remained in the cave for three days (and when the enemies had searched all the deserts of Mecca and returned tired and hopeless, he set out toward Medina). [^2]

Commentary

The Beginning of the Migration

Some believe that this verse and the five verses following it were revealed in Mecca, because they refer to the event of the Prophet's "Migration." However, the style of the verse's expression testifies that it was revealed after the Migration, as it is in the form of recounting a past event. Therefore, although the verse refers to the process of Migration, it was certainly revealed in Medina and recounts a great memory and a magnificent blessing of the Lord upon the Prophet and the Muslims. It first says:

"And remember when those who disbelieved plotted against you to imprison you, or to kill you, or to expel you" (وَ إِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِيُثْبِتُوكَ أَوْ يَقْتُلُوكَ أَوْ يُخْرِجُوكَ).

The word "Makr"—as we have mentioned before—in the Arabic language means planning, resourcefulness, and designing a scheme, not the common meaning it holds in modern Persian [deception]. Similarly, "Hilah" in the language means finding a solution, though in modern Persian it is used to mean harmful secret plots.

Then it adds: "They were plotting and planning, and Allah also planned, and Allah is the best of planners" (وَ يَمْكُرُونَ وَ يَمْكُرُ اللَّهُ وَ اللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ).

If we reflect deeply on the event of the Prophet's Migration, we encounter the point that they employed their maximum intellectual and physical strength to destroy the Prophet of Islam. Even after the Prophet escaped their clutches, they set a reward of one hundred camels—which was a vast fortune in those days—for finding him. Many individuals, out of religious fanaticism or to obtain this great prize, scoured the mountains and deserts around Mecca, even coming to the very mouth of the cave. Yet, Allah, through a very simple and small means—a few spider webs—brought all these designs to naught. Considering that the issue of Migration was the beginning of a new stage in the history of Islam, and indeed the history of humanity, we conclude that Allah changed the course of human history by means of a few spider webs!

This is not unique to the event of Migration; rather, the history of the Prophets shows that Allah has always used the simplest of means to crush the tyrants—sometimes by the blowing of a wind, sometimes by a swarm of gnats, sometimes by the small Ababil birds, and the like—to manifest the weakness and helplessness of man before His infinite power and to deter him from the thought of rebellion and transgression.

It is also noteworthy that resorting to these three things: "imprisonment," "exile," and "killing" was not exclusive to the polytheists of Mecca against the Prophet. Rather, tyrants have always resorted to one of these three to silence the tongues of reformers and eliminate their influence among the suffering masses of society. However, just as the action of the Meccan polytheists regarding the Prophet yielded the opposite result and became the prelude to a new movement and momentum in Islam, such severities in other cases have also usually yielded the opposite result. Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالُوا۟ قَدْ سَمِعْنَا لَوْ نَشَآءُ لَقُلْنَا مِثْلَ هَـٰذَآ ۙ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣١)
When Our signs are recited to them, they say, ‘We have heard already. If we want, we [too] can say like this. These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (31)
وَإِذْ قَالُوا۟ ٱللَّهُمَّ إِن كَانَ هَـٰذَا هُوَ ٱلْحَقَّ مِنْ عِندِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنَا حِجَارَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَوِ ٱئْتِنَا بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣٢)
And when they said, ‘O Allah, if this be the truth from You, rain down upon us stones from the sky, or bring us a painful punishment.’ (32)
وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنتَ فِيهِمْ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (٣٣)
But Allah will not punish them while you are in their midst, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness. (33)
وَمَا لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٤)
What [excuse] have they that Allah should not punish them, when they bar [the faithful] from the Holy Mosque, and they are not its custodians? Its custodians are only the Godwary, but most of them do not know. (34)
وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكَآءً وَتَصْدِيَةً ۚ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٥)
Their prayer at the House is nothing but whistling and clapping. So taste the punishment because of what you used to defy. (35)

Commentary

The Vain Talkers

In the previous verse, an example of the "practical logic" of the superstitious polytheists of Mecca was presented. In the verses under discussion, an example of their "intellectual logic" is reflected, so it may become clear that they possessed neither soundness of thought nor correctness of action; rather, all their programs were baseless and foolish!

In the first verse, it says: "And when Our verses are recited to them, they say, 'We have heard [this]; if we willed, we could say [something] like this'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا قالُوا قَدْ سَمِعْنا لَوْ نَشاءُ لَقُلْنا مِثْلَ هذا).

"These do not contain any significant content; they are nothing but the fables of the ancients!" (إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

They said this while they had repeatedly attempted to challenge the Quran and had proven utterly incapable of doing so. They knew full well that they lacked the power to oppose the Quran, but out of prejudice and malice, or to deceive the people, they would say, "These verses are not important; we too can produce the like of them." Yet, they never produced anything. This was one of their flawed logics—using hollow and baseless claims, like all tyrants of history, to try and maintain their palace of power for a few more days.

In the following verse, it recounts another strange logic, saying: "(And remember) when (they would raise their hands in prayer and) say, 'O Allah, if this (religion and this Quran) is indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky!'" (وَ إِذْ قالُوا اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كانَ هذا هُوَ الْحَقَّ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ فَأَمْطِرْ عَلَيْنا حِجارَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ). "Or bring us a painful punishment (of another kind)" (أَوْ ائْتِنا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ‌).

They said this because, due to the intensity of their prejudice and stubbornness, they imagined the religion of Islam to be 100% baseless; otherwise, someone who considers even a possibility of its truthfulness would not invoke such a curse upon themselves. There is also the possibility that the leaders of the polytheists said such things to deceive the people, showing simple-minded individuals that the religion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله was entirely false, while in their hearts, it was not so.

It seems the polytheists intended to imply: "You say regarding the previous prophets that Allah punished their enemies sometimes with a rain of stones (like the people of Lot); if you are truthful, then do the same to us!" In Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "After the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله appointed Ali عليه السلام as his successor at Ghadir Khumm and said:

'Whomsoever I am his master (Mawla),

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 7, p. 153

then Ali is his master,'

this matter spread everywhere. Nu'man ibn Harith al-Fahri (who was among the hypocrites) came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: 'You told us to bear witness to Monotheism and the rejection of idols, and to testify to your messengership; you commanded us to Jihad, Hajj, fasting, prayer, and Zakat—we accepted all of them. But you were not satisfied with this and appointed this youth (meaning Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام!) as successor and said: "Whomsoever I am his master, then Ali is his master." Is this word from you or a command from Allah?'

The Prophet said: 'By Allah, besides whom there is no deity, it is from Allah.' Nu'man turned away while saying: 'O Allah, if this is indeed the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky.' Not much time passed before a stone fell upon him and he was killed." [^1] This hadith does not contradict the fact that the verse was revealed before the event of Ghadir, because the occasion of the verse's revelation was not the incident of Nu'man; rather, Nu'man, in his curse, quoted a verse that had been revealed previously. This is similar to how we quote the Quran in our prayers and say: "Our Lord, give us in this world [that which is] good and in the Hereafter [that which is] good" (رَبَّنا آتِنا فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً). (Further explanation regarding the above hadith and the numerous sources we have for it from the books of the Ahl al-Sunnah will come, Allah willing, under the verse "A solicitor asked for a punishment bound to happen" at the beginning of Surah al-Ma'arij).

In the preceding verses, two objections were raised by the opponents against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The falsehood of one was so obvious that the Quran did not bother to answer it, which was their claim: "If we willed, we could produce the like of the Quran." This was certainly a false and hollow claim, for if they could have, they surely would have; therefore, it required no

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 151.

answer.

Their second objection was that if these verses are true and from God, then let Him punish us and send down a calamity upon us. The Quran answers them in the third verse under discussion as follows:

"But Allah would not punish them while you are among them" (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَ أَنْتَ فِيهِمْ‌).

In reality, your blessed existence, as you are a "Mercy to the worlds," prevents calamity from descending upon these sinners, sparing them from being destroyed like past nations who perished collectively or individually through various means.

Then it adds: "Nor will Allah punish them while they seek forgiveness" (and ask Him for pardon) (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ‌).

Commentators have offered several possibilities regarding the interpretation of this sentence. Some have said it means that some of the polytheists, after saying the sentence in the previous verse, regretted their words and pleaded: Ghufranaka Rabbana—"O Lord, forgive us for this statement"—and this was the reason they were not afflicted with calamity and destruction even after the Prophet's departure from Mecca.

Another group has said this sentence refers to the remaining believers in Mecca; for after the Prophet's migration, a group who were unable to migrate remained in Mecca, and their presence—which was a reflection of the Prophet's presence—prevented the descent of punishment upon the polytheists of Mecca.

It is also possible that this sentence carries the meaning of a conditional statement; meaning, if they regret their actions, turn to God, and seek forgiveness, the divine punishment will be lifted from them.

At the same time, combining these possibilities in the interpretation of the verse is not far-fetched; that is, the verse may refer to all of them.

In any case, the concept of the verse is not exclusive to the people of the Prophet's era; rather, it is a general law for all people. Thus, in a famous hadith narrated in Shi'a sources from Imam Ali عليه السلام and in Sunni sources from Ali's student, Ibn Abbas, we read:

"There were two means of security from Allah's punishment on earth. One of them (the presence of the Prophet) has been taken away. So, hold fast to the other one (seeking forgiveness)." Then he recited the above verse. [^1] From the above verse and this hadith, it becomes clear that the presence of Prophets is an effective means for the security of people against severe and heavy calamities, and after that, seeking forgiveness, repentance, and turning toward the Truth is another factor.

However, if the second factor is also removed, human societies will have no immunity against the painful punishments that await them because of their sins. These punishments manifest in the form of painful natural disasters, devastating and destructive wars, or other forms, just as we have seen or heard of various types until now.

In the Supplication of Kumayl, narrated from Imam Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"O Allah, forgive me the sins that bring down calamities." This expression shows that if there is no seeking of forgiveness, many sins can become the source of the descent of calamities.

It is also necessary to mention that the meaning of seeking forgiveness (Istighfar) is not merely saying and repeating the phrase

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings.

"O God, forgive me" or "Allahumma-ghfir li," but rather the spirit of Istighfar is a state of returning toward the Truth and a readiness to compensate for the past.

In the next verse, it says that they deserve divine punishment: "But what [plea] have they that Allah should not punish them while they obstruct [people] from the Sacred Mosque?" (وَ ما لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ هُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ‌). This refers to the time when the Muslims were in Mecca and did not have the right to freely establish congregational prayer beside the House of Allah, facing various harassments and tortures; or it refers to the obstructions they later created against the believers performing the Hajj and Umrah rituals.

Strangely, these defiled polytheists considered themselves the masters and guardians of this great center of worship, but the Quran adds: "And they were not its [rightful] guardians" (وَ ما كانُوا أَوْلِياءَهُ‌).

Even though they considered themselves the trustees and authorities of the House of Allah, "Its [rightful] guardians are none but the righteous (the God-fearing)" (إِنْ أَوْلِياؤُهُ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقُونَ‌).

"But most of them do not know [this reality]" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Although this ruling is stated regarding the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram), it actually encompasses all religious centers, mosques, and religious institutions. Their "trustees" and "administrators" must be among the purest, most pious, and most active people, who keep these centers clean, vibrant, and as hubs for education, training, awakening, and awareness—not a handful of filthy, dependent, sell-out, and corrupt individuals who turn these centers into "commercial stalls," "centers for numbing thoughts," and "alienation from the Truth." In our opinion, if

Muslims had implemented this very Islamic instruction regarding mosques and religious centers, Islamic societies would have a different form today.

Even stranger was that they claimed to have a prayer and worship, and they gave the name of "prayer" to their foolish acts—shouting and clapping around the House of Allah. Therefore, the Quran adds: "And their prayer at the House [of Allah] was nothing but whistling and hand-clapping" (وَ ما كانَ صَلاتُهُمْ عِنْدَ الْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكاءً وَ تَصْدِيَةً). We read in history that a group of Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) would strip stark naked while circumambulating the Kaaba, whistling and clapping, and calling it worship. It is also narrated that when the Prophet stood beside the Black Stone facing north (so that he would be facing both the Kaaba and Jerusalem!) and engaged in prayer, two men from the tribe of "Bani Sahm" would stand to his right and left; one would "shriek" and the other would "clap" to disturb the Prophet's prayer.

Following this sentence, it says: Now that all your actions, even your prayer and worship, are so foolish, ugly, and shameful, you deserve punishment: "So taste the punishment for what you used to disbelieve" (فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ‌).

When one flips through the pages of the history of the pre-Islamic Arabs and examines the parts mentioned in the Quran, one sees with great surprise that in our era—the so-called age of space and the atom—there are those who, by repeating the acts of the Age of Ignorance, consider themselves among the ranks of worshippers. They accompany Quranic verses and sometimes poems in praise of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and Ali عليه السلام with musical melodies, and perform movements similar to dancing with their heads, necks, and hands, and they call it

the honoring of these sanctities. These acts are sometimes performed under the name of Wajd (ecstasy) and Sama', sometimes as Dhikr and Hal, and sometimes under other names in Sufi lodges (Khanaqahs) and elsewhere.

While Islam is averse to all these things, and these acts are another example of Ignorant (Jahili) practices.

One question remains here: in the third verse under discussion, punishment and torment were negated for them (of course, with two conditions), but in the fourth verse, punishment is affirmed. Is there a contradiction between the two?

The answer is that the first verse refers to worldly punishments [that wipe out a nation], and the second verse may refer to punishments in the next world, or it may indicate that this group deserves punishment in this very world and the grounds for it are prepared for them; if the Prophet departs and they do not repent, it will overtake them.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ لِيَصُدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَسَيُنفِقُونَهَا ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ (٣٦)
Indeed the faithless spend their wealth to bar from the way of Allah. Soon they will have spent it, then it will be a cause of regret to them, then they will be overcome, and the faithless will be gathered toward Hell, (36)
لِيَمِيزَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُۥ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُۥ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُۥ فِى جَهَنَّمَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٣٧)
so that Allah may separate the bad ones from the good, and place the bad on one another, and pile them up together, and cast them into hell. It is they who are the losers. (37)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim and many other commentaries, it is stated that the above verse was revealed regarding the Battle of Badr and the financial contributions of the people of Mecca. When the polytheists of Mecca were informed of the situation by Abu Sufyan's messenger, they gathered vast amounts of wealth to assist their warriors. However, they were ultimately defeated and killed, hastening toward the fire of Hell, and what they had spent in this path became a source of regret and sorrow for them.

Of course, the first verse also refers to their other contributions in their struggles against Islam, and the issue is presented in a general manner.

Some have also said that the verse was revealed regarding Abu Sufyan's contributions to hire two thousand mercenaries for the Battle of Uhud. However, since these verses are positioned among those related to the Battle of Badr, the first occasion of revelation seems more accurate.

Commentary

Whatever the occasion of revelation may be, the concept of the verse is a comprehensive one that encompasses all financial contributions made by the enemies of truth and justice to advance their sinister goals.

First, It says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve spend their wealth to avert [people] from the way of Allah" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ لِيَصُدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ). But this spending of wealth will never bring them victory: "They will spend it; then it will be for them a regret" (فَسَيُنْفِقُونَها ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً).

"Then they will be overcome" by the supporters of truth (ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ).

Not only do they suffer regret and defeat in the life of this world, but in the other abode, these "disbelievers will be gathered into Hell" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ).

Here, several points should be noted

  1. It is understood from this verse that even before being defeated, they realize the futility of their actions. Because they do not see sufficient results for the wealth they have spent, they are afflicted with pain and sorrow, and this is one of their punishments in this world.

Their other punishment is the failure of their plans, for hired individuals, mercenaries, and those who fight for the love of wealth and property can never stand against people of faith who fight for a sacred goal.

The events of our contemporary world have also repeatedly shown that powerful governments that encouraged their soldiers to war with money and means of lust were ignominiously defeated by small nations fighting out of faith.

In addition to these two worldly punishments, they face a third punishment in the other world, which is being caught in the fire of Divine wrath and anger.

  1. What is mentioned in the above verse has many examples in our world today. The demonic forces of colonialism, the supporters of oppression, corruption, and tyranny, and the proponents of superstitious and false religions spend massive fortunes to advance their goals and bar humans from the path of truth in various forms. Sometimes in the guise of mercenaries of war, sometimes in the form of seemingly humanitarian aid—such as building hospitals and clinics—and at other times in the form of cultural aid and the like. However, the nature and ultimate goal of all are the same: the expansion of colonialism and oppression. If true believers, like the warriors of Badr, maintain organized and highly resistant ranks, they can thwart all these plans and leave the regret of these fortunes in their hearts, and ultimately send their collective to Hell!
  1. Some commentators have said that this verse is one of the signs of the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, as it foretells future events—the defeat of the enemies of Islam. Even if we do not consider the verse as news of the unseen regarding future events, it is at least a sign of the precise and calculated content of the Quran regarding the struggle between truth and falsehood, and it clarifies the greatness of the Quran and Islamic teachings.

After the three grim consequences of the financial spending of the enemies of truth were mentioned in the previous verse, the next verse states: This regret, defeat, and misery is "so that Allah may distinguish the wicked from the good" in this world and the next (لِيَمِيزَ اللَّهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ).

This is a Divine Sunnah (law) that forever ensures "pure" and "impure," "sincere" and "hypocrite," "true mujahid" and "false one," "Divine deeds" and "Satanic deeds," "humanitarian programs" and "anti-human programs" do not remain unrecognized. Rather, eventually, the ranks will be distinguished from one another, and the truth will manifest its radiance. Of course, this is provided that its supporters possess sufficient awareness and self-sacrifice, like the true Muslims of "Badr."

Then He adds: "And [so that He may] place the wicked some of them upon others and heap them all together and put them into Hell" (وَ يَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ).

The wicked and impure from every group and in every form or guise will ultimately have a single form, and the end of all of them will be loss and ruin, as the Quran says: "It is those who are the losers" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ).

Ayahs

قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَنتَهُوا۟ يُغْفَرْ لَهُم مَّا قَدْ سَلَفَ وَإِن يَعُودُوا۟ فَقَدْ مَضَتْ سُنَّتُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٨)
Say to the faithless, if they cease [their belligerence against the Muslims], what is already past shall be forgiven them. But if they resume [their hostilities], then the precedent of the predecessors has already come to pass. (38)
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ كُلُّهُۥ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٣٩)
Fight them until persecution is no more, and religion becomes exclusively for Allah. So if they desist, Allah indeed watches what they do. (39)
وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ (٤٠)
But if they turn away, then know that Allah is your master: an excellent master and an excellent helper! (40)

Commentary

We know that the method of the Quran is to combine glad tidings with warnings; that is, just as It threatens the enemies of truth with severe and painful punishments, It also leaves the door of return open to them.

The first verse of the verses under discussion follows this very method, instructing the Prophet: "Tell those who have disbelieved: if they cease [their opposition, stubbornness, and rebellion] and return to the religion of truth, their past will be forgiven" (قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَنْتَهُوا يُغْفَرْ لَهُمْ ما قَدْ سَلَفَ‌).

From this verse, it is understood that by accepting Islam, the past—whatever it may be—is forgiven. This is the same principle mentioned in Islamic narrations as a general law. Sometimes it is expressed as:

"الاسلام يجب ما قبله‌" "Islam covers [veils] what preceded it."

Or in another expression narrated from the Prophet through Sunni sources:

"ان الاسلام يهدم ما كان قبله، و ان الهجرة تهدم ما كان قبلها و ان الحج يهدم ما كان قبله‌" "Indeed, Islam destroys what was before it, and migration (Hijrah) destroys what was before it, and the Hajj to the House of Allah erases what was before it." [^1]

The meaning is that sinful acts and even the omission of religious obligations and duties performed before Islam will be nullified due to the acceptance of Islam. This law does not apply retroactively; hence, we read in Islamic jurisprudence books that a person who becomes a Muslim is not even required to make up (perform qada) for past acts of worship.

Then it adds that if they do not cease their incorrect ways "and return to their former actions, the Sunnah (way) of Allah regarding the ancients will be carried out upon them" (وَ إِنْ يَعُودُوا فَقَدْ مَضَتْ سُنَّتُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

What is meant by this "Sunnah" is the same fate that the enemies of truth faced against the Prophets, and even the pagans of Mecca themselves faced against the Prophet at the Battle of Badr. In Surah Ghafir, verse 51, we read: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ: "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand." Similarly, in Surah Al-Isra, verse 77, after mentioning the collapse of the lives of the enemies of Islam, we read: سُنَّةَ مَنْ قَدْ أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا وَ لا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنا تَحْوِيلًا: "[This is] the Sunnah of those We sent before you of Our messengers; and you will not find in Our Sunnah any alteration."

Since the previous verse invited the enemies to return to the truth, this invitation might have created the thought for Muslims that the era of Jihad had ended and there was no path forward except flexibility and leniency. To remove this misconception, it adds: "Fight these stubborn enemies and continue the battle until fitnah (persecution/strife) is removed and the religion is entirely for Allah" (وَ قاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَ يَكُونَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ لِلَّهِ‌).

"Fitnah," as we explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 193, has a broad meaning that includes any form of pressure. Therefore, sometimes in the Quran, the word fitnah is used to mean polytheism and idolatry, which involve various types of restrictions and pressures for society. Likewise, the pressures exerted by enemies to prevent the spread of the call to Islam, to stifle the voice of truth-seekers, and even to force believers back toward disbelief, are also called "fitnah."

In the above verse, some commentators have taken fitnah to mean polytheism, and others to mean the efforts of enemies to deprive Muslims of intellectual and social freedom. However, the truth is that the concept of the verse is broad, encompassing both "polytheism" (by the evidence of the phrase "and the religion be entirely for Allah") and other pressures imposed by enemies upon Muslims.

The Goal of Jihad and a Glad Tiding

The above verse points to two parts of the sacred goals of Islamic Jihad:

1- Dismantling the apparatus of idolatry and destroying idol temples. As we have stated in the discussion on the goals of Jihad, religious freedom is specific to those who follow one of the heavenly religions, and applying pressure on them to change their belief is not correct. However, idolatry is neither a religion nor a school of thought; rather, it is superstition and deviation. The Islamic government must first through preaching, and if not possible, through the use of force, dismantle the apparatus of idolatry everywhere and destroy the idol temples.

2- Obtaining freedom of expression, preaching, and the dissemination of Islam. In this part as well, Islam permits that if some people, through their actions, prevent the freedom of Muslims in disseminating, preaching, and inviting to Islam, they have the right to resort to a liberating Jihad to open the way for logical preaching (for more explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 2, pages 15 to 18).

In Sunni commentaries such as Tafsir al-Alusi (Ruh al-Ma'ani) and various Shia commentaries, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"لم يجي‌ء تاويل هذه الاية و لو قام قائمنا بعد سيرى من يدركه ما يكون من تاويل هذه الاية و ليبلغن دين محمد ما بلغ الليل حتى لا يكون مشرك على ظهر الارض‌"

"The final interpretation and realization (ta'wil) of this verse has not yet arrived. When our Qa'im rises, those who live in his time will see the realization of this verse. By Allah, at that time, the religion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله will reach every point where the night casts its soothing cover, until no polytheist or idolater remains on the face of the earth." [^2]

The author of Tafsir al-Manar, due to a specific prejudice he has regarding the issue of the uprising of Hazrat Mahdi عليه السلام, has denied this hadith. This is because of the incorrect preconception he holds regarding the uprising of Hazrat Mahdi. It is strange that, according to the explicit statements in his commentary, he has a particular inclination toward the "Wahhabi" school, while even the strict Wahhabis consider the appearance of Hazrat Mahdi عليه السلام a certainty and the narrations regarding it to be mutawatir (frequently narrated). The documents and evidence for this will be provided under verse 33 of Surah At-Tawbah (in this same volume), where we will also point out the main error of the aforementioned commentator and the response to it. We have also provided more detailed information in the book The Great Global Reformer (Mosleh-e Bozorg-e Jahani). If some narrations related to the appearance of Hazrat Mahdi عليه السلام are incorrect or contain superstitions, it does not justify ignoring all these authentic and mutawatir narrations.

At the end of the verse, in the face of the severity of action, It once again extends the hand of friendship and love toward them, saying: "If they cease [their ways], indeed Allah is Seeing of what they do, and He will treat them with His special grace and mercy" (فَإِنِ انْتَهَوْا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

"But if they continue their defiance and do not submit to the call of truth, then know that victory is for you and defeat awaits them, for Allah is your Protector and Master" (وَ إِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ‌ موليكم).

"He is the best Master and Leader, and the best Helper and Supporter" (نِعْمَ الْمَوْلى‌ وَ نِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ).

Beginning of the Tenth Juz'
