Ayah

۞ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا غَنِمْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُۥ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْفُرْقَانِ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤١)
Know that whatever thing you may come by, a fifth of it is for Allah and the Apostle, for the relatives and the orphans, for the needy and the traveller, if you have faith in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Separation, the day when the two hosts met; and Allah has power over all things. (41)

Commentary

Khums, an Important Islamic Decree

At the beginning of this Surah, we saw that some Muslims disputed over the distribution of war spoils after the Battle of Badr. To uproot the cause of conflict, Allah placed the spoils entirely at the disposal of the Prophet to utilize as He saw fit, and the Prophet distributed them equally among the combatants.

This verse is, in fact, a return to the issue of spoils, fitting with the verses regarding Jihad mentioned previously. Since Jihad is often intertwined with the matter of spoils, it is appropriate to mention the ruling on spoils (rather, as we shall explain, the Quran here has extended the ruling of Khums beyond war spoils to include all forms of income). At the beginning of the verse, He says: "And know that whatever of a thing you acquire as booty, a fifth of it is for Allah, and for the Messenger, and for the near of kin (the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt), and the orphans, and the needy, and the wayfarer (from the family of the Prophet)" (وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَ لِلرَّسُولِ وَ لِذِي الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينِ وَ ابْنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

For emphasis, He adds: "If you have believed in Allah and that which We sent down upon Our servant on the Day of Criterion (the day of the Battle of Badr)—the day the two hosts met (the believers and the disbelievers)—then you must act upon this decree and submit to it" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللَّهِ وَ ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى‌ عَبْدِنا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ‌).

Here, it is necessary to note this point: although the address in this verse is to the believers—since it discusses the spoils of Islamic Jihad and it is clear that an Islamic mujahid is a believer—it nevertheless says: "If you have believed in Allah and the Prophet." This indicates that merely claiming faith is not a sign of true faith; even participating in the field of Jihad may not be a sign of complete faith, as it might be done for other objectives. A complete believer is one who submits to all decrees, especially financial ones, and does not practice discrimination among Divine programs.

At the end of the verse, referring to the unlimited Divine power, He says: "And Allah is over all things competent" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

This means that although you were in the minority in every respect at the field of Badr, and the enemy apparently had a significant superiority in every way, the All-Powerful Allah defeated them and aided you until you were victorious.

Here, several points should be noted

1- The Day of Separation between Truth and Falsehood

The name of the day of the Battle of "Badr" is mentioned in this verse as the Day of Criterion (يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ‌) and the day of the clash between the supporters of disbelief and the supporters of faith. This indicates that, firstly, the historical day of Badr was the day when the signs of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله truthfulness became manifest; for He had previously promised victory to the Muslims while there was apparently no sign of it. Various unexpected factors combined for victory in a way that could not be attributed to chance. Therefore, the truth of the verses revealed to this Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله on such a day contains its own proof.

Secondly, the clash at Badr (يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ‌) was actually a great Divine blessing for the Muslims, which some initially avoided. However, this very clash and victory propelled them forward by years, and their name and fame spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula, forcing everyone to contemplate the new religion and the astonishing power born under its shadow in Arabia. Furthermore, on that day, which was a day of "every man for himself" for the small community of Islam, the true believers were distinguished from the false claimants. Thus, this day was, in every respect, the day of separating truth from falsehood.

2- There is no contradiction between the verse of Anfal and this verse

As we stated at the beginning of the Surah, there is no contradiction between the verse of Anfal and this verse, and it is not necessary to consider one as abrogating the other. According to the verse of "Anfal," war spoils also belong to the Prophet. However, the Prophet grants four-fifths of it to the combatants and retains one-fifth for the purposes specified in the verse (for further explanation, refer to our discussion under the first verse of this Surah).

3- What is meant by "Dhul-Qurba" (Near of Kin)?

The term "Dhul-Qurba" in this verse does not refer to all relatives, nor all relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but rather the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. The evidence for this is the mutawatir (frequently narrated) traditions reported through the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [1] There are also indications of this in the books of the Ahl al-Sunnah.

Therefore, those who consider one share of the Khums to belong to all relatives of the Prophet are faced with the question: what kind of privilege is this that Islam has granted to the kin and relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, while we know that Islam transcends race, ethnicity, and tribe?

However, if we consider it specific to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام—given that they were and are the successors of the Prophet and the leaders of the Islamic government—the reason for giving this one share of Khums to them becomes clear. In other words, the "share of Allah," the "share of the Prophet," and the "share of Dhul-Qurba" all three belong to the "Leader of the Islamic Government." He manages His simple life from it and spends the rest on the various expenses necessitated by the position of leading the Ummah—meaning, in reality, on the needs of society and the people.

Some commentators of the Ahl al-Sunnah, such as the author of "Al-Manar," because they considered "Dhul-Qurba" to be all relatives, struggled to answer the above objection and attributed a ceremonial nature to the Prophet's Islamic government, claiming He was obliged to keep His tribe and kin around Him through wealth! It is clear that such logic is in no way consistent with the logic of a global and humanitarian government and the elimination of tribal privileges (we have further explanation on this which will come in future discussions).

4- What is meant by "the orphans, the needy, and the wayfarer..."

The terms "orphans," "needy," and "wayfarers" refer only to the orphans, the needy, and the wayfarers of the Bani Hashim and the Sayyids. Although the literal appearance of the verse is absolute and no restriction is seen in it, our evidence for this restriction is the numerous traditions narrated through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام in the commentary of the verse. We know that many rulings appear in the text of the Quran in an absolute form, but their "conditions and restrictions" are explained through the "Sunnah," and this is not unique to the verse under discussion such that it should be surprising.

Furthermore, considering that Zakat is certainly forbidden for the needy of the Bani Hashim, their needs must be met through another channel. This itself becomes a contextual clue that the above verse refers specifically to the needy of the Bani Hashim. Thus, we read in the hadiths that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام used to say: "When Allah forbade Zakat for us, He designated Khums for us; therefore, Zakat is forbidden for us and Khums is permissible." [1]

5- Is "Ghanimah" restricted to war spoils?

Another important subject that must be carefully examined in this verse—and in fact, the major part of the discussion centers on it—is whether the word "Ghanimah" mentioned in the verse includes only war spoils or encompasses every kind of income?

In the first case, the verse only explains the Khums of war spoils, and for Khums in other matters, one must rely on the Sunnah and authentic, reliable reports and traditions. There is no obstacle to the Quran mentioning part of the ruling of Khums in connection with the issues of Jihad, while other parts are explained in the Sunnah.

For example, the five daily prayers are explicitly mentioned in the Holy Quran, as are the prayers of Tawaf (which are among the obligatory prayers), but the Prayer of Signs (Salat al-Ayat), which is agreed upon by all Muslims—both Shia and Sunni—is not mentioned. We find no one saying that because the Prayer of Signs is not mentioned in the Quran and only appears in the Sunnah of the Prophet, it should not be practiced. Or that because the Quran mentions some ritual baths (Ghusl) and not others, the others should be ignored. This is a logic that no Muslim accepts.

Therefore, there is no problem if the Quran explains only a portion of the instances of Khums and leaves the rest to the Sunnah; similar cases are very frequent in Islamic jurisprudence.

However, we must see what "Ghanimah" means linguistically and in the view of common usage (Urf). Is it truly restricted to war spoils, or does it include every kind of income?

What is gathered from lexicons is that the linguistic root of this word does not inherently contain the concept of war or what is taken from an enemy; rather, it includes every income. As evidence, we refer to several parts of famous and well-known lexicons relied upon by scholars and Arab literati:

In the book "Lisan al-Arab," volume 12, we read: "Al-Ghunm is the attaining of a thing without hardship... and al-Ghunm, al-Ghanimah, and al-Maghnam are al-Fay'... and in the hadith: 'The pledge belongs to the one who pledged it; its Ghunm is for him and its Ghurm is upon him.' Its Ghunm is its increase, its growth, and its surplus value... and he 'Ghanima' the thing means he attained it."

"Ghunm means accessing something without hardship, and 'Ghunm,' 'Ghanimah,' and 'Maghnam' mean Fay' (Fay' is also defined in lexicons as things that reach a person without toil...)."

And it is narrated in the hadith that the collateral is in the hands of the one who took it as a pledge; its booty and benefits are for him, and its liability and loss are also directed at him. Also, Ghunm means increase, growth, and additional value, and "he took such a thing as Ghanimah" means he gained access to it. In the book "Taj al-Arus," volume 9, we read: "Al-Ghunm is the attaining of a thing without hardship."

In the book "Al-Qamus," Ghanimah is also mentioned with the same meaning as above.

In the book "Mufradat al-Raghib," we read that Ghanimah is derived from the root "Ghanam" meaning "sheep," and then it says: "Then they used it for everything obtained from the enemy or otherwise." This means: "Then it was used for everything a person acquires from an enemy or a non-enemy." Even those who mention war spoils as one of the meanings of Ghanimah do not deny that its original meaning is a broad one referring to any good that a person attains without hardship.

In common usage, "Ghanimah" is also mentioned in contrast to "Ghuramah" (liability/loss). Just as the meaning of "Ghuramah" is broad and includes any kind of liability, "Ghanimah" also has a broad meaning and is applied to any significant income. This word appears in "Nahj al-Balagha" in many instances with this same meaning. In Sermon 76, we read: "Seize (Ightanim) the opportunities and respites."

In Sermon 120, He says: "Whoever takes it (the religion of Allah) reaches the goal and gains (Ghanima)." In Letter 53 to Malik al-Ashtar, He says: "Do not be a predatory beast over them, considering the devouring of them as Ghanimah (a gain/income) for yourself."

In Letter 45 to Uthman ibn Hunayf, He says: "By Allah, I have not treasured any gold from your world, nor have I amassed wealth from its Ghanimah (spoils/incomes)."

In the Short Sayings, in sentence 331, He says: "Indeed, Allah has made obedience the Ghanimah (gain) of the wise."

In Letter 41, we read: "Consider it a Ghanimah (gain) if someone asks you for a loan while you are wealthy." Such expressions are very numerous, all showing that "Ghanimah" is not restricted to war spoils.

As for the commentators:

Many commentators who have discussed this verse have explicitly admitted that Ghanimah originally has a broad meaning and includes war spoils and others, and generally anything a person attains without great hardship. Even those who, due to the fatwa of Sunni jurists, restricted the verse to war spoils, still confess that this restriction does not exist in its original linguistic meaning, but rather they applied this restriction due to other evidence.

"Qurtubi," the famous Sunni commentator, writes in his Tafsir under this verse: "Ghanimah linguistically is the good that an individual or a group acquires through effort"...

"And know that the consensus (of Sunni scholars) is that the meaning of Ghanimah in the verse (وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ‌) is the property that reaches the people through force and victory in war. However, it must be noted that this restriction, as we said, does not exist in its linguistic meaning, but in the terminology of the Sharia, this restriction has been introduced." [1]

"Fakhr al-Razi" in his Tafsir states: "Al-Ghunm is the attaining of a thing." After mentioning this broad linguistic meaning, he says: "The Sharia meaning of Ghanimah (according to Sunni jurists) is specifically war spoils." [1] Also, in "Tafsir al-Manar," "Ghanimah" is mentioned with a broad meaning and is not restricted to war spoils, although he believes the broad meaning of the verse should be restricted to war spoils due to a Sharia constraint. [2] In "Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani" by "Alusi," the famous Sunni commentator, it is also stated: "Ghunm originally means every kind of profit and benefit." [3] In "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan," he first interprets Ghanimah as war spoils, but when explaining the meaning of the verse, he says: "Our companions (the Shia scholars) say that Khums is obligatory on every benefit a person obtains from earnings and trade profits, and in treasures, minerals, diving, and other things mentioned in the books. It is possible to deduce this from this verse, for in linguistic usage, the name Ghunm and Ghanimah is applied to all of that." [4]

It is astonishing that some biased individuals, who seemingly have a specific mission to poison public opinion, have committed a scandalous distortion in a book they wrote about Khums regarding the text of "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan." They mentioned the first part of his statement, which includes the interpretation of Ghanimah as "war spoils," but completely ignored the explanation he provided at the end regarding the generality of the linguistic meaning and the verse's meaning, attributing a false claim to this great Islamic commentator. It is as if they thought "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan" was only in their hands and no one else would read it. What is strange is that they did not commit this betrayal only in this instance; in other cases as well, they took what was in their favor and ignored what was against them.

In "Tafsir al-Mizan," relying on the words of lexicographers, it is also stated that Ghanimah is any benefit that falls to a person through trade, work, or war. Although the occasion of the verse's revelation is war spoils, we know that the occasion never restricts the generality of the verse's concept. [1] From all that has been said, the following conclusion is drawn:

The verse of Ghanimah has a broad meaning and includes every kind of income, profit, and benefit, because the linguistic meaning of this word is general and there is no clear evidence to restrict it.

The only thing some Sunni commentators have relied upon is that the preceding and succeeding verses were revealed regarding Jihad, and this subject becomes a contextual clue that the verse of Ghanimah also refers to war spoils.

However, we know that the occasions of revelation and the context do not restrict the generality of a verse. To put it more clearly, there is no obstacle to the concept of the verse being a universal and general meaning, while the occasion of the verse's revelation was war spoils, which is one of the instances of this general ruling. Such rulings are frequent in the Quran and Sunnah, where the ruling is general and the instance is specific:

For example, in verse 7 of Surah al-Hashr, we read: "Whatever the Messenger gives you, take it; and what he forbids you, abstain from it." This verse states a general ruling regarding the necessity of following the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله commands, while its occasion of revelation was property that falls to the Muslims from enemies without war (technically called "Fay'").

Also, in verse 233 of Surah al-Baqarah, a general law is stated: "No soul shall be burdened beyond its capacity," while the occasion of the verse is about the wages of nursing women, where the father of the infant is commanded to pay them according to his ability. But can the entry of the verse in such a specific case prevent the generality of this law (the lack of obligation for what is beyond one's power)? In short: the verse was revealed among the verses of Jihad, but it says: whatever income accrues to you from any source—one of which is war spoils—pay its Khums. Especially the relative pronoun "Ma" (whatever) and "Shay'" (thing), which are two general words without any condition or restriction, support this.

6- Is allocating half of the Khums to the Bani Hashim discrimination?

Some imagine that this Islamic tax, which includes twenty percent of many properties and half of which is allocated to the Sayyids and descendants of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, is considered a type of racial privilege, and that considerations of kinship and discrimination are visible in it. They argue this is inconsistent with the spirit of Islamic social justice and its global and universal nature.

The answer for those who think this way is that they have not fully examined the conditions and specifics of this Islamic ruling, for the answer to this objection is entirely hidden within these conditions.

To explain: firstly, the half of the Khums belonging to the Sayyids and Bani Hashim must exclusively be given to their needy, and only to the extent of one year's requirements, and no more. Therefore, only those can use it who are incapacitated, ill, orphaned children, or others who, for some reason, are in a deadlock regarding living expenses.

Therefore, those who are capable of working (actually or potentially) and can have an income that sustains their life can never use this portion of Khums. The sentence famous among some common folk saying that Sayyids can take Khums even if the gutters of their house are made of gold is nothing more than a vulgar saying and has no foundation.

Secondly: the poor and needy among the Sayyids and Bani Hashim have no right to consume anything from Zakat; instead, they can only use this portion of Khums. [1]

Thirdly: if the share of the Sayyids, which is half of the Khums, exceeds the needs of the existing Sayyids, it must be put into the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and used for other purposes. Just as if the share of the Sayyids does not suffice for them, they must be given from the public treasury or the "Zakat" share.

Considering these three aspects, it becomes clear that in reality, no difference in material terms has been placed between Sayyids and non-Sayyids. The needy non-Sayyids can take their annual expenses from the Zakat fund but are deprived of Khums, and the needy Sayyids can only use the Khums fund but have no right to use Zakat.

In reality, there are two funds here: the "Khums Fund" and the "Zakat Fund," and each of these two groups only has the right to use one of these two funds, and that too in an equal amount—meaning the extent of one year's need. (Observe carefully). But those who have not scrutinized these conditions and specifics imagine that a larger share of the public treasury has been designated for Sayyids or that they enjoy a special privilege.

The only question that arises is: if there is no difference in the result between these two, what is the fruit of this program?

The answer to this question can also be understood by noting one point: there is an important difference between Khums and Zakat. Zakat is among the taxes that are actually considered part of the public property of the Islamic society; hence its expenditures are generally in this area. But Khums is among the taxes related to the Islamic government—meaning the expenses of the Islamic government apparatus and its operators are met from it.

Therefore, the deprivation of Sayyids from accessing public property (Zakat) is actually to keep the relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله away from this portion so that opponents do not have an excuse to say the Prophet has given His relatives control over public property.

But on the other hand, the needy Sayyids must also be provided for. This issue is anticipated in Islamic laws such that they benefit from the budget of the Islamic government, not from the public budget. In reality, Khums is not only not a privilege for Sayyids, but a kind of sidelining them for the sake of the public interest and to prevent any suspicion. [1] Interestingly, this point is mentioned in both Shia and Sunni hadiths: in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "A group of the Bani Hashim came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and requested that He appoint them to collect the Zakat on livestock, saying: 'We are more deserving of this share that Allah has designated for the collectors of Zakat.' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'O Bani Abd al-Muttalib, Zakat is not permissible for Me, nor for you. But in exchange for this deprivation, I promise you intercession... Be satisfied with what Allah and the Prophet have designated for you (and do not interfere in the matter of Zakat).' They said: 'We are satisfied.'" [2]

From this hadith, it is well understood that the Bani Hashim saw this as a kind of deprivation for themselves, and the Prophet promised them intercession in return!

In "Sahih Muslim," which is among the most famous books of the Ahl al-Sunnah, we read a hadith whose summary is: "Abbas and Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith came to the Prophet and requested that their sons, namely Abd al-Muttalib ibn Rabi'ah and Fadl ibn Abbas, who were two young men, be appointed to collect Zakat and receive a share like others so they could provide for their marriage expenses through this path. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله refused this and ordered that their marriage means be provided through another way, and their wives' dowries be paid from the Khums fund." [1] From this hadith, the explanation of which is long, it is also understood that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله insisted on keeping His relatives away from accessing Zakat (the public property of the people).

From all that we have said, it became clear that Khums is not only not considered a privilege for Sayyids, but was a type of deprivation to preserve public interests.

7- What is meant by the share of Allah?

Mentioning a share for Allah as "for Allah" (لِلَّهِ) is for the sake of greater importance regarding the principle of Khums and to emphasize and establish the authority and sovereignty of the Prophet and the leader of the Islamic government. That is, just as Allah has designated a share for Himself and considered Himself most entitled to dispose of it, He has likewise given the Prophet and the Imam the right of guardianship, oversight, and disposal. Otherwise, the share of Allah will be at the disposal of the Prophet and spent on the purposes the Prophet or Imam deems appropriate, for Allah has no need for a share.

Ayahs

إِذْ أَنتُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلْقُصْوَىٰ وَٱلرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ تَوَاعَدتُّمْ لَٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْمِيعَـٰدِ ۙ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَىَّ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٤٢)
