Commentary

A Task That Had to Be Done

The Quran once again, in connection with the mention of "Yawm al-Furqan" (the Day of the Battle of Badr) in the previous verse and the victories granted to the Muslims in this perilous scene, reminds the Muslims of other details of this battle so they may become more aware of the significance of this blessing of victory.

First, It says: "That day you were on the near side [of the valley, closer to Medina] and they were on the farther side" (إِذْ أَنْتُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الدُّنْيا وَ هُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الْقُصْوى‌).

"‘Udwah" (عُدوَة) is derived from the root "‘adw" (عَدْو), which originally means to transgress, but the edge or side of anything is also called "‘adw" because it has transgressed from the center toward one side. In the verse under discussion, it carries this meaning of "side" or "bank."

"Dunya" (دُنیا) is derived from "dunuww" (دُنُوّ), meaning lower or nearer, and its opposite is "aqsa" (أقصى) or "quswa" (قُصوى), meaning farther.

In this battlefield, the Muslims were positioned on the northern side, which was closer to Medina, while the enemies were on the southern side, which was farther away. It is also possible that the location the Muslims were forced to choose for combat was lower ground, while the enemy's position was elevated, which was considered an advantage for the enemy.

Then It says: "And the caravan [which you were pursuing—the trade caravan of Quraysh and Abu Sufyan] was at a lower base than you" (وَ الرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ‌). As previously mentioned, when Abu Sufyan learned of the Muslims' movement, he changed the caravan's route and, taking a bypass along the coast of the Red Sea, hurried toward Mecca. Had the Muslims not lost the caravan's trail, they might have pursued it and avoided the confrontation with the enemy army—the very clash that ultimately led to that great conquest and victory.

Beyond all this, the number of personnel and the military resources of the Muslims were fewer and weaker than the enemy in every respect; they were at a lower level while the enemy was at a higher level. Therefore, the Quran adds: "Conditions were such that had you made a mutual appointment [to meet for battle], you would have surely failed in the appointment" (وَ لَوْ تَواعَدْتُمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيعادِ).

This is because many of you would have been influenced by the outward appearance and your weak position relative to the enemy, and you would have fundamentally opposed such a battle.

But Allah placed you before a fait accompli "so that Allah might accomplish a matter that was already destined to be done" (وَ لكِنْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللَّهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولًا).

So that in the light of this unexpected and miraculous victory, truth might be distinguished from falsehood, "that those who were to perish [in misguidance] might perish after a clear proof had been established, and those who were to live [in guidance] might live after a clear proof" (لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَ يَحْيى‌ مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ).

The meaning of "life" and "perishing" here is "guidance" and "misguidance." For the Day of Badr, whose other name is "Yawm al-Furqan" (the Day of Criterion), clearly demonstrated Allah's support for the Muslims to everyone and proved that this group has a connection with God and that the truth is with them.

And at the end, It says: "Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

He heard your cries for help and was aware of your intentions; for this reason, He aided you until you were victorious over the enemy.

All evidence indicates that at least some of the Muslims, had they known the true extent of the enemy's power and army, would not have submitted to this confrontation—even though another group of sincere believers were surrendered to the will of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the face of all events. For this reason, Allah brought about circumstances so that both groups would inevitably face the enemy and engage in this decisive battle.

Among these circumstances was that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had previously seen a scene of the battle in a dream where the enemies appeared few in number before the Muslims. This was an indication and glad tidings of victory. He related this exact dream to the Muslims, which served to strengthen their morale and resolve in advancing toward the field of Badr.

Of course, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had seen this dream correctly, for although the enemy's strength and numbers were outwardly much greater, inwardly they were few, weak, and helpless. We know that dreams usually have an aspect of symbolism and interpretation, and in a true dream, the inner reality of a matter reveals itself.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله explained this dream to the Muslims, but perhaps a question remained deep in their minds: why did the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not see their outward appearance in the dream and describe it to the Muslims?

The second verse under discussion points to the wisdom behind this matter and the blessing Allah bestowed upon the Muslims through this means, saying:

"At that time, Allah showed them to you in your dream as few; and if He had shown them to you as many, you would have surely faltered" (إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي مَنامِكَ قَلِيلًا وَ لَوْ أَراكَهُمْ كَثِيراً لَفَشِلْتُمْ‌).

And not only would you have faltered, but "you would have disputed over the matter [some favoring going to the field and others opposing it]" (وَ لَتَنازَعْتُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ).

"But Allah saved [you from this weakness, disagreement, and turmoil through this dream which showed the inner reality rather than the outward appearance]" (وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ سَلَّمَ‌).

For Allah was aware of the morale and inner state of all of you, "and He is All-Knowing of what is within the hearts" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

In the next verse, It reminds of another stage of the Battle of Badr, which differs greatly from the previous stage. In this stage, the Muslims—in the light of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله stirring words, attention to divine promises, and witnessing events such as timely rain to quench thirst and the hardening of shifting sands in the battlefield—found a new spirit and became hopeful and encouraged regarding the final victory. This was to such an extent that the massive enemy army appeared small and insignificant in their eyes. Thus, It says: "And when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes" (وَ إِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا).

But the enemy, because they were unaware of the morale and this position of the Muslims, looked only at the outward numbers, and the Muslims appeared insignificant in their eyes—even fewer than they actually were. Thus, It says: "And He made you appear few in their eyes" (وَ يُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِهِمْ‌).

To the extent that it is narrated from Abu Jahl that he used to say: "Muhammad's companions are but a single meal for a camel!" (انما اصحاب محمد اكلة جزور). This was an allusion to their extreme fewness, or an indication that they would finish them off from morning until evening. For it is mentioned in the reports of the Battle of Badr that the Quraysh army slaughtered about ten camels every day, which was the daily food for their army of one thousand men.

In any case, these two matters had a profound effect on the victory of the Muslims. On one hand, the enemy's numbers appeared few to them so they would not feel fear or hesitation in engaging in battle. On the other hand, the number of Muslims appeared few to the enemy so they would not withdraw from a battle that would ultimately lead to their defeat, nor would they seek more reinforcements or increase their military readiness—under the assumption that the Islamic army was of no importance.

Therefore, following the above phrases, the Quran says: "All this was so that Allah might accomplish a matter that was already destined to be done" (لِيَقْضِيَ اللَّهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولًا).

Not only did this battle end according to what Allah willed, but "all matters and all things in this world return to His command and will" and His will permeates everything (وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ).

In verse 13 of Surah Al-Imran, which refers to a third stage of the battle on the Day of Badr, we read that after the battle began and the enemies witnessed the crushing blows of the Islamic army falling upon them like lightning, they were struck with terror. This time they felt that the Islamic army had become numerous—even twice what they actually were—and thus their morale was shaken, contributing to their defeat.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between the above verses, nor between them and the thirteenth verse of Surah Al-Imran, because each of these verses refers to a specific stage of the battle.

The first stage was before arriving at the battlefield, when their number was shown as few to the Prophet in a dream. The second stage was upon entering the land of Badr, when the Muslims became aware of the enemy's large numbers and some felt fear. The third stage was at the start of the combat, when by the grace of the Lord and by witnessing hopeful signs, the enemy's numbers appeared few in their eyes (note carefully).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَٱثْبُتُوا۟ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٤٥)
O you who have faith! When you meet a host [in battle], then stand firm, and remember Allah greatly so that you may be felicitous. (45)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَا تَنَـٰزَعُوا۟ فَتَفْشَلُوا۟ وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ ۖ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٤٦)
And obey Allah and His Apostle, and do not dispute, or you will lose heart and your power will be gone. And be patient; indeed Allah is with the patient. (46)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بَطَرًا وَرِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (٤٧)
Do not be like those who left their homes vainly and to show off to the people, and to bar [other people] from the way of Allah, and Allah comprehends what they do. (47)

Commentary

Six More Instructions Regarding Jihad!

Commentators have written that when Abu Sufyan, with particular cunning, safely led the Quraysh trade caravan out of the Muslims' territory, he sent someone to the Quraysh army—which was heading toward the battlefield of Badr—to inform them that there was no longer a need for their combat and they should return. However, "Abu Jahl," who possessed a particular pride, arrogance, and prejudice, swore that they would never return until they reached the land of Badr (and before this event, Badr was one of the gathering centers for Arabs where a trade market was held every year). He declared they would stay there for three days, slaughter camels, prepare a lavish feast, drink wine, and have singers sing and play for them so that their fame would reach the ears of all Arabs and their power and strength would be established.

However, their end resulted in defeat; instead of cups of wine, they drank the cups of death, and instead of "singers," "mourners" sat in lamentation for them! The above verses both refer to this subject, forbidding Muslims from such actions, and follow the previous instructions regarding Jihad by providing a series of further directives.

Altogether, six important instructions are given to the Muslims in the above verses:

1- First, He says: "O you who have believed, when you encounter a group [of enemies] in the battlefield, remain steadfast" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَاثْبُتُوا).

This means that one of the clear signs of faith is being steadfast in all fields, especially in the struggle against the enemies of Truth.

2- "And remember Allah much that you may be successful and victorious" (وَ اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَثِيراً لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ‌).

There is no doubt that the purpose of remembering Allah is not merely verbal remembrance, but rather seeing Allah as present within one's soul and being mindful of His infinite knowledge, power, and vast mercy. This attentiveness to Allah strengthens the spirit of the mujahid soldier, and in its light, he feels that he is not alone on the battlefield. He has a powerful support that no power can resist, and even if he is killed, he has attained the greatest felicity—the felicity of martyrdom—and will be successful in the proximity of Divine mercy. In short, the remembrance of Allah grants him strength, tranquility, power, and perseverance.

Furthermore, the remembrance and love of Allah drive the love of wife, children, wealth, and status out of the heart; focusing on Allah causes one to forget those things that lead to weakness in the matter of struggle and Jihad. As Imam Sajjad Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام, in the famous supplication of the Sahifa for the "Frontier Guards of Islam" and the defenders of Muslim borders, petitions Allah's presence as follows:

و انسهم عند لقائهم العدو ذكر دنياهم الخداعة و امح عن قلوبهم خطرات المال الفتون و اجعل الجنة نصب اعينهم‌

"O Lord! (In the light of Your remembrance) make them forget the thought of the deceptive world when they encounter the enemy, and erase from their hearts the temptations of alluring wealth, and place Paradise before their eyes." [^1]

3- Another of the most important programs of struggle is attention to the issue of leadership and obedience to the orders of the leader. This is the very instruction which, had it not been followed, would have led the Battle of Badr to the complete defeat of the Muslims. Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "And obey Allah and His Messenger" (وَ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ‌).

4- "And do not dispute and fall into conflict" (وَ لا تَنازَعُوا).

"For the friction, dispute, and disagreement of the mujahideen in the face of the enemy has as its first effect lethargy, inability, and weakness in the struggle" (فَتَفْشَلُوا).

"And the result of this lethargy and slackness is the vanishing of your power, strength, prestige, and greatness" (وَ تَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ‌).

[^1]: Sahifa Sajjadiyya.

"Rih" (ريح) means wind. When He says: "If you rise in dispute with one another, you will become weak and subsequently your wind will depart," it is a subtle reference to the fact that your strength, greatness, and the flow of affairs according to your desire and purpose will vanish. This is because the blowing of favorable winds has always been the cause of ships moving toward their intended destination, and at that time, when the only motive force for a ship was the blowing of the wind, this matter was of extraordinary importance.

Additionally, the blowing of the wind upon flags is a sign of the flag being raised, which is a symbol of power and sovereignty; the above expression is an allusion to this meaning.

5- Then He commands steadfastness against enemies and in the face of hardships, saying: "And be patient, for Allah is with the patient" (وَ اصْبِرُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ‌).

The difference between "steadfastness" (the first instruction) and "patience/endurance" (the fifth instruction) is that steadfastness has more of a physical and outward aspect, while patience and endurance encompass more psychological and inward aspects.

6- In the final verse, He restrains the Muslims from following foolish deeds, arrogant and hollow actions, and meaningless, empty clamor. Referring to the conduct of Abu Sufyan and the mindset of him and his companions, He says: "And do not be like those who came forth from their homes insolently and to be seen by people" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ بَطَراً وَ رِئاءَ النَّاسِ‌).

"Those whose goal was to avert people from the way of Allah" (وَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

Both their goal was unholy and the means of reaching this goal. And we saw that ultimately, despite all that force and military equipment, they were crushed; instead of pleasure and joy, a group rolled in dust and blood, and another group shed tears in mourning for them.

"And Allah is encompassing of what such individuals do and is aware of their actions" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَقَالَ لَا غَالِبَ لَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَإِنِّى جَارٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَرَآءَتِ ٱلْفِئَتَانِ نَكَصَ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ وَقَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ مَا لَا تَرَوْنَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٤٨)
When Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, and said [to the faithless], ‘No one shall overcome you today from among all mankind, and I will stand by you.’ But when the two hosts sighted each other, he took to his heels, saying, ‘Indeed I am quit of you. I see what you do not see. Indeed I fear Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution.’ (48)
إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ غَرَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ دِينُهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٤٩)
When the hypocrites said, and [also] those in whose hearts is a sickness, ‘Their religion has deceived them.’ But whoever puts his trust in Allah, then Allah is indeed all-mighty, all-wise. (49)
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۙ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٥٠)
Were you to see when the angels take away the faithless, striking their faces and their backs, [saying], ‘Taste the punishment of the burning. (50)
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٥١)
That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to the servants.’ (51)

Commentary

The Polytheists, the Hypocrites, and Satanic Whispers

In these verses, another scene from the Battle of Badr is depicted, consistent with the preceding verses on this subject or the most recent verse which spoke of the satanic deeds of the polytheists during the Battle of Badr.

Just as the men of Truth are supported by their Lord and His angels in the path they pursue, the followers of falsehood and the ill-intentioned remain under the umbrella of satanic whispers and the seduction of devils.

In some of the previous verses, the nature of the angels' support for the warriors of Badr was explained along with its commentary; here, in the first verse under discussion, the futile support of Satan for the polytheists is mentioned.

First, it says: "And on that day, Satan made their deeds seem fair and adorned to them" so that they would be optimistic, encouraged, and hopeful regarding their actions (وَ إِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

Satan’s "adornment" and "beautification" work such that by stimulating lusts, whims, and man's ugly and reprehensible traits, he portrays the face of one's actions in such a way that the person becomes intensely attracted to them, considering them wise, logical, and desirable from every perspective.

"And he made them understand that with all these numbers and war equipment, no one among the people today shall overcome you," and that you are an invincible army (وَ قالَ لا غالِبَ لَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ مِنَ النَّاسِ).

Furthermore, he said: "I am also your neighbor and by your side," and like a loyal and caring neighbor, I shall not withhold any support in the time of need (وَ إِنِّي جارٌ لَكُمْ).

Another possibility suggested in the commentary of this phrase is that the word "Jar" (neighbor) does not mean a literal neighbor, but rather one who grants protection and refuge. This is because it was the custom of the Arabs that powerful individuals and tribes would grant asylum to their friends when necessary, defending them with all their resources. "Satan" gave his polytheist friends a guarantee of protection and asylum.

"But when the two armies came in sight of each other, and the angels rose to support the army of Monotheism, and he witnessed the power of faith and the steadfastness of the Muslims, he turned back on his heels and cried out: 'I am free of you'—meaning the polytheists" (فَلَمَّا تَراءَتِ الْفِئَتانِ نَكَصَ عَلى‌ عَقِبَيْهِ وَ قالَ إِنِّي بَرِي‌ءٌ مِنْكُمْ).

And for this terrifying retreat of his, he provided two reasons: First:

"He said: 'I see what you do not see'" (إِنِّي أَرى‌ ما لا تَرَوْنَ).

I clearly see the signs of victory in these angry faces of the faithful Muslims, and I observe the signs of Divine support, unseen aid, and the assistance of the angels among them. Fundamentally, wherever the specific help of the Lord and His unseen forces are involved, I shall retreat.

Second: "I fear the painful punishment of the Lord in this scene," and I see it drawing near to me (إِنِّي أَخافُ اللَّهَ).

The punishment of Allah is not something simple that one can resist.

Rather, "His retribution is severe and harsh" (وَ اللَّهُ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

Did Satan appear through whispering or through physical manifestation?

There is a discussion among commentators, both past and present, regarding how Satan influenced the hearts of the polytheists and conducted these dialogues with them on the battlefield of Badr. Overall, two opinions exist:

  1. A group believes that this occurred through internal whispers. Using his suggestions and exploiting the negative and ugly satanic traits of the polytheists, he beautified their deeds in their eyes and made it appear to them that they possessed an invincible force, creating a kind of internal refuge and reliance within them.

However, after the fierce struggle of the Muslims and the miraculous events that led to their victory, the effects of these whispers were removed from their hearts. They felt that defeat was upon them and that they had no support; rather, a severe punishment from Allah awaited them.

  1. Another group believes that Satan manifested in a human form and appeared before them. In a narration cited in many books, we read:

When the Quraysh made a firm decision to move toward the field of Badr, they were fearful of an attack from the Bani Kinanah tribe, as they had prior hostilities. At this moment, Iblis appeared to them in the form of "Suraqah ibn Malik," one of the notables of the Bani Kinanah tribe. He reassured them, saying, "I am in agreement and harmony with you, and no one shall overcome you," and he participated in the field of Badr.

But when he witnessed the descent of the angels, he retreated and fled. The army, following the heavy blows they received from the Muslims and witnessing the action of Iblis, also took to flight. When they returned to Mecca, they said, "Suraqah ibn Malik caused the flight of the Quraysh." This reached Suraqah, and he swore, "I have no knowledge of this matter whatsoever." When they reminded him of various signs of his presence at Badr, he denied them all and swore that such a thing never happened and that he had not left Mecca. Thus, it became clear that the person was not Suraqah ibn Malik. [1]

The argument of the proponents of the first interpretation is that Iblis cannot appear in human form, while the proponents of the second interpretation say there is no evidence for the impossibility of this matter, especially since similar instances are narrated, such as the story of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) migration and the coming of an old man in the form of a person from "Najd" to the "Dar al-Nadwa." Furthermore, the literal sense of the expressions and dialogues in the above verse is more consistent with the physical manifestation of Iblis.

In any case, the verse shows that particularly in collective and group programs—whether on the path of Truth or falsehood—a series of Divine aids and forces or satanic forces are active. They manifest in whatever form they may, and those who tread the path of God must be mindful of this.

In the next verse, it refers to the collective morale of a group of supporters of the army of polytheism and idolatry at the scene of Badr, saying: "At that time, the hypocrites and those in whose hearts was a disease were saying: 'These Muslims have been deluded by their religion, and with this small number and meager weaponry, they have stepped into this dangerous scene that leads to death, thinking of victory or imagining martyrdom in the way of God and eternal life!'"

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and other commentaries under the aforementioned verse.

(إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ).

But due to their lack of faith and lack of awareness of the favors of the Lord and His unseen aid, they are unaware of this reality: "Whoever puts his trust in Allah and, after mobilizing all his forces, entrusts himself to Him, Allah will help him. For Allah is the Almighty, whom no one can resist, and He is the All-Wise, who would never leave His friends and the mujahideen of His path alone" (وَ مَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

Regarding who is meant by "the hypocrites" and "those in whose hearts is a disease," commentators have discussed much. However, it is not unlikely that both phrases refer to the group of hypocrites in Medina, for the Holy Quran says regarding the hypocrites (whose state was described at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah): "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease" (Al-Baqarah, verse 10).

These are either the hypocrites who had joined the ranks of the Muslims in Medina and professed Islam and faith but were not with them in their hearts, or those who had apparently believed in Mecca but refused to migrate to Medina and joined the ranks of the polytheists at the field of Badr. When they saw the small number of Muslims against the army of disbelief, they were filled with wonder and said these Muslims were deceived by their religion and creed to enter this field. In any case, Allah informs of their inner intentions and clarifies the mistake of them and their like-minded associates.

The next verse depicts the scene of the death of the disbelievers and the end of their ominous lives. First, addressing the Prophet, it says: "If you could see the exemplary state of the disbelievers when the angels of death were striking their faces and their backs, saying to them: 'Taste the punishment of the Burning,' you would have known their pitiable fate" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَ أَدْبارَهُمْ وَ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ).

Although "tara" (you see) is a present-tense verb, the presence of "law" (if) gives it a past meaning. Therefore, the above verse refers to the past state of the disbelievers and their painful death. For this reason, a group of commentators considers it a reference to their death at the field of Badr at the hands of angels, and some unconfirmed narrations have been cited in this regard. However, as we previously mentioned, there are indications that the angels did not directly intervene in the fighting at Badr. Thus, the verse refers to the angels of death, the moment the soul is taken, and the painful punishment they inflict upon the enemies of Truth and the faithless sinners at that moment.

"The punishment of the Burning" (عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ) refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, as it appears with the same meaning in other verses of the Quran, such as verses 9 and 22 of Surah Al-Hajj, and verse 10 of Surah Al-Buruj.

Then it says they are told: This painful punishment you are now tasting is because of what your own hands have sent forth and sent to this world in advance (ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ). The expression "hands" is used because man performs most of his actions with the help of his hands; otherwise, the verse includes all physical and spiritual deeds.

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "Allah is never unjust to His servants," and whatever punishment or retribution overtakes them in this world or the next is from their own side (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ).

The word "Zallam" is an intensive form meaning "one who is very unjust." The reason for choosing this word here and in similar places, as well as other discussions regarding injustice, was explained in the third volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 195.

Ayahs

كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٥٢)
Like the precedent of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who defied Allah’s signs, so Allah seized them for their sins. Indeed Allah is all-strong, severe in retribution. (52)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٣)
That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing: (53)
كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ وَكُلٌّ كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٥٤)
Like the precedent of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who denied the signs of their Lord; so We destroyed them for their sins, and We drowned Pharaoh’s clan; and they were all wrongdoers. (54)

Commentary

An Unchanging Sunnah!

In these verses, an "eternal Divine Sunnah" (way/law) regarding nations and peoples is mentioned, so that the misconception does not arise that what occurred regarding the fate of the polytheists at the Battle of Badr and their ill-fated end was an exceptional or exclusive ruling. Rather, these actions, whether committed by anyone in the past or in the future, will yield such results.

First, it says: "The condition of the polytheists of the Quraysh is like that of the folk of Pharaoh and those before them" (كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ‌); "those who denied the signs of Allah, so Allah seized them for their sins" (كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ‌).

"For indeed, Allah is All-Strong, Mighty in power, and His punishment is severe" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَوِيٌّ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ‌).

Therefore, it was not only the Quraysh and the polytheists and idolaters of Mecca who were caught by the punishment for their sins due to denying Divine signs, stubbornness against the truth, and conflict with the true leaders of humanity. This is an eternal law that encompasses more powerful and stronger nations like the Pharaohs, as well as weaker nations.

Then, it clarifies this subject by mentioning the fundamental root of the issue, saying: "All this is because Allah never changes a favor or blessing He has bestowed upon a people or nation unless that population itself changes and undergoes a transformation" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ‌).

In other words, the flow of Allah's mercy is boundless, general, and universal, but it reaches people in proportion to their merits and worthiness. Initially, Allah extends His material and spiritual blessings to nations. If they use these Divine blessings as a means for their perfection, seek help from them in the path of truth, and offer gratitude—which is their correct utilization—He makes His blessing lasting, or rather, increases it. However, when these bounties become a means for rebellion, defiance, oppression, injustice, discrimination, ingratitude, arrogance, and pollution, at that point He takes away the blessings or transforms them into calamity and misfortune. Therefore, changes always originate from our side; otherwise, Divine bounties are inexhaustible.

Following this objective, the Quran once again refers to the state of the powerful ones like the Pharaohs and another group of past nations, saying: "The state and habit of the idolaters regarding the withdrawal of blessings and being caught in the grip of severe punishments was like the state and condition of the folk of Pharaoh and those before them" (كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ‌). "They also denied and trampled upon the signs of their Lord, which were revealed for their guidance, empowerment, and prosperity" (كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

"So We destroyed them for their sins" (فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ‌).

"And We drowned the folk of Pharaoh" (وَ أَغْرَقْنا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ‌).

"And all these nations and their individuals were wrongdoers and oppressors," both toward themselves and toward others (وَ كُلٌّ كانُوا ظالِمِينَ‌).

Answer to a Question

A question arises here: why is the verse "كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ ..." repeated within such a short interval with a slight difference?

In response to this question, one must note that although repetition and emphasis in sensitive and vital matters is one of the principles of eloquence and is frequently seen in the speech of the eloquent and articulate, there is also an important difference in the above verses that removes the expression from being a mere repetition.

That is, the first verse refers to Divine punishments in response to the denial of the signs of truth, and then compares their state in this regard to the Pharaohs and past nations.

However, the second verse refers to the transformation of worldly blessings and the disappearance of Divine bounties—namely victories, security, power, and other honors—and then their state is compared to the state of the Pharaohs and past nations.

In reality, in one instance the discussion is about the withdrawal of blessings and the punishments resulting from it, and in the other instance, the discussion is about the change of blessings and their transformations.

In these verses, two important points are mentioned that are noteworthy from every perspective.

1- Factors of Life and Death of Nations

History shows us various nations and peoples: a group that rapidly traversed the final stages of progress, and a group that reached the lowest stage of decline; a third group that was one day scattered, helpless, and defeated, but another day became powerful and proud; and a fourth group that, conversely, fell from the highest stage of honor to the depths of the valley of humiliation and abasement.

There are many who pass easily by the various scenes of history without giving them the slightest thought, and there are also many who, instead of examining the primary and foundational causes and factors of the life and death of nations, place the blame on insignificant factors that do not play a fundamental role, or on superstitious, mythical, and imaginary factors.

Many attribute the entire cause of their misery to foreigners and their destructive policies, and some consider all these events to be the product of the favorable or unfavorable rotation of the celestial spheres. Finally, some resort to "Qada and Qadar" (Fate and Destiny)—in its distorted sense—or to luck, horoscope, and fortune, justifying all bitter and sweet events through this path.

All of this is because they are terrified of understanding the real causes.

In the above verses, the Quran puts its finger on the main point of "pains" and "remedies" and the factors of victory and defeat, saying: To find the main factor, it is not necessary to search the heavens and the earth or to follow imaginary and illusory factors; rather, it is sufficient to search only within your own existence, in your thoughts, spirit, and ethics, and in your social systems. Whatever exists is here!

Nations that employed their thought and intellect, joined hands in unity and brotherhood, possessed effort, endeavor, and a strong will and determination, and when necessary, practiced self-sacrifice and gave offerings, certainly became victorious. But when stagnation, lethargy, and laziness replaced effort and striving; when negligence and ignorance replaced awareness; when hesitation and doubt replaced decision; when conservatism replaced courage; when hypocrisy and division replaced unity; when self-indulgence and selfishness replaced sacrifice; and when ostentation and hypocrisy replaced sincerity and faith, decline and misery began.

In fact, the sentence "ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ حَتَّى‌ يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ‌" expresses the highest law of human life and makes it clear that the school of the Quran is the most authentic and clearest of schools regarding the life of societies. It even announces to those who, in the age of the atom and space, have forgotten the human being and consider the drivers of the wheels of history to be tools of production and economic issues—which are themselves products of humans—that you too are gravely mistaken. You have taken the effect and forgotten the primary cause, which is the people themselves and the transformation of humans; you have clung to a branch—and only one branch at that—and forgotten the main root!

Let us not look far; the history of Islam, or more accurately the history of the lives of Muslims, bears witness to brilliant victories at the beginning and bitter, painful defeats following them. In the early centuries, Islam advanced rapidly in the world, spreading the light of knowledge and freedom everywhere. It cast the shadow of science and knowledge over nations, was power-generating, strengthening, stirring, and developing, and created a dazzling civilization unprecedented in history. But not more than a few centuries passed before this surge turned into silence. Division and scattering, isolation and indifference, weakness and inability, and consequently backwardness, took the place of all that progress, to the point where the Muslims of the world were forced to turn to others for the basic necessities of life, sending their children to foreign lands to acquire science and knowledge, while one day Muslim universities were the top universities in the world and the center for students, both friend and stranger. But it reached a point where they not only stopped exporting science, industry, and technology but even imported basic food materials from outside their own countries!

Their land of Palestine, which was once the center of the glory and greatness of Muslims, and which even the Crusaders with millions of dead and wounded over two hundred years could not take from the soldiers of Islam, was easily lost in six days! While to take back an inch of it from the enemy, they must haggle for months and years—haggling whose end is unknown.

Has this Divine promise which says "وَ كانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌": "And it was a duty incumbent upon Us to help the believers" (Ar-Rum: 47) become subject to failure?

Or has it been abrogated where it says "وَ لِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَ لِرَسُولِهِ وَ لِرَسُولِهِ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌": "And to Allah belongs the honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers" (Al-Munafiqun: 8)?!

Or has it changed where it says: "وَ لَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ‌": "And We have already written in the Psalms after the Reminder that the earth shall be inherited by My righteous servants" (Al-Anbiya: 105)?

Is Allah—God forbid—incapable of fulfilling His promises? Or has He consigned His promises to oblivion? Or has He changed them?! ... If not, then why did all that glory, greatness, power, pride, and honor vanish?!

In the short verse above, the Holy Quran gives only one answer to all these questions and hundreds like them, and that is: take a look into the depths of your hearts, and cast a glance at the corners of your society; see that the changes began from your own side. Allah's grace and mercy are extended to everyone; it is you who destroyed the merits and capabilities and fell into such a sorrowful day! This verse does not only speak of the past, such that we might say the past with all its bitterness and sweetness has passed and will not return, and speaking of it is futile.

Rather, it speaks of today and the future as well: that if you return to Allah once more, strengthen the foundations of faith, awaken the minds, remember your commitments and responsibilities, press each other's hands, rise up and cry out, and roar and surge, give sacrifices and perform Jihad, and employ effort and striving in all fields, the water that has flowed away will return to the stream, the dark and gloomy days will pass, a brilliant horizon and a bright destiny will appear before you, and the ancient glory and greatness will be renewed at a higher level.

Come, change yourselves: let your scholars speak and write, your warriors fight, your merchants and laborers strive, your youth study more and more and become pure and strive to gain awareness, so that fresh blood flows in the veins of your society and you gain such power that the stubborn enemy who today does not return an inch of land upon request, returns all the lands to you with pleading! But these are truths that are easy to say, difficult to know and believe, and even more difficult to act upon; but in any case, one must move forward in the light of hope.

It is also necessary to mention that the issue of leadership plays a very effective role in the fate of nations and peoples, but it must not be forgotten that awakened nations always accept worthy leaders for their leadership, and unworthy, coercive, and oppressive leaders are crushed before the anger and iron will of nations.

This too must not be forgotten: that beyond the apparent causes and factors, there is a series of unseen aids and Divine favors awaiting the faithful, vibrant, and sincere servants; but these are not given to anyone without account; rather, they require the necessary merit and readiness.

We end this discussion by mentioning two narrations. First, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "ما أنعم اللَّه على عبد بنعمة فسلبها إياه حتى يذنب ذنبا يستحق بذلك السلب" "Allah does not bestow a blessing upon a servant and then take it away from him unless he commits a sin by which he deserves the withdrawal of that blessing." [1] In another Hadith from that Imam عليه السلام, we read: "Allah commanded a Prophet to say this to his people: No population or group that has been in ease and comfort under the shadow of My obedience has changed their state from what causes My pleasure, except that I too have changed their state from what they love to what they dislike; and any group or family that has been caught in distress because of sin, then changed their position from what causes My displeasure, I too have brought them to what they love and changed their position." [2]

2- Determinism of Fate, Historical Determinism, and Other Determinisms are Forbidden!

Another important subject clearly derived from the above verses is that man does not have a specific destiny that is predetermined, nor is he under the influence of "historical determinism," "determinism of time," or "environment." Rather, the factor that builds history and human life is the transformations that occur in his method, ethics, thought, and spirit by his own will.

Therefore, those who believe in a deterministic Qada and Qadar and say all events are by the forced will of the Lord are condemned by the above verse. Likewise, material determinism, which considers man a plaything in the hands of unchanging instincts and the principle of heredity, or environmental determinism, which considers him condemned by the state of economic conditions and production requirements, are worthless and incorrect from the perspective of the school of Islam and the Quran. Man is free and builds his own destiny.

Man—according to the principle we read in the above verses—holds the reins of his own destiny and history in his hands; it is he who creates honor and victory for himself, and it is he who catches himself in defeat and humiliation. His pain is from himself and his remedy is in his own hands; until a transformation occurs in his state and he changes himself through self-construction, no change will occur in his destiny!

[1] and [2]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 163.

Ayahs

إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٥)
Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are those who are faithless; so they will not have faith. (55)
ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتَّ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِى كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَتَّقُونَ (٥٦)
—Those with whom you made a treaty, and who violated their treaty every time, and who are not Godwary. (56)
فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِم مَّنْ خَلْفَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (٥٧)
So if you confront them in battle, treat them [in such a wise] as to disperse those who are behind them, so that they may take admonition. (57)
وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً فَٱنۢبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ (٥٨)
And if you fear treachery from a people, break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner. Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous. (58)
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَبَقُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَا يُعْجِزُونَ (٥٩)
Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmanoeuvred [Allah]. Indeed, they cannot frustrate [His power]. (59)

Commentary

Severity of Action Against Treaty-Breakers

In these verses, reference is made to another group of the enemies of Islam who, throughout the eventful history of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, inflicted severe blows upon the Muslims and ultimately tasted the painful consequences of their actions.

This group was the "Jews" of Medina, who repeatedly entered into treaties with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and unchivalrously broke their covenants.

These verses outline the firm approach the Prophet must adopt toward this treaty-breaking group—an approach that serves as a lesson for others and eliminates the danger posed by this group.

First, it introduces them as the most worthless living beings in this world, stating: "Indeed, the worst of living creatures in the sight of Allah are those who have disbelieved and continue in it, so they will not believe" (إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

The expression "الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا" (those who disbelieved) perhaps refers to the fact that many of the Jews of Medina, prior to the emergence of the Prophet of Islam, expressed interest and faith in him based on what they had seen in their own scriptures; in fact, they were his heralds, preparing the people for his appearance. However, after his appearance, because they saw their material interests in danger, they turned to disbelief and showed such stubbornness in this path that there was no hope for their faith, just as the Quran says: "so they will not believe" (فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Then it says: "These were the ones with whom you made a treaty so that,

at the very least, they would observe neutrality and not seek to harass the Muslims or aid the enemies of Islam, but they broke their treaty every time" (الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتَ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِي كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ) [1]. They neither felt shame before God "nor did they guard against [the consequences of] opposing His command," nor did they hesitate to trample upon human principles (وَ هُمْ لا يَتَّقُونَ).

The use of "ينقضون" (they break) and "لا يتقون" (they do not guard/fear), which are in the present continuous tense (Mudari'), indicates that they repeatedly broke their covenant with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the next verse, the manner of dealing with this treaty-breaking, faithless, and stubborn group is described as follows: "If you gain mastery over them in war [and they take up arms and stand against you], then deal with them so as to strike fear in those behind them, so that they may take a lesson and disperse, and not dare to show themselves" (فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِمْ مَنْ خَلْفَهُمْ).

"تثقفنهم" is derived from the root "ثقف" (rhyming with saqf), meaning to perceive something with precision and speed. This indicates that you must become aware of their positions quickly and accurately, and before they trap you in a surprise war, you must descend upon them like a lightning bolt!

And "شرد" is from the root "تشريد," meaning "to disperse accompanied by agitation."

[^1]: The word "من" in the phrase "عاهدت منهم" either signifies partitivity (tab'id), meaning you made a treaty with a group of the Jews of the Arabian Peninsula or with the leaders of the Jews of Medina; or it is what is termed a "silah" (connective), making the meaning of the sentence "عاهدتهم" (you made a treaty with them). There is also the possibility that the sentence means "you took a covenant from them."

That is, attack them in such a way that other groups of enemies and treaty-breakers are scattered and abandon the thought of attacking.

This command is so that other enemies, and even future enemies, may take a lesson and refrain from initiating war, and also so that those who have a treaty with the Muslims, or will have one in the future, refrain from breaking their covenant, "and perhaps they all will take heed" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ).

"And if they do not appear before you on the battlefield, but signs and indications have appeared from them suggesting they intend to break the treaty, and there is fear that they will commit treachery and unilaterally break their covenant without prior notice, then you take the initiative and announce to them that their treaty is nullified" (وَ إِمَّا تَخافَنَّ مِنْ قَوْمٍ خِيانَةً فَانْبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلى‌ سَواءٍ).

Lest you attack them without announcing the nullification of their treaty, "for indeed, Allah does not love the treacherous and those who pursue the path of betrayal in their covenants" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ). Although the above verse permits the Prophet to nullify the treaty based on the fear of treachery and treaty-breaking by the enemy, it is clear that this fear is not without reason; it is certainly in a context where they commit acts showing they are thinking of breaking the treaty, conspiring with the enemy, and launching a surprise attack. This amount of evidence and signs allows the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to declare their treaty nullified.

The phrase "فَانْبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ" is from the root "انباز" (anbaz), meaning to cast away or to announce. That is, cast their treaty back at them, nullify it, and announce its cancellation.

The expression "عَلى‌ سَواءٍ" either means that just as they have practically nullified their treaty,

you also nullify it from your side—this is a just and equal ruling—or it means announcing it in a clear, straightforward manner, free from any trickery or deception.

In any case, while the above verse warns Muslims to ensure they are not attacked by treaty-breakers, it also reminds them to observe human principles in maintaining commitments or nullifying treaties.

In the final verse under discussion, the address is turned toward this treaty-breaking group, warning them: "And let not those who disbelieve think that they have won through their treacherous acts and have escaped the realm of Our power and punishment" (وَ لا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَبَقُوا).

"They will never frustrate [Us] and will not escape the sphere of Our power" (إِنَّهُمْ لا يُعْجِزُونَ).

Ayahs

وَأَعِدُّوا۟ لَهُم مَّا ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن قُوَّةٍ وَمِن رِّبَاطِ ٱلْخَيْلِ تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِۦ عَدُوَّ ٱللَّهِ وَعَدُوَّكُمْ وَءَاخَرِينَ مِن دُونِهِمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٦٠)
Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power and war-horses, awing thereby the enemy of Allah, and your enemy, and others besides them, whom you do not know, but Allah knows them. And whatever you spend in the way of Allah will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged. (60)
۞ وَإِن جَنَحُوا۟ لِلسَّلْمِ فَٱجْنَحْ لَهَا وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦١)
If they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it, and put your trust in Allah. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (61)
وَإِن يُرِيدُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِۦ وَبِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٢)
But if they desire to deceive you, Allah is indeed sufficient for you. It is He who strengthened you with His help and with the means of the faithful, (62)
وَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ لَوْ أَنفَقْتَ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مَّآ أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٣)
and united their hearts. Had you spent all that is in the earth, you could not have united their hearts, but Allah united them together. Indeed He is all-mighty, all-wise. (63)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ حَسْبُكَ ٱللَّهُ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٤)
O Prophet! Sufficient for you is Allah and those of the faithful who follow you. (64)

Commentary

Increasing Combat Power and Its Objective

In alignment with the previous instructions regarding Islamic Jihad, the first verse under discussion points to a vital principle that must be considered by Muslims in every age and time: the necessity of sufficient military preparedness against enemies.

First, It says: "And prepare against them whatever you can of power" (وَ أَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ).

This means do not wait for the enemy to attack you and only then prepare to confront them; rather, you must have sufficient readiness in advance against potential enemy incursions.

Then It adds: "and of tethered horses" (وَ مِنْ رِباطِ الْخَيْلِ‌) to provide enough trained horses for the field of Jihad.

"Ribat" means to tie or link, and it is mostly used for tying an animal in a spot for keeping and protection. Consequently, it has come to mean protection and surveillance in general. "Murabatah" means guarding the borders as well as watching over anything else. The place where animals are tied and kept is called a "Ribat," and by extension, the Arabs call a caravanserai a "Ribat."

Here, several points should be noted

  1. In the short phrase above, a fundamental principle regarding Islamic Jihad and the preservation of the existence of Muslims, their glory, greatness, and honors is expressed. The expression of the verse is so broad that it fully applies to every age, time, and place.

What a small yet meaningful word "Quwwah" (power/force) is! It encompasses not only the military equipment and modern weapons of every age but also includes all forces and powers that in some way affect victory over the enemy, whether material or spiritual forces.

Those who imagine that the way to victory over the enemy and preserving one's existence depends solely on the quantity of war weapons are gravely mistaken. In the battlefields of our own era, we have seen nations with fewer personnel and weapons triumph over more powerful nations with advanced weaponry—such as the Muslim nation of Algeria against the powerful government of France!

Therefore, in addition to utilizing the most advanced weapons of any time—as a definitive Islamic duty—one must attend to strengthening the morale and faith of the soldiers, which is a more important "Quwwah" (power).

One must also not neglect economic, cultural, and political powers, which are also included in the concept of "Quwwah" and play a very effective role in victory over the enemy.

Interestingly, in Islamic narrations, various interpretations have been given for the word "Quwwah," which indicates the breadth of this word's concept. For example, in some narrations, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said the meaning of Quwwah is "archery" [1]. In another narration found in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read that it refers to any kind of weapon [2]. Again, in a narration in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, we read that it refers to the sword and shield [3].

[^1], [^2], [^3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 164-165.

Finally, in another narration found in the book "Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih," we read:

"Among it is dyeing [the hair] black" [1]: "One of the instances of Quwwah in the verse is dyeing white hair with black color!" This means Islam has not overlooked even the color of hair, which gives an older soldier a more youthful appearance to intimidate the enemy. This shows how vast the concept of "Quwwah" is in the above verse.

Therefore, those who have only seen some of the narrations and limited the word "Quwwah" to a single instance have fallen into a strange error.

Alas, despite having such an explicit and clear instruction, it seems Muslims have forgotten everything. There is no sign among them of preparing spiritual and psychological forces to confront the enemy, nor of economic, cultural, political, and military forces. The wonder is that with this great forgetfulness and casting aside such an explicit command, we still consider ourselves Muslims and blame the sin of our backwardness on Islam, saying: "If Islam is a religion of progress and victory, then why are we Muslims backward?!"

In our opinion, if this great Islamic instruction (وَ أَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ) were propagated as a universal slogan everywhere, and if Muslims—young and old, scholar and non-scholar, writer and speaker, soldier and officer, farmer and merchant—applied it in their lives, it would be sufficient to compensate for their backwardness.

The practical life (Sira) of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the great leaders of Islam also shows that they did not neglect any opportunity to confront the enemy. In preparing weapons and personnel, strengthening the morale of soldiers, choosing the campsite, selecting the appropriate time for attacking the enemy, and employing any kind of war tactic, they did not overlook any matter, large or small.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 165.

It is well known that during the days of the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was informed that a new effective weapon had been invented in Yemen. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله immediately sent people to Yemen to procure that weapon for the army of Islam!

In the events of the Battle of Uhud, we read that in response to the slogan of the idolaters, "Exalt Hubal, exalt Hubal" (the great idol Hubal), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله taught the Muslims a more crushing and penetrating slogan to say:

"Allah is Higher and more Majestic" (اللَّه اعلى و اجل).

And in response to the slogan "We have Uzza and you have no Uzza," they were to say:

"Allah is our Master and you have no master" (اللَّه مولانا و لا مولا لكم).

This shows that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims were not even negligent of the impact of a powerful slogan against the enemy and chose the best for themselves.

The important Islamic jurisprudential rule regarding "archery and horse-racing competitions," which even permits financial wagering in these fields and invites Muslims to these competitions, is another example of Islam's deep insight regarding preparedness against the enemy.

  1. Another important point derived from the above verse is the secret of the universality and eternity of the religion of Islam. The concepts and contents of this religion are so broad that they do not become obsolete or worn out with the passage of time. The sentence "And prepare against them whatever you can of power" had a living meaning a thousand years ago, it is the same today, and ten thousand years from now its meaning will remain just as alive. For whatever weapon or power emerges in the future is hidden within the comprehensive word "Quwwah," because the phrase "whatever you can" is "general" and the word "Quwwah," which appears as an "indefinite noun," reinforces its generality and includes every kind of force.
  1. A question arises here: why, after mentioning the word "Quwwah" which has such a broad concept, is the issue of trained warhorses mentioned?

The answer to this question becomes clear in one sentence: while the above verse expresses a broad instruction for all centuries and ages, it also expresses a specific instruction for the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the era of the Quran's revelation. In fact, It implemented this general concept by mentioning a clear example for the needs of that age and time. For although the horse does not play much of a role in today's battlefields with the existence of tanks, armored vehicles, airplanes, and helicopters, in that era, it was considered a nimble and fast means for brave and pioneering warriors.

The Ultimate Goal of Preparing Weapons and Increasing Combat Power

Then, following this instruction, the Quran points to the logical and humane goal of this matter and says: the goal is not to mow down the people of the world or even your own nation with various destructive and devastating weapons, nor to lead settlements and lands to ruin. The goal is not to seize the lands and properties of others, nor is the goal to expand the principles of slavery and colonialism in the world. Rather, the goal is that "you may terrify thereby the enemy of Allah and your enemy" (تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِ عَدُوَّ اللَّهِ وَ عَدُوَّكُمْ‌).

For most enemies do not listen to the word of reason, logic, and humane principles; they understand nothing but the logic of force!

If Muslims are weak, all kinds of impositions will be forced upon them. But when they acquire sufficient power, the enemies of truth and justice, and the enemies of independence and freedom, fall into terror and stay in their place.

Even now, as we write the commentary of this verse, a significant part of Islamic lands in Palestine and other countries is under the boots of Israeli soldiers. The cowardly attack that recently occurred in southern Lebanon, displacing thousands of families, killing hundreds of people, and turning settlements into horrific ruins, added a new chapter to this tragic story.

While the public opinion of the world has collectively condemned this act, and even the friends of Israel have joined others in this matter, and UN resolutions command Israel to evacuate all these lands, this nation of a few million does not listen to any of these issues. This is because they have force, weapons, power, sufficient combat readiness, and a strong supporter, and they have prepared themselves for such aggressions for many years. The only logic that can answer them is the logic of: "And prepare against them whatever you can of power... you may terrify thereby the enemy of Allah and your enemy." It is as if this verse was revealed in our era and for our situation today, saying: become so powerful that the enemy falls into terror, returns the usurped lands, and stays in his place.

It is noteworthy that It coupled the phrase "enemy of Allah" with "your enemy," indicating that in the matter of Jihad and Islamic defense, personal motives are not at play. Rather, the goal is to preserve the humane school of Islam. Those whose enmity with you is a form of enmity with God—meaning enmity with truth, justice, faith, monotheism, and humane programs—should be the target of your attacks or defense in these areas.

In fact, this expression is similar to the expression "in the way of Allah" or "Jihad in the way of Allah," which shows that Islamic Jihad and defense are neither in the form of the conquest-seeking of past monarchs, nor the expansionism of today's colonialists and imperialists, nor in the form of the plundering of pre-Islamic Arab tribes. Rather, it is all for God, in the way of God, and in the path of reviving truth and justice.

Then It adds: "and others besides them whom you do not know," and with the increase of your combat readiness, they too will be afraid and stay in their place (وَ آخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ‌).

Here, two points should be noted

  1. Although commentators have given various possibilities regarding this group—some have considered it a reference to a group of Jews in Medina who kept their enmity hidden, and others have considered it a reference to future enemies of Muslims such as the Roman Empire and the Sassanid court, with whom Muslims did not expect a conflict at that time—what seems more correct is that they are the hypocrites (Munafiqun). For they existed unrecognized among the ranks of the Muslims, and in the event of the full readiness of the Islamic army, they too would fall into terror and restrain themselves.

The evidence for this is verse 101 of Surah At-Tawbah, which says: "And among the people of Medina, they are stubborn in hypocrisy; you do not know them, We know them" (وَ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ مَرَدُوا عَلَى النِّفاقِ لا تَعْلَمُهُمْ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ‌).

It is also possible that all groups of unknown enemies of Islam, whether hypocrites or others, are included in the concept of the verse.

  1. The verse also contains an instruction for Muslims today: they should not only focus on their known enemies and limit their readiness to the level of fighting them. Rather, they must also consider potential and latent enemies and provide the maximum necessary force and power. If Muslims had truly kept such a point in mind, they would never have been caught by surprise attacks from powerful enemies.

At the end of this verse, It points to another important matter: that providing sufficient force and power, tools and weapons of war, and various means of defense requires financial capital. Therefore, It commands Muslims that they must provide this capital through general cooperation. And know that whatever you give in this way, you have given in the way of Allah and it will never be lost: "And whatever you spend in the way of Allah shall be repaid to you in full" (وَ ما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ‌).

And all of it and more will reach you, "and you shall not be wronged" (وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تُظْلَمُونَ‌).

This reward will reach you both in the life of this world through the victory of Islam and its glory and greatness—for a weak and defeated nation will also have its financial assets endangered and will lose its security, peace, and independence. Therefore, the wealth spent in this way will return to the spenders through another way and at a higher level.

And a greater reward awaits you in the other world in the proximity of the Lord's mercy. In this state, not only will no injustice or oppression befall you, but you will gain the highest profit and benefit.

It is noteworthy that in the above sentence, the word "Shay'" (thing), which has a broad meaning, is used. That is, any kind of thing—whether life, wealth, intellectual power, the power of logic, or any other capital—that you spend in the way of strengthening the defensive and military foundation of Muslims against the enemy will not be hidden from God; He will preserve it and give it to you at the appropriate time.

In the interpretation of the phrase "and you shall not be wronged" (وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تُظْلَمُونَ‌), some commentators have also suggested that the sentence might be conjoined to the word "Turhibun" (you terrify). That is, if you provide sufficient force to confront the enemies, they will be terrified of attacking you and will not have the ability to wrong or oppress you; therefore, no wrong or oppression will occur against you.

The Goal and Pillars of Islamic Jihad

Another point derived from the above verse, which answers many questions and objections from critics and uninformed individuals, is the form, goal, and program of Islamic Jihad. The verse clearly says the goal is not to kill humans, nor is the goal to violate the rights of others. Rather, as we said, the main goal is for the enemies to be afraid and not to aggress against you or bully you. All your effort and endeavor should be summarized in curtailing the evil of the enemies of God, truth, and justice.

Have the opponents ever visualized such a picture of Islamic Jihad, which the Quran explicitly brings in the above verse, when they repeatedly attack this Islamic law? Sometimes they say Islam is the religion of the sword, sometimes they say Islam resorted to weapons to impose belief, and sometimes they compare the Prophet of Islam with other conquerors of history! In our opinion, the answer to all such objections is for them to return to the Quran and reflect on the ultimate goal of this program so that everything becomes clear to them.

Readiness for Peace

Although the previous verse sufficiently specified the goal of Islamic Jihad, the next verse, which discusses peace with the enemy, makes this reality even clearer. It says: "And if they incline to peace, then incline to it [also]" (وَ إِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها).

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the above sentence that if they spread their wings toward peace, you too spread your wings toward it. For "Janahu" comes from the root "Junuh" meaning "inclination," and the wings of birds are called "Janah" because each of their wings inclines to one side. Therefore, in the interpretation of the verse, one can use both the root of the word and its secondary concept.

And since at the time of signing a peace treaty, individuals are often caught in doubts and hesitations, It commands the Prophet: do not hesitate in accepting the proposal of peace, and if its conditions are logical, wise, and just, accept it, "and trust in Allah; indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing" (وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

However, It warns the Prophet and the Muslims that there might be deception and trickery involved in peace proposals, and they might make peace a prelude to a surprise strike, or their goal might be to delay the war to prepare more force. But do not let this matter cause you concern either, for Allah is sufficient for you and is your supporter in all circumstances: "And if they intend to deceive you, then indeed, Allah is sufficient for you" (وَ إِنْ يُرِيدُوا أَنْ يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ اللَّهُ‌).

The history of your life is also a witness to this reality, for "It is He who supported you with His help and with the believers" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِ وَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Many times great dangers were prepared for you and dangerous plans were plotted that were impossible to overcome through ordinary means, but He preserved you against all of them.

Furthermore, these sincere believers who have gathered around you and do not withhold any kind of sacrifice were previously a scattered people and enemies of one another. Allah shed the light of guidance upon them "and brought together their hearts" (وَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

For many years, there was war and bloodshed between the tribes of Aws and Khazraj in Medina, and their chests were overflowing with malice and enmity, such that no one believed they would one day extend the hand of friendship and love toward each other and stand in one rank. But Allah, the Almighty, accomplished this in the light of Islam and under the shadow of the Quran's revelation. Not only did the Aws and Khazraj, who were from the Ansar, have such conflict, but the Muhajir companions of the Prophet who had come from Mecca also did not have the bond of friendship before Islam, and perhaps their chests were full of malice toward one another. But Allah washed away and removed all those grudges, such that from the three hundred and thirteen warriors of Badr—of whom about eighty were Muhajirun and the rest were Ansar—a small but integrated, powerful, united, and unanimous army was created that crushed their powerful enemy.

Then It adds that bringing about this affection and bond of hearts was not possible through material and ordinary means: "If you had spent all that is in the earth, you could not have brought their hearts together" (لَوْ أَنْفَقْتَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً ما أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

"But Allah brought them together" through faith (وَ لكِنَ اللَّهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

Those who are familiar with the psychology of stubborn and vengeful individuals, especially ignorant peoples like the people of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), know that such grudges and enmities cannot be washed away by wealth or status. The only way to quench it is revenge—the same revenge that would be repeated in a chain, and each time its ugly face would become more terrifying and its scope wider. The only thing that can remove those deep-seated and rooted grudges is to create a kind of revolution and transformation in thoughts, ideas, and souls—a revolution that transforms personalities, changes mindsets, and places them at a level much higher than they were, such that past actions appear base, insignificant, and foolish to them. Following that, they undertake a house-cleaning in the depths of their being and throw out the trash of malice, cruelty, vengefulness, tribal prejudices, and the like. This is something that can never be achieved by money and wealth, but is only possible under the shadow of faith and true monotheism.

And at the end of the verse, It adds: "Indeed, He is Exalted in Might and Wise" (إِنَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

His Might (Izzah) necessitates that no one has the power to resist Him, and His Wisdom (Hikmah) causes all His actions to be based on calculation. Therefore, a calculated program united the scattered hearts and placed them at the disposal of His Prophet to spread the light of Islamic guidance throughout the world through them.

Here, two points should be noted:

  1. Some commentators have considered the above verse to be only a reference to the differences between "Aws" and "Khazraj" who were from the Ansar. However, considering that both the Muhajirun and Ansar rose to help the Prophet in one rank, it becomes clear that the concept of the verse is a broad one. Perhaps they thought that the only difference was between Aws and Khazraj, whereas there were a thousand kinds of social differences and rifts between the poor and rich classes, and between the small and great masters of this tribe and that tribe—rifts that were filled under the shadow of Islam and whose effects were removed, just as the Quran says elsewhere: "And remember the favor of Allah upon you—when you were enemies and He brought your hearts together and you became, by His favor, brothers" (Aal-Imran: 103).

[The Connecting Link of Faith]

  1. This law was not only related to the first Muslims. Today, too, as Islam has spread its shadow over eight hundred million Muslims of the world and has followers from various races, completely different peoples, and diverse social groups, no connecting link can unite and relate them to each other (except the connecting link of faith and monotheism). Wealth, riches, material encouragements, congresses, and conferences alone cannot achieve anything. The same flame must be kindled in the hearts that was in the hearts of the first Muslims, and victory and triumph are also only possible through this path of Islamic brotherhood.

In the last verse under discussion, to strengthen the pure morale of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the discourse is directed at him, saying: "O Prophet, Allah is sufficient for you and for whoever follows you of the believers" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللَّهُ وَ مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Some commentators have narrated that this verse was revealed when the Jewish tribes of "Bani Qurayza" and "Bani Nadir" said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "We are ready to submit to you and follow you (and we will help you)." The above verse was revealed and warned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not to trust or rely on them, but rather to make his reliance only on Allah and the believers [1]. Hafiz Abu Nu'aym, who is among the famous scholars of the Ahl al-Sunnah, narrated in his book Fada'il al-Sahaba with his chain of transmission that this verse was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام and the meaning of "the believers" is Ali عليه السلام [2]. We have repeatedly said that such interpretations and occasions of revelation do not restrict or limit the verse; rather, the meaning is that a person like Ali عليه السلام, who stands in the first rank of the believers, is the primary reliance of the Prophet after God among the Muslims, even though other believers are also in the subsequent ranks as helpers and supporters of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.
