Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ حَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقِتَالِ ۚ إِن يَكُن مِّنكُمْ عِشْرُونَ صَـٰبِرُونَ يَغْلِبُوا۟ مِا۟ئَتَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُم مِّا۟ئَةٌ يَغْلِبُوٓا۟ أَلْفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (٦٥)
O Prophet! Urge on the faithful to fight: If there be twenty patient men among you, they shall overcome two hundred; and if there be a hundred of you, they shall overcome a thousand of the faithless, for they are a lot who do not understand. (65)
ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ خَفَّفَ ٱللَّهُ عَنكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفًا ۚ فَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُم مِّا۟ئَةٌ صَابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا۟ مِا۟ئَتَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُمْ أَلْفٌ يَغْلِبُوٓا۟ أَلْفَيْنِ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٦٦)
Now Allah has lightened your burden, knowing that there is weakness in you. So if there be a hundred patient men among you, they shall overcome two hundred; and if there be a thousand, they shall overcome two thousand, by Allah’s leave; and Allah is with the patient. (66)

Commentary

Do Not Wait for Equal Strength!

In these two verses, military instructions and the rulings of Islamic Jihad continue to be addressed.

In the first verse, the Noble Messenger is commanded: "O Prophet! Urge and encourage the believers to fight the enemy" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَى الْقِتَالِ).

No matter how prepared warriors and combatants may be, they must be spiritually strengthened and, so to speak, "recharged" before starting a battle. This is included in the programs of all conscious armies of the world, where commanders and officers strengthen the fighting spirit of their troops by mentioning appropriate matters before moving toward the battlefield or before launching an attack, warning them of the danger of defeat.

However, the scope of this encouragement and urging is limited in materialistic schools and their likes, whereas in heavenly schools, it is much broader. Focusing on the command of the Lord, the impact of faith in Allah, remembering the status of the martyrs in the path of Truth, the virtue and the countless rewards awaiting them, and the honors and spiritual blessings found in victory over the enemy in the arena of Jihad, are the best means to encourage and stimulate the spirit of gallantry, perseverance, and steadfastness in soldiers. In Islamic wars, sometimes the recitation of a few verses from the Holy Quran would make the mujahid soldiers so ready that they became restless with passion, fervor, and excitement.

In any case, this phrase of the verse clarifies the importance of propagation and strengthening the morale of soldiers as much as possible as an Islamic instruction.

Following that, it gives a second instruction, saying: "If there are twenty steadfast soldiers among you, they will overcome two hundred, and if there are a hundred of you, they will overcome a thousand of those who disbelieve" (إِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ عِشْرُونَ صَابِرُونَ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ وَ إِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا أَلْفاً مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

Although the expression of the verse is in the form of reporting the victory of one person over ten, by the context of the next verse which says: "Now Allah has lightened [the burden] for you" (الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللَّهُ عَنْكُمْ), it becomes clear that the intention is to define a duty, a program, and an instruction, not merely a simple report. Thus, Muslims should not wait to be at an equal level with the enemy in terms of combat personnel; rather, even if their number is one-tenth of the enemy's personnel, the duty of Jihad is still obligatory upon them.

Then, it points to the reason for this ruling, saying: "That is because your faithless enemies are a people who do not understand" (بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ).

This reasoning might initially seem strange: what connection is there between "awareness" and "victory," or "lack of awareness" and "defeat"? But in reality, the relationship between these two is very close and firm. This is because the believers know their path well, understand the purpose of their creation and existence, and are aware of the positive results in this world and the abundant rewards awaiting the mujahideen in the next world. They know "why" they are fighting, for "whom" they are battling, and for "what sacred goal" they are sacrificing themselves. And if they are sacrificed and martyred in this path, they know with "whom" their account lies.

This clear path and this awareness grant them patience, perseverance, and steadfastness.

However, faithless individuals or idolaters do not truly know why they are fighting or for whom they are battling. If they are killed in this path, who will compensate for their blood? They have followed this school only out of habit, blind imitation, or dry, illogical prejudice. This darkness of the path, lack of awareness of the goal, and not knowing the end of the matter and the result of the struggle, weakens their nerves, takes away their power and perseverance, and turns them into weak beings.

However, following the heavy instruction above, Allah lightens it by several degrees and says: "Now Allah has lightened [the burden] for you, and He knows that there is weakness in you" (الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللَّهُ عَنْكُمْ وَ عَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً).

Then He says: "In this state, if there are a hundred steadfast soldiers among you, they will overcome two hundred, and if there are a thousand, they will overcome two thousand by Allah's permission" (فَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ صَابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ وَ إِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ أَلْفٌ يَغْلِبُوا أَلْفَيْنِ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

But in any case, do not forget that "Allah is with the patient" (وَ اللَّهُ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ).

Key Points

1- Is the first verse abrogated?

As we observed, the first verse commands Muslims that even if the enemy army is ten times their size, they should not refuse to confront them, while the second verse has reduced the ratio to double.

This apparent difference has caused some to consider the ruling of the first verse abrogated by the second verse, or to interpret the first verse as a recommended (mustahabb) ruling and the second as an obligatory (wajib) ruling. That is, if the number of enemies is at most double the number of Muslims, they have a duty not to retreat from the field of Jihad; but if it is more than double, up to ten times, they may refrain from Jihad, though it is better not to give up the struggle.

However, a group of commentators believes that the apparent difference seen between the two verses is neither evidence of abrogation nor evidence of recommendation. Rather, each of these two rulings applies to a specific case: when Muslims are afflicted with weakness and lethargy, and there are inexperienced, untrained, and unrefined individuals among them, the measure of assessment is the ratio of double. But when there are refined, experienced, and strong-in-faith individuals, like many of the warriors of Badr, this ratio rises to ten times.

Therefore, the two rulings mentioned in the two verses pertain to two different groups under different conditions, and thus there is no abrogation here. If we see the term "abrogation" (naskh) used in some narrations, we must note that "naskh" in the language of narrations has a broad meaning that also includes "specification" (takhsis).

2- The Myth of the Balance of Power

These verses—in any case—contain the definitive ruling that Muslims should never sit waiting for an apparent balance of power with the enemy. Rather, they must sometimes stand against twice their population and sometimes even ten times, and not flee from the enemy under the pretext of a shortage of personnel. Interestingly, in most Islamic battlefields, the balance of power was skewed in favor of the enemy, and Muslims were often in the minority. This was true not only in the wars that occurred during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, such as Badr, Uhud, and Ahzab, or wars like Mu'tah—where the number of Muslims was three thousand and the minimum number recorded for the enemy was one hundred and fifty thousand—but also in the wars that took place after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where this difference existed in a staggering manner. For example, the number of personnel in the Islamic liberation army in the war against the "Sassanid" army was recorded as fifty thousand, while the number of Khosrow Parviz's troops was recorded as five hundred thousand.

In the event of "Yarmouk," which was the great encounter of the Islamic army with the Roman forces, historians have narrated that the army gathered by Heraclius was about two hundred thousand, while the Islamic army did not exceed twenty-four thousand!! Even more amazing is that they wrote the number of enemies who fell in this field reached over seventy thousand.

Doubtless, the balance and superiority of power is outwardly one of the factors of victory, but what caused this large and striking difference to be compensated in favor of the Muslims?

The Quran provides the answer to this important question with three expressions in the two verses above. In one place it says: "عِشْرُونَ صَابِرُونَ" (twenty steadfast ones) and "مِائَةٌ صَابِرَةٌ" (a hundred steadfast ones).

This means the spirit of perseverance and steadfastness, which is the fruit of the tree of faith, caused each person to stand against ten and overcome them.

In another place, it says: "بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ" (because they are a people who do not understand), meaning their lack of awareness of their goal and your awareness of your sacred goal compensates for the shortage of personnel.

And in another place, it uses the expression "بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ" (by Allah's permission), meaning divine assistance, unseen and spiritual help, and the grace and mercy of the Lord encompass such faithful and steadfast mujahideen.

Today, too, Muslims face powerful enemies, but the strange thing is that in many battlefields, the number of Muslims is far superior to the enemy, yet there is still no sign of victory, and they are moving exactly in the opposite direction of the "early Muslims."

This is because, unfortunately, sufficient awareness is not present among today's Muslims; they have lost the spirit of perseverance due to surrendering to factors of corruption and materialistic glitter, and divine support has been withdrawn from them due to being tainted with sin. Consequently, they have been caught in such a fate! But the way back is still open, and we hope a day comes when the concept of the above verses is revived once again among Muslims and they put an end to their current humiliating situation.

3- It is noteworthy that in the first verse, which speaks of the ratio of one to ten, "عشرون" (twenty) and "مائتين" (two hundred) are chosen as examples. However, in the second verse, which speaks of the double ratio, the examples of one hundred against two hundred and one thousand against two thousand are chosen. This difference in examples seemingly expresses the reality that strong-willed and faithful individuals can form an army even from twenty people, whereas weak individuals cannot form an army from such a small number; rather, they must assemble an army from several times that amount.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِنَبِىٍّ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَسْرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يُثْخِنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٧)
A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly decimated [the enemy] in the land. You desire the transitory gains of this world, while Allah desires [for you] [the reward of] the Hereafter, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (67)
لَّوْلَا كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيمَآ أَخَذْتُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٦٨)
Had it not been for a prior decree of Allah, surely there would have befallen you a great punishment for what you took. (68)
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٦٩)
Avail yourselves of the spoils you have taken as lawful and good, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (69)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّمَن فِىٓ أَيْدِيكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَسْرَىٰٓ إِن يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْرًا يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْرًا مِّمَّآ أُخِذَ مِنكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧٠)

But if they seek to betray you, then they have already betrayed Allah earlier, and He gave [you] power over them; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (71)

Commentary

Prisoners of War

In the preceding verses, significant portions of the rulings on Jihad and engagement with enemies were explained. In the verses under discussion, this discourse is completed by mentioning some of the rulings regarding prisoners of war, as wars are usually accompanied by the issue of taking captives. The manner of treating prisoners of war is of extraordinary importance from both humanitarian perspectives and the objectives of Jihad.

The first important point stated in this regard is:

"It is not for a prophet to have captives until he has sufficiently established his foothold in the land and dealt heavy, decisive blows to the body of the enemy" (مَا كَانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"Yuthkhin" (يُثْخِنَ) is derived from the root "thukhn" (ثخن), which originally means thickness, density, and heaviness. Subsequently, it has been applied to manifest victory, dominance, strength, power, and intensity.

A group of commentators have interpreted "حَتَّىٰ يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ" as meaning exaggeration and intensity in killing the enemy, suggesting that taking prisoners should only occur after a great slaughter of the enemy. However, considering the phrase "in the land" (فِي الْأَرْضِ) and the root of the word meaning intensity and thickness, it becomes clear that the primary meaning is not such. Rather, the main objective is complete superiority over the enemy, demonstrating strength and power, and consolidating one's authority over the region.

However, since crushing and killing the enemy sometimes leads to the consolidation of the Muslims' position, killing the enemy can be one of the instances of this phrase under specific circumstances, but it is not the primary concept of the phrase!

In any case, the verse under discussion draws the Muslims' attention to a sensitive military point: Muslims should never think of taking prisoners before the complete defeat of the enemy. As inferred from some narrations, some novice Muslims at the Battle of Badr tried to take as many prisoners as possible because, according to the custom of wars in those days, after the war ended, they would receive a significant amount called "fidya" (ransom) and release the prisoners in exchange for it.

This action might be considered good in some instances, but before being completely certain of the enemy's defeat, it is a dangerous act. Engaging in taking prisoners, binding their hands, and transferring them to a suitable location often distracts warriors from the primary objective of the war. It may even allow a "wounded enemy" to intensify their attacks and crush the warriors, just as in the event of the Battle of Uhud, focusing on collecting spoils occupied a group of Muslims, and the enemy seized the opportunity to deliver their final blow! Therefore, taking prisoners is only permissible when complete certainty of victory over the enemy is achieved; otherwise, one must disable the power and force of the attacking enemy with decisive and continuous blows. But as soon as certainty is achieved, humanitarian goals require that they stop killing and suffice with taking prisoners.

These two important "military" and "humanitarian" points are expressed in the above verse in a short phrase.

Then, He reproaches the group that acted contrary to this instruction, saying: "You only think of material aspects and desire the fleeting goods of this world, while Allah desires for you the eternal abode and everlasting happiness" (تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ الدُّنْيَا وَاللَّهُ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ).

"Arad" (عَرَضَ) means "fleeting matters," and since the material wealth of this world does not last, it is called "عَرَضَ الدُّنْيَا" (the fleeting goods of the world).

Of course, as we mentioned, focusing on the material aspects of prisoners of war and neglecting the ultimate goals—namely, victory over the enemy—not only harms the happiness and reward of the Hereafter but is also detrimental to the life of this world, its honor, dignity, and peace. In truth, these ultimate goals are considered among the lasting matters of this world. In other words, one should not jeopardize continuous future interests for the sake of immediate and transient gains!

At the end of the verse, He states that the above instruction is actually a blend of "honor and victory" and "wisdom and planning," because it is issued by Allah, "and Allah is Almighty, All-Wise" (وَاللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

In the next verse, He once again reproaches and rebukes those who jeopardized important social interests to secure transient material gains, saying: "Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah, a great punishment would have afflicted you for what you took" (لَوْلَا كِتَابٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيمَا أَخَذْتُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ). Regarding the phrase "لَوْلَا كِتَابٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ سَبَقَ" (Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah), commentators have offered various possibilities.

However, what is most consistent with the interpretation of the verse as a whole is: "If it were not for the fact that Allah had previously decreed that He does not punish His servants until He has explained a ruling to them through His Prophet, He would have severely punished you for pursuing prisoners to gain material benefits and jeopardizing the position of the Islamic army and its final victory. But as explicitly stated in other verses of the Quran, the Sunnah (tradition) of the Lord is to first clarify the rulings and then punish the violators," such as: "وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا" (And We never punish until We have sent a messenger) [Surah Al-Isra, Verse 15].

Here, several points should be noted

  1. The apparent meaning of the above verses—as we said—is a discussion about taking prisoners of war, not the issue of taking "ransom" after the war. Thus, many of the difficulties encountered by some commentators in understanding the verse are automatically resolved.

Furthermore, the reproach and blame are directed at the group that engaged in taking prisoners for material goals before complete victory, and it has no connection to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or that group of believers who were pursuing the objective of Jihad.

Therefore, discussions such as whether the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله committed a sin here and how it reconciles with his status of Infallibility (Ismah) are all misplaced.

Similarly, the hadiths mentioned in some Sunni books [1] regarding the interpretation of the verse—which claim the verse relates to the Prophet and Muslims taking ransom for prisoners after the Battle of Badr and before Allah's permission, and that the only person who opposed ransom and favored killing the prisoners was "Umar" or Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, and that the Prophet said about him: "If a punishment were to descend from Allah, no one among us would be saved except Umar or Sa'd ibn Mu'adh!"—all such discussions are baseless. These types of narrations are entirely foreign to the interpretation of the verse, especially since the signs of fabrication in these hadiths are quite evident, as they place the status of Umar or Sa'd ibn Mu'adh even above the status of the Prophet!!

  1. The above verses do not oppose taking "fida" (ransom) and releasing prisoners of war if the interest of the Islamic society requires it. Rather, they say that the Mujahideen should not resort to taking prisoners for this purpose. Therefore, it is consistent in every respect with Verse 4 of Surah Muhammad, which says: "فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقَابِ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثَاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَاءً":

"So when you meet those who disbelieve (and enemies who do not recognize your right to life) in the battlefield, strike their necks until you have overcome them; then at that point, do not kill them anymore but bind them and take them captive. Thereafter, either release them as a favor without taking ransom or by taking ransom."

However, one point must be noted here: whenever there are dangerous individuals among the prisoners of war whose release would lead to the reigniting of the flames of war and jeopardize victory, Muslims have the right to eliminate such dangerous individuals. The reason for this is hidden within the verse itself and in the terms "يُثْخِنَ" and "أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ".

For this reason, it is mentioned in some narrations that the Prophet ordered two of the prisoners of the Battle of Badr, named "Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt" and "Nadr ibn al-Harith," to be executed and did not accept any ransom from them. (Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 135).

  1. In the above verses, there is once again an emphasis on the issue of human free will and a rejection of the school of determinism (Jabr), as it says Allah desires for you the eternal abode while a group of you are bound by transient material interests.

In the next verse, another ruling regarding prisoners of war is mentioned, which is the issue of taking "fida" (ransom). As we read in some narrations regarding the "circumstances of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) of the verses under discussion: After the end of the Battle of Badr and the taking of prisoners, and after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the execution of two dangerous prisoners, "Uqbah" and "Nadr," the Ansar became terrified that this order might be executed regarding the other prisoners (and they would be deprived of taking ransom). Therefore, they said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "We killed seventy and captured seventy, and these are from your tribe and are your captives; grant them to us so that we may take ransom in exchange for their freedom." (The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was waiting for the descent of divine revelation in this regard). The verses under discussion were revealed and granted permission to take ransom in exchange for the release of prisoners. [1] It is also narrated that the highest amount set for the release of prisoners was four thousand dirhams and the lowest was one thousand dirhams. When this news reached the Quraysh, they sent the ransom amounts one after another to free their prisoners.

Surprisingly, the Prophet's son-in-law, "Abu al-As," was also among the prisoners. The Prophet's daughter, Zaynab, the wife of Abu al-As, sent a necklace that "Khadijah" علیها السلام had given her at her wedding as ransom to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When the Prophet's eyes fell upon the necklace, the memory of Khadijah, that self-sacrificing and mujahid woman, was visualized before him. He said: "May Allah have mercy on Khadijah; this is the necklace she made part of my daughter Zaynab's dowry." (According to some other narrations, out of respect for Khadijah, he refrained from accepting the necklace and sought the consent of the Muslims in this matter to observe their rights). [1] Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله released Abu al-As on the condition that he send his daughter Zaynab (whom he had married before Islam) to Medina to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. He accepted this condition and later fulfilled it. [2] In any case, the above verse allows Muslims to use this war booty (i.e., the amount they received for the release of prisoners) and says:

"So consume of what you have taken as spoils, [it being] lawful and good" (فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا).

This phrase may have a broad meaning and, in addition to the subject of "ransom," include other spoils as well.

Then He commands them: "And fear Allah and avoid disobeying His command" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ).

This indicates that the permissibility of such spoils should not cause the Mujahideen's goal in the field of Jihad to be the collection of booty or taking prisoners for the sake of "ransom."

And if they had such ugly intentions in their hearts in the past, they should cast them out. Regarding the past, He promises pardon and forgiveness, saying: "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Is Taking Ransom Logical and Just?

A question arises here: how does taking an amount in exchange for releasing prisoners align with the principles of justice? And is this not a form of human trafficking?

With a little reflection, the answer to this question becomes clear: "fida" (ransom) is, in fact, a type of war reparation. In every war, a large amount of economic capital and human resources are lost. The group fighting for the truth has the right to demand compensation for their losses from the enemy after the war ends. One way of obtaining compensation is the issue of "ransom." Considering that the ransom amount at that time was set at four thousand dirhams for wealthy prisoners and one thousand dirhams for those with less wealth, it becomes clear that the total wealth taken from the Quraysh in this way was not very significant; it did not even cover the financial and human losses inflicted upon the army of Islam.

Furthermore, a great deal of the Muslims' property remained in the hands of the enemy in Mecca when they were forced to migrate to Medina due to the pressure of the Quraysh. From this perspective as well, the Muslims had the right to compensate for it.

It is also necessary to note that taking "ransom" is not mandatory, and the Islamic government can, if it sees fit, exchange prisoners of war or release them without taking any advantage, as mentioned in Verse 4 of Surah Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, the interpretation of which will come, Allah willing.

Another important issue regarding prisoners of war is the matter of their reform, education, and guidance. This subject might not be raised in materialist schools, but in a Jihad conducted for the liberation and reform of humans and the generalization of truth and justice, it is certainly raised.

Therefore, in the fourth verse under discussion, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to invite and encourage the prisoners toward faith and the reform of their conduct with heartening words, saying: "O Prophet, say to those who are in your hands of the captives, 'If Allah knows [any] good in your hearts, He will give you [something] better than what was taken from you'" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ مِنَ الْأَسْرَىٰ إِنْ يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْرًا يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْرًا مِمَّا أُخِذَ مِنْكُمْ).

The meaning of the word "khayr" (good) in the phrase "إِنْ يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْرًا" is faith and the acceptance of Islam, and the meaning of "khayr" in the subsequent phrase refers to the material and spiritual rewards that will come to them under the shadow of Islam and faith, which are far superior to the amount they paid as "ransom."

In addition to these rewards, He has shown you another favor: "and He will forgive you [the sins you committed in the past and before accepting Islam], and Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Since it was possible that some prisoners might abuse this program and join the ranks of the Muslims by professing Islam with the intention of betrayal and seeking revenge, in the next verse, He both warns them and alerts the Muslims, saying:

"But if they intend to betray you—they have already betrayed Allah before" (وَإِنْ يُرِيدُوا خِيَانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خَانُوا اللَّهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ).

What betrayal is greater than ignoring the call of nature (fitrah), casting aside the judgment of reason, attributing partners and likenesses to God, and replacing the superstitious creed of idolatry with monotheism (Tawhid)?

But they must not forget that "Allah gave [you and your companions] power over them" (فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ).

In the future, too, if they follow the path of betrayal, they will not achieve victory; they will again be afflicted with defeat.

Allah is aware of their intentions, and the instructions He has given regarding the prisoners are according to wisdom, for "Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

In the commentaries of both the Shia and the Sunnis, it is narrated under the two verses above that a group of the Ansar asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for permission not to take ransom from "Abbas," the Prophet's uncle—who was among the prisoners of Badr—out of respect for him. But the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "وَاللَّهِ لَا تَذَرُونَ مِنْهُ دِرْهَمًا" meaning, "By Allah, do not waive even a single dirham of it." (If taking ransom is Allah's law, it must be implemented for everyone, even my uncle, and there should be no difference between him and others).

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Abbas: "You must pay ransom for yourself and your nephew (Aqeel ibn Abi Talib)." Abbas (who had a particular fondness for wealth) said: "O Muhammad! Do you want to make me so poor that I stretch out my hand before the tribe of Quraysh?!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Pay the ransom from that same treasure you left with your wife Umm al-Fadl, saying: 'If I am killed in the battlefield, let this wealth be for your expenses and those of your children!'" Abbas was deeply astonished by this and said: "Who gave you this news?" (while it had been completely confidential). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Gabriel, from Allah."

Abbas said: "I swear by the One whom Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله swears by, no one knew of this secret except me and my wife." Then he said: "أَشْهَدُ أَنَّكَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ" (I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah) and became a Muslim.

All the prisoners of Badr returned to Mecca after their release, except "Abbas," "Aqeel," and "Nawfal," who accepted Islam and stayed in Medina, and the above verses refer to their situation. [1] Regarding Abbas's conversion to Islam, it is mentioned in some histories that after accepting Islam, he returned to Mecca and would inform the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of the polytheists' conspiracies via letters, then migrated to Medina before the eighth year of the Hijra, the year of the Conquest of Mecca.

In the book "Qurb al-Isnad," it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام from his father Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام that one day a significant amount of wealth was brought to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned to Abbas and said: "Open your cloak and take a portion of this wealth." Abbas did so. Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "This is from what Allah has said," and he recited the verse: "يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ..." [2] indicating that Allah's promise regarding the compensation of the wealth taken from you has now been fulfilled.

From this hadith, it is clear that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله intended to encourage the prisoners who accepted Islam in the best possible way and to compensate for the wealth they had paid in a better manner.

[1] Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Rawdat al-Kafi, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, and Tafsir al-Manar under the verse in question. [2] Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 168.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ مَا لَكُم مِّن وَلَـٰيَتِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنِ ٱسْتَنصَرُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٧٢)
Indeed those who have believed and migrated and waged jihad with their possessions and persons in the way of Allah, and those who gave [them] shelter and help—they are heirs of one another. As for those who have believed but did not migrate, you have no heirdom in relation to them whatsoever until they migrate. Yet if they ask your help for the sake of religion, it is incumbent on you to help them, excepting against a people with whom you have a treaty; and Allah watches what you do. (72)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتْنَةٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَفَسَادٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧٣)
As for the faithless, they are heirs of one another. Unless you do the same, there will be strife in the land and great corruption. (73)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٧٤)

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ مَعَكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۢ (٧٥)
Those who believed afterwards and migrated, and waged jihad along with you, they belong to you; but the blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (75)

Commentary

Four Different Groups

These verses, which form the final section of Surah Al-Anfal, discuss the "Migrants" (Muhajirin), the "Helpers" (Ansar), and other groups of Muslims, highlighting the existential value of each. In doing so, they complete the previous discussions regarding Jihad and the mujahideen.

In other words, these verses outline the system of Islamic society in terms of various social bonds, because programs of "war" and "peace"—like any other public program—are impossible without considering a sound social connection.

In these verses, five groups are mentioned, four of which are Muslims and one of which is non-Muslim. The four groups are:

  1. The early Migrants (Muhajirin).
  2. The Helpers (Ansar) of Medina.
  3. Those who believed but did not migrate.
  4. Those who believed later and joined the Migrants.

In the first verse under discussion, it says: "Indeed, those who have believed and emigrated and fought with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah and those who gave shelter and aided - these are allies (awliya) of one another" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ هاجَرُوا وَ جاهَدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ الَّذِينَ آوَوْا وَ نَصَرُوا أُولئِكَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ).

In this part of the verse, reference is made to the "first" and "second" groups: namely, the believers who embraced faith in Mecca and then migrated to Medina, and the believers in Medina who believed in the Prophet and rose to aid and support him and the Migrants. It introduces them as allies, supporters, and those committed to one another.

It is noteworthy that for the first group, four attributes are mentioned: first, faith; second, migration; third, financial and economic Jihad (by leaving behind their wealth in Mecca or spending their property in the Battle of Badr and the like); and fourth, Jihad with their own blood and lives in the way of Allah. Regarding the "Ansar," two attributes are mentioned: first, "giving shelter" (iwa'), and second, "aiding." By stating the phrase "some of them are allies of others," He considers them all committed and responsible toward one another.

In reality, these two groups were like the "warp" and "weft" in the fabric of Islamic society; neither could do without the other.

Then, referring to the third group, it says: "And those who believed and did not emigrate—for you there is no guardianship (wilayah), commitment, or responsibility toward them until they emigrate" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ لَمْ يُهاجِرُوا ما لَكُمْ مِنْ وَلايَتِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَتَّى يُهاجِرُوا). In the following sentence, it excludes only one type of support and responsibility, affirming it for this group: "But if they seek help from you in the matter of religion (i.e., if they are under severe pressure from enemies), it is your duty to help them" (وَ إِنِ اسْتَنْصَرُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ النَّصْرُ).

"Except against a people with whom you have a treaty of non-aggression" (إِلَّا عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ). In other words, the necessity of defending them applies when they face common enemies; however, if they are pitted against disbelievers with whom you have a treaty, honoring the treaty is more necessary than defending this passive group!

At the end of the verse, to ensure these responsibilities are respected and the regulations are followed precisely, it says: "And Allah is Seeing of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ). He sees all your actions and is aware of your efforts, struggles, and sense of responsibility, as well as any indifference, weakness, laziness, or lack of responsibility toward these great duties!

In the second verse, it points to the opposite of the Islamic society—the society of disbelief and the enemies of Islam—stating: "And those who disbelieved are allies of one another" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ). This means their bond is only among themselves, and you have no right to have a bond with them, support them, or invite them to support you. You should neither give them shelter nor seek shelter from them. In short, they must not be part of the fabric of Islamic society, nor should you be part of theirs.

Then, it warns the Muslims: "If you do not do so (i.e., follow this important Islamic instruction), there will be fitnah (discord/trial) on earth and great corruption" (إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُنْ فِتْنَةٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ فسادٌ كَبِيرٌ). What fitnah or corruption could be greater than the blurring of your lines of victory, the effectiveness of enemy conspiracies within your society, and the revival of their sinister plans to overthrow the religion of truth and justice?

In the next verse, it once again emphasizes the importance of the status of the Migrants and the Helpers, and their influence in advancing the goals of Islamic society, praising them as follows: "Those who have believed and emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah and those who gave shelter and aided - it is they who are the believers in truth" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ هاجَرُوا وَ جاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ الَّذِينَ آوَوْا وَ نَصَرُوا أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا). This is because during the hard and difficult days, and the times of Islam's loneliness and isolation, each of them rushed in their own way to aid the religion of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

And "for them is forgiveness and a noble provision" (لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ). They will enjoy great blessings in the presence of Allah and the hereafter, and they will also have a worthy share of greatness, honor, victory, security, and peace in this world.

In the final verse, it refers to the fourth group of Muslims, the "later Migrants," saying: "And those who believed after [this] and emigrated and fought with you - they are of you" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَ هاجَرُوا وَ جاهَدُوا مَعَكُمْ فَأُولئِكَ مِنْكُمْ). This means that Islamic society is not a closed or exclusive society; rather, its doors are open to all future believers, migrants, and mujahideen. Although the early Migrants hold a special rank and status, this superiority does not mean that future believers and migrants—who joined and came toward Islam during its time of influence and progress—are not part of the fabric of Islamic society.

At the end of the verse, it refers to the guardianship and priority of relatives toward one another, saying: "And blood relatives are more entitled to [inheritance from] one another in the decree of Allah" (وَ أُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى‌ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ).

In fact, the previous verses spoke of the general guardianship and priority of Muslims toward one another, and this final verse emphasizes that this guardianship and priority is stronger and more comprehensive in the case of relatives. This is because a Muslim relative possesses the bond of kinship in addition to the bonds of faith and migration.

For this reason, they inherit from one another, whereas non-relatives do not.

Therefore, this final verse does not only state the rule of inheritance; rather, it has a broad meaning of which inheritance is a part. If we observe that this verse and the similar verse in Surah Al-Ahzab are used as evidence in Islamic narrations and all books of jurisprudence regarding inheritance laws, it does not mean it is limited to the issue of inheritance. Instead, it can clarify a general law of which inheritance is one section.

Hence, we see that in the matter of the succession to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—which does not fall under the concept of financial inheritance—this verse has been cited in some Islamic narrations. Similarly, it has been used to establish the priority of relatives in matters such as washing the deceased (ghusl al-mayyit) and the like.

Given what has been said above, it becomes clear that the insistence of some commentators that this final verse exclusively addresses inheritance is groundless. If we were to choose such an interpretation, the only way would be to consider it an exception to the "absolute guardianship" mentioned in the previous verses for the general Migrants and Helpers, saying that the general guardianship of Muslims toward one another does not include inheritance.

As for the possibility that the previous verses included inheritance and were then abrogated by this final verse, it seems very unlikely. The conceptual connection and spiritual continuity of these verses, and even their linguistic similarity, indicate that they were revealed together; thus, they cannot be abrogation (nasikh) and abrogated (mansukh).

In any case, the most appropriate interpretation consistent with the concept of the verses is what we stated at the beginning.

In the final sentence of this verse, which is the last sentence of Surah Al-Anfal, He says: "Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ). All the laws He revealed in this Surah regarding "spoils" (anfal), "war booty," the "system of Jihad," "peace," "rules for prisoners of war," and matters related to "migration" and the like, were all stated based on a precise plan and calculation that perfectly aligns with the spirit of human society, human emotions, and their comprehensive interests.

Key Points

1- "Migration and Jihad"

A study of Islamic history shows that these two subjects were the two fundamental factors in the victory of Islam against powerful enemies. If not for migration (Hijrah), Islam would have perished in the suffocating environment of Mecca, and if not for Jihad, Islam would never have grown and flourished.

Migration took Islam out of its regional form and placed it in a global form, and Jihad taught Muslims that if they do not rely on power, enemies who do not adhere to logic or reason will grant them no rights!

Even now, to save Islam from deadlocks and to break the various barriers that enemies have created from every side, there is no way but to revive the principles of "Migration" and "Jihad." Migration carries their voice to the ears of the world and places at their disposal ready hearts, constructive forces, and nations thirsty for truth and justice. Jihad gives them movement and life and removes from their path stubborn opponents who listen to nothing but the logic of force.

Several migrations occurred in Islam:

The migration of the Muslims of Mecca to "Abyssinia," which both sowed the seeds of Islam outside the Arabian Peninsula and served as a stronghold for the few early Muslims against intense enemy pressure.

And the migration of the Prophet and the early Muslims to Medina. These migrants, sometimes referred to as the "Migrants of Badr," hold extraordinary importance in Islamic history because they seemingly moved toward a completely dark future and, in reality, gave up all their material assets for the sake of God.

These migrants, referred to as "The First Migrants" (Al-Muhajirun al-Awwalun), actually formed the foundation stone of the magnificent palace of Islam, and the Quran speaks of them with special greatness because they were considered among the most self-sacrificing Muslims.

The "Second Migration" refers to the migration of a group of Muslims after the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and the attainment of a relatively secure environment following this peace. Sometimes this title is applied to all those who migrated to Medina from the time of the Battle of Badr until the Conquest of Mecca.

After the Conquest of Mecca, migration in its previous form—moving from Mecca to Medina—ceased, as Mecca became an Islamic city. The hadith "There is no migration after the Conquest," narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, refers to this very subject.

However, this does not mean that the issue of "Migration" in Islam was completely abolished—as some have imagined. Rather, the issue of migrating from Mecca to Medina was abolished; otherwise, whenever conditions similar to those of the early Muslims arise, the law of migration remains in full force for them and will continue until the day Islam encompasses the entire world.

Unfortunately, today, due to forgetting this important Islamic principle, Muslims have mostly retreated into their shells, while Christian missionaries and the propagators of deviant sects and colonial religions migrate to the East, West, North, and South of the world—even among wild and cannibalistic tribes and even to the Arctic and Antarctic regions. In reality, this command belongs to the Muslims, but the action is performed by others!

Even more surprising is that around large Islamic cities, there are many villages, sometimes only a few leagues away, that are unaware of Islamic issues and sometimes have never even seen an Islamic preacher. Therefore, their environment is ripe for the growth of germs of corruption and fabricated colonial religions. It is not clear what answer today's Muslims, who are the heirs of the first Migrants, have prepared for God regarding this situation?! Although a movement in this regard has been seen recently, it is by no means sufficient.

In any case, the subject of migration and its role in the history and destiny of Muslims is too important to examine all its aspects in this brief space. (We also had discussions on this in Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 89 onwards, and God willing, we will speak again under appropriate verses).

2- Exaggeration in the Sanctification of the Companions

A group of our Sunni brothers has sought to use the respect and importance the Quran accords to the "First Migrants" to conclude that they never committed any wrongdoing until the end of their lives, and that we must respect all of them without exception and without question. They then extended this subject to all "Companions" (Sahaba) because of the praise the Quran gave them during the Pledge of Ridwan and elsewhere. In practice, they have considered the Sahaba as exceptional human beings regardless of their actions, depriving themselves of any right to critique or examine their deeds.

Among them, the famous commentator, the author of Al-Manar, under the verses in question, launched a harsh attack on the Shi'a, asking why they point fingers at and criticize some of the First Migrants?!! However, they fail to notice how such a belief regarding the Sahaba contradicts the spirit of Islam and its history.

There is no doubt that the "Sahaba," especially the early Migrants, hold a special respect, but this respect lasted as long as they walked the correct path and showed self-sacrifice. From the day a group of the Sahaba deviated from the true path of Islam, the Quran's judgment regarding them would certainly be different.

For example, how can we exonerate "Talha" and "Zubayr" for breaking their pledge and opposing a leader who, besides the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) explicit designation, was chosen by the general Muslims and even by themselves? How can we wash their hands of the blood of seventeen thousand Muslims shed on the battlefield of the Camel? Anyone who spills the blood of one innocent person will have no excuse before God, whoever they may be, let alone this large number. Fundamentally, is it possible to hold both "Ali عليه السلام and his companions" to be in the right on the battlefield of the Camel, and also "Talha and Zubayr" and some other Sahaba who joined them?

Does any logic or reason accept this clear contradiction? Can we, under the title of "sanctifying the Sahaba," close our eyes and consider them a "separate breed," consigning the entire history of Islam after the Prophet to oblivion and trampling the Islamic criterion "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you" (إِنَّ أَكْرَمُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ أَتْقاكُمْ)? What kind of illogical judgment is this?

Fundamentally, what prevents a person or persons from being in the ranks of the people of Paradise and the supporters of truth one day, and in the ranks of the people of Hell and the enemies of truth another day?

Is everyone infallible? Have we not seen so many changes in the states of individuals with our own eyes?

The story of the "People of Apostasy" (Ashab al-Riddah)—the apostasy of a group of Muslims after the Prophet's passing—is narrated by everyone, both Shi'a and Sunni, in their books; the first Caliph rose to fight them and put them in their place. Did none of the "People of Apostasy" see the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or stand in the ranks of the Sahaba?!

More surprisingly, to escape this contradiction and strange deadlock, some have resorted to the issue of "Ijtihad," saying that individuals like "Talha," "Zubayr," "Mu'awiyah," and their colleagues were mujtahids and made a mistake, but no sin was committed by them; rather, they will receive a reward from God for these very actions!! Truly, what a scandalous logic! Is rising against the successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, breaking a covenant, and spilling the blood of thousands of innocents—and that for the sake of ambition and attaining wealth and status—such a complex and unknown matter that no one would be aware of its ugliness? Does spilling all that innocent blood have a reward in the presence of God?! If we wish to exonerate a group of Sahaba who committed crimes in this way, then surely no sinner will exist in the world, and with this logic, we would exonerate all murderers, criminals, and tyrants.

Such indiscriminate defenses of the Sahaba cause pessimism toward the essence of Islam.

Therefore, we have no choice but to hold respect for everyone, especially the Sahaba of the Prophet, but only as long as they have not deviated from the path of truth, justice, and the programs of Islam!

3- Inheritance in the System of Islamic Laws

As we pointed out in the commentary of Surah An-Nisa, during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), inheritance among the Arabs was through one of three ways: through "lineage" (lineage to them was limited only to male children, and children and women were deprived of inheritance); through "adoption" (tabanni); and through "covenant and treaty," which they referred to as "wala'." [1] At the beginning of Islam, when the law of inheritance had not yet been revealed, this same method was followed. However, soon the issue of "Islamic brotherhood" took its place, and only the Migrants and Helpers who had made a pact of brotherhood with one another inherited from each other. After some time, as Islam expanded further, inheritance was transferred to blood and marital relatives, and the rule of Islamic brotherhood regarding inheritance was abrogated. The final law of inheritance was revealed, as referred to in the above verses and verse 6 of Surah Al-Ahzab, where it says: (وَ أُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى‌ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ).

All of this is historically certain, but as we said, the phrase "and blood relatives" (wa ulu al-arham) mentioned in the verses under discussion is not exclusive to the issue of inheritance; rather, it conveys a broad meaning of which inheritance is a part. [2]

4- What is meant by "Fitnah" and "Great Corruption"?

Commentators have offered various possibilities in interpreting these two words mentioned in the verses under discussion. However, what is most consistent with the concept of the verse is that "fitnah" refers to discord, division, and the shaking of the foundations of the Muslims' beliefs due to the whispers of enemies, and "corruption" (fasad) includes any kind of disorder and destruction of various social systems, especially the shedding of innocent blood, insecurity, and the like.

[1]: We discussed inheritance through wala' in detail in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 367.

[2]: Detailed information regarding inheritance was provided in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 285 onwards.

In reality, the Holy Quran warns Muslims that if they do not strengthen the bonds of connection, cooperation, and brotherhood among themselves, and if they do not cut ties and cooperation with enemies, day by day, discord and division in their ranks will increase. With the infiltration of enemies into the Islamic community and their deceptive whispers, the foundations of faith will become weak and shaky, and through this, a great fitnah will afflict them.

Likewise, due to the lack of a strong social bond and the enemy's infiltration into their ranks, all kinds of corruption, insecurity, bloodshed, destruction of property and children, and disorders will appear in their society, and "great corruption" will spread everywhere.

O Lord! Awaken our Islamic society by Your grace. Make us aware of the dangers of bonding and cooperating with enemies.

And in the light of self-awareness and unity of word, purify our community from "fitnah" and "corruption"!

"End of the Commentary of Surah Al-Anfal"
